The organizational process without the technical implementation also fails — an ownership policy with no enforcement mechanism is just a document. Senior-level platform architecture designs both together.

HydroAlpes' mid-level platform ran on a single PostgreSQL instance. The billing pipeline, the analytical dashboards, the ML feature engineering, and the data quality framework all queried the same database. At mid-level scale (1.2 million meters, 3 teams), PostgreSQL handled everything — the instance had enough CPU, memory, and I/O to serve both the transactional writes from the pipeline and the analytical reads from the dashboards. At AlpineEnergy scale (5 million meters, 10 teams), the workloads collide: the nightly billing pipeline's large hash joins consume work_mem that the concurrent dashboard queries need, and a 3-hour analytical scan on the historical archive blocks the autovacuum that the transactional tables require. The single-engine architecture forces competing workloads to share resources without isolation.
The Dual-Engine Topology

AlpineEnergy operates two SQL engines with distinct roles. PostgreSQL serves the operational workload: the source of truth for meter metadata, the billing pipeline, the SCD Type 2 dimension processing, and the low-latency API-backing queries that serve the customer portal. Spark SQL serves the analytical workload: the large-scale transformations that produce Gold and Platinum tables, the ML feature engineering that reads terabytes of historical data, and the ad-hoc analytical queries that the three analytics teams run. Data flows from PostgreSQL to Spark SQL via a nightly CDC pipeline (Module 9) that replicates Silver tables to Delta Lake. The reverse flow — Spark SQL results written back to PostgreSQL — occurs for materialized summaries that the customer portal needs.
The Engine Selection Decision Matrix

rows: Sub-second point lookups, < 5s for indexed queries
If the consumer needs < 1s response, PostgreSQL
Efficient up to ~100M rows per query
Efficient at 100M+ rows, designed for terabyte scans
If the query scans > 100M rows, Spark SQL
Hundreds of concurrent connections, each lightweight
Tens of concurrent queries, each heavyweight
ACID via Delta Lake, but no cross-table transactions
If multi-table atomicity is required, PostgreSQL
If row-level DML with foreign keys, PostgreSQL
If analytics reads 5 of 50 columns, Spark SQL

blocks.content: The matrix is a decision framework, not a prescription. Some workloads sit at the boundary: a 50-million-row aggregation query with a 3-second latency target could run on either engine. The tiebreaker is operational: where does the data already reside, what engine does the team know, and what is the marginal cost of adding a cross-engine data flow? At AlpineEnergy, the billing summary runs on PostgreSQL (the source data is already there, the query is indexed, and the result serves the customer portal). The capacity forecast runs on Spark SQL (it scans 3 years of history across all regions, and the columnar format makes the scan 8x faster than the equivalent PostgreSQL query).
When NOT to Use Dual-Engine

Dual-engine architecture introduces operational complexity: two sets of credentials, two monitoring systems, two upgrade cycles, two sets of expertise, and a CDC pipeline that must be maintained between them. If the total data volume is under 500 GB, the team count is under 5, and no workload requires terabyte-scale scans, a single PostgreSQL instance with read replicas handles everything at lower operational cost. AlpineEnergy's Italian subsidiary ran on a single PostgreSQL instance for 6 months after the expansion before the analytical workload justified adding Spark SQL. The decision to add a second engine should be driven by a measured workload conflict — not by the assumption that bigger platforms need more engines.

blocks.title: Engine proliferation is an anti-pattern
blocks.content: Two engines is an architectural decision. Three engines is a red flag. AlpineEnergy evaluated adding DuckDB for notebook analytics, Clickhouse for real-time dashboards, and BigQuery for the Italian subsidiary's legacy workloads. Each addition would have solved a narrow problem while multiplying operational complexity: more CDC pipelines, more schema synchronization, more failure modes. The platform team's policy: two engines maximum, justified by workload measurement, with exceptions requiring quarterly governance review.

When HydroAlpes had two schemas (silver and gold), every engineer knew where every table lived. At AlpineEnergy with 300+ tables across two engines, three countries, and four data layers, discoverability is the first architectural casualty. A new engineer on the Italian team cannot find the Swiss billing summary table. The Austrian analytics team does not know whether the regional consumption data they need is in PostgreSQL or Spark SQL. The ML team discovers three tables named meter_features in different schemas with different definitions. Namespace design — the hierarchical naming convention that organizes tables by country, layer, and domain — solves the discoverability problem by making the table's location, ownership, and purpose inferrable from its fully qualified name.
The AlpineEnergy Namespace Hierarchy

blocks.title: AlpineEnergy namespace hierarchy across engines
blocks.content:
PostgreSQL                                  Spark SQL (Delta Lake)
─────────────────────────                   ─────────────────────────
alpineenergy_ch.silver.meter_readings       alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings
alpineenergy_ch.gold.regional_consumption   alpine_ch.gold.regional_consumption
alpineenergy_ch.gold.billing_summary        alpine_ch.platinum.cross_country_demand
alpineenergy_at.silver.meter_readings       alpine_at.silver.meter_readings
alpineenergy_at.gold.regulatory_report      alpine_at.gold.regulatory_report
alpineenergy_it.silver.meter_readings       alpine_it.silver.meter_readings
alpineenergy_it.gold.grid_operations        alpine_it.gold.grid_operations
                                            alpine_shared.platinum.enterprise_dashboard

Pattern: {org}_{country}.{layer}.{table_name}
blocks.caption: Each country has its own database/catalog. Each layer (bronze, silver, gold, platinum) is a schema. Tables within a schema follow domain-specific naming. The Spark SQL namespace mirrors the PostgreSQL namespace, enabling engineers to locate the same logical table in either engine by substituting the engine prefix.

blocks.content: The hierarchy encodes three decisions in the fully qualified name. The database/catalog prefix (alpineenergy_ch, alpine_at) encodes the country — enabling country-level isolation for data residency compliance. The schema (silver, gold, platinum) encodes the data layer — making it immediately clear whether a table contains raw validated data, business-aggregated data, or cross-domain curated data. The table name encodes the domain (meter_readings, billing_summary, grid_operations). A new engineer who sees alpineenergy_ch.gold.billing_summary knows instantly: this is Swiss data, at the Gold business layer, owned by the billing domain. No catalog search required.
Cross-Engine Namespace Alignment

The namespace alignment between engines means that any cross-engine reference follows a predictable pattern: replace the engine-specific prefix. The CDC pipeline that replicates from PostgreSQL to Spark SQL uses this alignment for automatic table mapping — no manual configuration per table. The lineage system (Module 10) traces data flows across engines using the shared namespace as the join key. When the Italian team asks 'where is the Austrian meter readings data?' the answer is the same in both engines: the {at} database, the silver schema, the meter_readings table.

blocks.title: Namespaces are the platform's API
blocks.content: The namespace hierarchy is the interface between the platform team (which manages the infrastructure) and the domain teams (which produce and consume data). A well-designed namespace is self-documenting: the name tells you the country, the layer, and the domain without consulting any external documentation. At AlpineEnergy, the namespace convention is enforced by CI validation: a CREATE TABLE statement that does not follow the {org}_{country}.{layer}.{table} pattern is rejected by the schema registry's PR hook.

At mid-level, table ownership was implicit: Lena's team built the tables, so Lena's team owned them. At AlpineEnergy, 10 teams produce and consume 300+ tables across two engines. Without explicit ownership, accountability vanishes. When the regional_consumption table has stale data, who investigates? When the meter_readings schema needs a column addition, who reviews the impact analysis? When the billing_summary query slows down, who is paged at 04:00? The answer to all three questions must be one team — not 'whoever last touched it' or 'the platform team handles everything.' Table ownership assigns every table to exactly one producing team that is accountable for its schema, freshness, quality, and availability.
The Ownership Model

-- AlpineEnergy's ownership registry: a PostgreSQL table in the platform schema
CREATE TABLE platform.table_ownership (
    table_catalog   VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,  -- e.g., 'alpineenergy_ch'
    table_schema    VARCHAR(30)  NOT NULL,  -- e.g., 'gold'
    table_name      VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,  -- e.g., 'billing_summary'
    engine          VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,  -- 'postgresql' or 'sparksql'
    owning_team     VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,  -- e.g., 'swiss-billing'
    tech_contact    VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,  -- e.g., 'lena.hartmann@alpineenergy.ch'
    oncall_channel  VARCHAR(50),            -- e.g., '#swiss-billing-oncall'
    sla_tier        VARCHAR(10)  NOT NULL CHECK (sla_tier IN ('platinum', 'gold', 'silver')),
    registered_at   TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    last_reviewed    DATE,
    PRIMARY KEY (table_catalog, table_schema, table_name, engine)
);

-- Enforce: no table may exist without an ownership record
-- The CI hook queries information_schema and compares against this registry
-- Unregistered tables are flagged for immediate ownership assignment

The registry records who owns each table, how to reach them, and what SLA tier the table carries. The sla_tier determines the incident response expectations: platinum tables (billing, regulatory) trigger PagerDuty alerts and require resolution within 1 hour; gold tables (dashboards, ML features) trigger Slack notifications and require resolution within 4 hours; silver tables (internal intermediates) require resolution within 24 hours. The CI hook that compares the catalog against the registry ensures that no table exists without an owner — preventing the orphaned tables that accumulate when teams decommission pipelines without cleaning up their outputs.
Producer-Consumer Contracts

Ownership defines who is accountable. Contracts define what they guarantee. The producer-consumer contract is a formal agreement: the producer guarantees the schema (column names, types, constraints), the freshness (data arrives by a specific time), and the quality (completeness, accuracy, and consistency metrics from the dq_metrics framework). The consumer agrees not to write to the table, not to depend on columns not listed in the contract, and to update their queries within the deprecation window when the schema changes. At AlpineEnergy, contracts are YAML files in the schema registry (Module 5) — but the concept is introduced here because ownership without contracts is accountability without definition.
The GRANT/REVOKE Pattern

Role-based access control enforces the producer-consumer contract at the database level: consumers physically cannot write to tables they do not own. This prevents the mid-level failure mode where an analytics team's notebook accidentally overwrites a Silver table that three production pipelines consume. At AlpineEnergy, the GRANT/REVOKE statements are generated automatically from the ownership registry and applied via migration scripts during the weekly deployment cycle. When a new consumer registers for a table, the platform tooling generates the appropriate GRANT statement and adds it to the next deployment batch.

blocks.title: Ownership without enforcement is documentation, not governance
blocks.content: A registry that records ownership but does not enforce it through GRANT/REVOKE is a spreadsheet — teams can ignore it without consequence. AlpineEnergy's governance model enforces ownership at three levels: the database (GRANT/REVOKE prevents unauthorized writes), the CI pipeline (the schema registry hook rejects PRs that create unregistered tables), and the operational process (the on-call rotation uses the ownership registry to route alerts). All three levels must be active. Remove any one and the ownership model degrades from enforced governance to suggested documentation.

The mid-level track used three layers: Bronze (raw ingestion), Silver (validated and conformed), and Gold (consumer-ready analytics). At AlpineEnergy, a fourth layer emerged from operational necessity. The commercial analytics team needed a cross-country consumption comparison that joined Swiss Gold tables with Austrian Gold tables. The regulatory team needed a unified compliance report that combined data from all three countries. The ML team needed a feature store that integrated meter readings, weather data, and grid topology from multiple Gold tables. Each of these requirements crosses domain boundaries — they consume from multiple Gold tables owned by different teams. Creating these cross-domain datasets in Gold would violate the ownership model: who owns a table that joins data from three teams across three countries?
The Platinum Layer

The Platinum layer contains curated, cross-domain datasets that combine data from multiple Gold tables. Unlike Gold (owned by domain teams), Platinum is owned by the analytics or platform teams that serve cross-cutting consumers. The Platinum table's contract explicitly lists its upstream Gold dependencies, making the cross-domain provenance visible. At AlpineEnergy, three Platinum tables exist: the enterprise consumption dashboard (combines all countries), the regulatory compliance dataset (combines all countries with regulatory-specific transformations), and the ML feature store (combines meter readings, weather, and grid topology). Each Platinum table has a declared owner, an SLA, and a refresh schedule that respects the SLAs of its upstream Gold dependencies.
Layer Contracts

rows: Raw data preserved exactly as received; append-only; no transformations
Validated, deduplicated, conformed to EEDA standard; referential integrity maintained
Gold layer pipelines, data quality framework
Business-aggregated, consumer-ready; schema, freshness, and quality per contract
Dashboards, APIs, Platinum layer pipelines, ML pipelines
Cross-domain curated; joins verified across upstream Gold sources; refresh respects upstream SLAs
Executive dashboards, regulatory filings, ML feature store

blocks.content: Each layer has a strict consumer policy: Bronze is consumed only by Silver pipelines. Silver is consumed only by Gold pipelines and the quality framework. Gold is consumed by dashboards, APIs, and Platinum pipelines. Platinum is consumed by cross-cutting consumers. Skipping layers — a dashboard reading directly from Silver, or a Platinum table reading from Bronze — is prohibited because it bypasses the quality gates and ownership contracts that each layer enforces. At AlpineEnergy, the CI schema registry hook validates that new table references respect the layer hierarchy: a Gold table cannot reference a Bronze table, and a Platinum table cannot reference a Silver table.
When NOT to Use Four Layers

Four layers add routing complexity and pipeline latency. Each additional layer introduces a refresh dependency: Platinum cannot refresh until Gold completes, Gold cannot refresh until Silver completes, Silver cannot refresh until Bronze completes. A four-layer pipeline has three refresh boundaries. If the total end-to-end SLA is tight (Gold must be ready by 06:00 and Bronze arrives at 04:00), the 2-hour window must accommodate three refresh steps — leaving approximately 40 minutes per step. If the organization has fewer than 5 teams and no cross-domain reporting requirements, three layers (Bronze-Silver-Gold) are sufficient. Add Platinum only when a measurable cross-domain consumer need exists — not as a preemptive architectural decision.

blocks.title: Layers are governance boundaries, not performance optimizations
blocks.content: The value of layers is not in the data transformation they perform — a two-layer pipeline could produce the same final output. The value is in the governance they enforce: each layer has an owner, a contract, and a quality guarantee. When the enterprise dashboard shows wrong numbers, the investigation follows the layers: is the Platinum refresh correct (analytics team)? Is the upstream Gold data correct (domain team)? Is the Silver data complete (DE team)? Each layer boundary is a checkpoint with a responsible team. Without layers, the investigation is 'something is wrong somewhere in the pipeline.'

A table with an ownership entry and a GRANT policy is governable. A data product is discoverable, self-describing, and contractual. The distinction matters at enterprise scale: AlpineEnergy has 300+ tables, and a new analyst on the Italian team should not need to browse 300 entries in the ownership registry to find the meter consumption data they need. A data product wraps a table (or a coordinated set of tables) with a manifest that declares its schema, SLA, quality guarantees, sample queries, and contact information — everything a consumer needs to evaluate and use the data without contacting the producing team.
The Data Product Manifest

# File: data-products/alpine_ch/gold/regional_consumption.yml
product:
  name: Swiss Regional Consumption
  description: Monthly energy consumption aggregated by region and customer type.
  domain: billing
  owner: swiss-billing
  contact: lena.hartmann@alpineenergy.ch
  oncall: '#swiss-billing-oncall'

tables:
  primary: alpineenergy_ch.gold.regional_consumption
  engine: postgresql
  spark_mirror: alpine_ch.gold.regional_consumption

schema:
  columns:
    - name: region
      type: VARCHAR(30)
      description: Sub-region identifier (e.g., BERN-NORTH)
    - name: customer_type
      type: VARCHAR(20)
      description: RESIDENTIAL, COMMERCIAL, or INDUSTRIAL
    - name: month
      type: DATE
      description: First day of the month
    - name: total_kw
      type: DOUBLE PRECISION
      description: Total consumption in kilowatts for the region-type-month
    - name: active_meters
      type: INTEGER
      description: Count of distinct meters that reported in the month

sla:
  tier: gold
  freshness: Daily refresh by 06:00 UTC
  completeness: '>= 99.5% of expected regions per month'
  retention: 3 years

sample_queries:
  - description: Monthly consumption by region
    sql: |
      SELECT region, month, SUM(total_kw) AS total
      FROM alpineenergy_ch.gold.regional_consumption
      WHERE month >= '2025-01-01'
      GROUP BY region, month;

The manifest is a YAML file stored in the schema registry repository alongside the table's migration script and contract tests. It serves three audiences: the consumer (who reads the description, schema, and sample queries to evaluate the product), the monitoring system (which reads the SLA section to configure freshness and completeness alerts), and the platform tooling (which reads the tables section to register cross-engine mappings and lineage). At AlpineEnergy, the manifest is the single source of truth for a data product's contract — the ownership registry, the monitoring configuration, and the documentation page are all generated from it.
The Discovery Catalog

Manifests are machine-readable but not browsable. The discovery catalog is a web interface generated from all manifests that lets consumers search for data products by domain, country, or keyword. At AlpineEnergy, the catalog is a static site built by a CI pipeline that reads every YAML manifest, generates a searchable page per product, and publishes it to an internal URL. The Italian analyst searching for 'meter consumption' finds the Swiss Regional Consumption product, reads the description and schema, sees the sample query, and can start querying immediately. The catalog replaces the informal discovery process (asking in Slack, browsing schema names, reading someone else's notebook) with a self-service interface that scales to 300+ tables without requiring human intermediaries.
When NOT to Use Data Products

The data product model adds manifest maintenance overhead: every schema change requires updating the manifest, every SLA change requires updating the monitoring configuration, and the discovery catalog must be rebuilt. For internal intermediate tables that have exactly one consumer (the next step in the same team's pipeline), a data product manifest is unnecessary overhead — ownership in the registry and a schema contract are sufficient. At AlpineEnergy, the data product model is reserved for tables consumed by 3+ teams or by external-facing systems (the customer portal, the regulatory filing API). Internal Silver tables and single-consumer Gold tables are governed by the simpler ownership + contract model from lesson 4.

blocks.title: Start with contracts, graduate to products
blocks.content: New tables start with an ownership entry and a schema contract. When a third team requests access, the table is promoted to a data product with a full manifest, sample queries, and a discovery catalog entry. This progressive formalization avoids the overhead of maintaining manifests for tables that nobody outside the producing team uses — while ensuring that widely-consumed tables have the discoverability and documentation that multi-team consumption demands.

When AlpineEnergy's Austrian analytics pipeline reads from the Swiss Silver meter_readings table, a dependency exists: the Austrian pipeline cannot produce correct results until the Swiss Silver load completes. At mid-level, this dependency was managed informally — Markus knew that Lena's pipeline finished by 05:30, so he scheduled his pipeline for 05:45. At 10-team scale, informal scheduling breaks: the Austrian team does not know the Swiss pipeline's schedule, the Swiss team does not know that the Austrian pipeline depends on their data, and when the Swiss pipeline runs 20 minutes late, the Austrian pipeline silently processes stale data. Cross-team dependencies require formal declaration, automated coordination, and failure propagation.
The Dependency Graph

-- The dependency registry: declares producer-consumer relationships
CREATE TABLE platform.pipeline_dependencies (
    consumer_pipeline   VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    consumer_team       VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    producer_table      VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,  -- fully qualified: catalog.schema.table
    producer_team       VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    producer_engine     VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,
    freshness_required  INTERVAL     NOT NULL,  -- e.g., INTERVAL '2 hours'
    registered_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (consumer_pipeline, producer_table)
);

-- Dependency-aware scheduling: check all upstream tables before starting
-- This query returns tables that are NOT fresh enough for the consumer
SELECT pd.producer_table,
       pd.freshness_required,
       dq.metric_value AS actual_freshness_minutes,
       pd.producer_team
FROM   platform.pipeline_dependencies AS pd
  LEFT JOIN monitoring.dq_metrics AS dq
    ON dq.table_name = split_part(pd.producer_table, '.', 3)
   AND dq.metric_name = 'data_freshness_minutes'
   AND dq.checked_at >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1 hour'
WHERE  pd.consumer_pipeline = 'austrian_analytics_refresh'
  AND  (dq.metric_value IS NULL
   OR   dq.metric_value > EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM pd.freshness_required) / 60);

The dependency registry declares that the Austrian analytics pipeline depends on the Swiss Silver meter_readings table with a freshness requirement of 2 hours. Before the Austrian pipeline starts, the scheduling system runs the freshness check query. If any upstream table is staler than the declared requirement, the pipeline waits and retries. If the upstream table is still stale after a configurable timeout (30 minutes at AlpineEnergy), the pipeline fails with a clear error: 'upstream dependency stale: alpineenergy_ch.silver.meter_readings (produced by swiss-de team, last refresh 4.5 hours ago).' The error message includes the producing team's identity so the on-call engineer knows who to contact.
Circular Dependency Detection

Circular dependencies (team A depends on team B, team B depends on team A) create deadlocks: neither pipeline can start because each is waiting for the other. At AlpineEnergy, circular dependencies are detected at registration time using a recursive CTE that traverses the dependency graph and checks for cycles — the same cycle-detection pattern from the mid-level recursive CTE lesson, applied to the pipeline dependency graph rather than the grid topology. The CI hook rejects dependency registrations that would create a cycle, requiring the teams to redesign their data flow. In one case, the cycle was resolved by having both teams consume from a shared Silver table rather than from each other's Gold tables.
Failure Propagation

When an upstream pipeline fails, the downstream impact must be communicated immediately. AlpineEnergy's failure propagation system queries the dependency graph to identify all consumer pipelines affected by a producer failure, sends notifications to each affected team's Slack channel, and optionally blocks downstream pipelines from starting (to prevent them from processing stale data and producing incorrect results). The propagation is recursive: if the Swiss Silver failure affects the Austrian Gold refresh, which in turn affects the Platinum enterprise dashboard, all three teams receive notifications with the root cause traced to the original failure. Without this propagation, the Austrian and Platinum teams would spend time diagnosing their own pipelines before discovering that the root cause was upstream.

blocks.title: Dependencies are a graph, not a list
blocks.content: A pipeline's direct dependencies are the tables it reads. Its transitive dependencies include everything those tables depend on. At AlpineEnergy, the Platinum enterprise dashboard has 3 direct dependencies (Gold tables from CH, AT, IT) and 14 transitive dependencies (Silver tables, Bronze ingestion pipelines, external source systems). The platform team maintains a visualization of the full dependency graph, updated nightly, that reveals the critical path for each SLA-bound table. The critical path — the longest dependency chain — determines the earliest possible completion time and is the primary input for SLA negotiation.

AlpineEnergy's data platform costs approximately EUR 35,000 per month: EUR 18,000 for the PostgreSQL instances (primary + replicas across 3 countries), EUR 12,000 for the Spark SQL cluster (compute + storage), and EUR 5,000 for supporting infrastructure (monitoring, CDC pipeline, schema registry). Without cost allocation, the entire cost is absorbed by the platform team's budget — which creates a perverse incentive: domain teams consume resources without consequence, and the platform team bears the cost of other teams' inefficient queries. Cost allocation assigns each team a share of the total cost based on their measured resource consumption, making teams accountable for their usage patterns.
PostgreSQL Cost Attribution

-- Attribute PostgreSQL costs per team using pg_stat_statements
-- Cost proxy: total execution time per role (team) as a fraction of total
WITH team_usage AS (
  SELECT rolname                       AS team_role,
         SUM(total_exec_time) / 1000.0 AS total_seconds,
         SUM(calls)                    AS total_calls,
         SUM(shared_blks_hit + shared_blks_read) AS total_blocks
  FROM   pg_stat_statements AS ss
    JOIN pg_roles AS r ON ss.userid = r.oid
  WHERE  r.rolname IN ('swiss_billing', 'austrian_analytics',
                       'italian_de', 'platform_team', 'ml_team')
  GROUP BY rolname
),
totals AS (
  SELECT SUM(total_seconds) AS grand_total_seconds
  FROM   team_usage
)
SELECT tu.team_role,
       ROUND(tu.total_seconds)          AS exec_seconds,
       tu.total_calls,
       ROUND(tu.total_seconds / t.grand_total_seconds * 100, 1) AS pct_of_total,
       ROUND(tu.total_seconds / t.grand_total_seconds * 18000, 0) AS allocated_eur
FROM   team_usage tu, totals t
ORDER BY allocated_eur DESC;

The cost attribution uses pg_stat_statements to measure each team's total execution time as a fraction of the grand total, then allocates the EUR 18,000 PostgreSQL budget proportionally. The Swiss billing team consumes 34.2% of PostgreSQL compute, paying EUR 6,156. The Austrian analytics team runs many more queries (892,000 vs 284,000) but they are smaller — consuming 25.5%. The ML team runs few queries (42,000) but each is large (feature engineering scans), consuming 15.1%. This measurement makes resource consumption visible and actionable: the Austrian analytics team can see that their 892,000 calls cost EUR 4,590 and evaluate whether query optimization or caching would reduce that cost.
When NOT to Use Chargeback

Cost allocation adds accounting overhead: the measurement queries, the monthly reports, the internal billing process, and the inevitable disputes ('that query was the platform team's migration, not ours'). If the total platform cost is under EUR 5,000/month, the allocation overhead may exceed the behavioral benefit. At AlpineEnergy, chargeback was introduced when the monthly cost exceeded EUR 20,000 and two teams were running unoptimized queries that consumed 40% of the cluster's resources. The chargeback model made the cost visible; within one quarter, both teams optimized their queries and the cluster utilization dropped 25%. Below EUR 5,000/month, a simpler 'showback' model (reporting costs without charging) achieves most of the behavioral benefit without the accounting overhead.

blocks.title: Showback before chargeback
blocks.content: Start with showback: report each team's resource consumption without charging them. Most teams optimize voluntarily when they see their usage — the visibility alone changes behavior. If showback does not reduce waste after two quarters, escalate to chargeback. AlpineEnergy ran showback for 6 months before switching to chargeback; three of the five teams optimized during the showback period without any financial consequence.

The mid-level track built a quality monitoring framework (Module 9) for one team's tables on a single PostgreSQL instance. At enterprise scale, observability spans 300+ tables across two engines, 10 teams, and three countries with distinct regulatory requirements. The monitoring system must answer three questions instantly: Is every SLA-bound table fresh? Is any table's quality degrading across any dimension? Is any engine's resource utilization approaching capacity limits? These questions must be answerable by any engineer on any team — not just the platform team. Platform observability is the shared visibility layer that makes the multi-team, multi-engine architecture manageable.
The Platform Health Dashboard

-- Platform health: freshness status for every SLA-bound table
-- Combines ownership, freshness metrics, and SLA definitions
SELECT own.table_catalog,
       own.table_schema,
       own.table_name,
       own.owning_team,
       own.sla_tier,
       dq.metric_value AS freshness_minutes,
       CASE
         WHEN own.sla_tier = 'platinum' AND dq.metric_value > 60  THEN 'BREACH'
         WHEN own.sla_tier = 'gold'     AND dq.metric_value > 120 THEN 'BREACH'
         WHEN own.sla_tier = 'silver'   AND dq.metric_value > 480 THEN 'BREACH'
         WHEN dq.metric_value IS NULL                             THEN 'NO DATA'
         ELSE 'OK'
       END AS sla_status
FROM   platform.table_ownership AS own
  LEFT JOIN LATERAL (
    SELECT metric_value
    FROM   monitoring.dq_metrics
    WHERE  table_name = own.table_name
      AND  metric_name = 'data_freshness_minutes'
    ORDER BY checked_at DESC
    LIMIT 1
  ) AS dq ON TRUE
WHERE  own.sla_tier IN ('platinum', 'gold')
ORDER BY sla_status DESC, freshness_minutes DESC NULLS FIRST;

The query joins the ownership registry with the latest freshness metric from dq_metrics to produce a real-time status for every SLA-bound table. Tables in BREACH status appear first; NO DATA (tables with no recent freshness check) appear next; OK tables appear last. This query is the backing data for the Grafana dashboard that AlpineEnergy's engineering leads check every morning. The dashboard is organized by country (tabs for CH, AT, IT) and by SLA tier (platinum tables at the top, gold tables below). Any engineer on any team can see the status of any table — the observability is shared, even though the ownership is distributed.
Engine Health Monitoring

Table freshness monitors the data. Engine health monitors the infrastructure. For PostgreSQL, the critical metrics are: connection pool utilization (PgBouncer stats), replication lag (for read replicas), autovacuum backlog (tables approaching wraparound), and disk utilization per tablespace. For Spark SQL, the critical metrics are: cluster utilization, pending job queue depth, shuffle spill rate, and Delta Lake file count per partition. Each metric has a threshold that triggers an alert. At AlpineEnergy, the PostgreSQL autovacuum backlog alert has prevented two potential transaction ID wraparound incidents — each of which would have required a full database shutdown for emergency vacuum.
Cross-Team Incident Visibility

When the Swiss billing pipeline fails, the Austrian analytics team needs to know — because their pipeline depends on the Swiss data. The observability system provides cross-team incident visibility by combining the dependency graph (lesson 7) with the monitoring alerts: when an incident is declared for a producer table, the system automatically notifies all registered consumer teams. At AlpineEnergy, the notification message includes the affected table, the producing team, the estimated resolution time, and the downstream impact assessment generated from the dependency graph. This proactive notification prevents the scenario where 5 teams independently discover the same upstream failure and each spends 30 minutes diagnosing it before realizing it is not their problem.

blocks.title: Observability is shared; responsibility is distributed
blocks.content: Every engineer can see every table's status. But each table's owning team is responsible for resolving its issues. The observability dashboard is not a centralized control room — it is a shared window. The platform team monitors the engines and the infrastructure. The domain teams monitor their tables. The analytics teams monitor their Platinum products. The shared dashboard makes each team's status visible to all teams, enabling the kind of coordinated incident response that distributed ownership requires.

This module designed a multi-engine, multi-team SQL platform from first principles: the dual-engine topology that separates OLTP and analytical workloads, the namespace hierarchy that makes tables discoverable across engines and countries, the ownership model that assigns accountability with database-level enforcement, the four-layer data architecture that governs data flow with layer contracts, the data product design that makes tables self-describing and contractual, the dependency graph that coordinates cross-team pipelines with failure propagation, the cost allocation model that makes resource consumption visible, and the observability layer that provides shared visibility with distributed responsibility.
The AlpineEnergy Architecture Summary

blocks.title: AlpineEnergy Group platform architecture
blocks.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                           │     Discovery Catalog / Registry     │
                           │  Manifests · Ownership · Contracts   │
                           └────────────┬────────────────────────┘
                                        │
     ┌──────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┐
     │                                  │                                │
  PostgreSQL                     CDC Pipeline                     Spark SQL
  (OLTP / Operational)         (Debezium → Kafka)          (Analytical / Batch)
     │                                  │                                │
  ┌──┴──────────┐                       │                    ┌───────────┴──┐
  │ CH  AT  IT  │                       │                    │  CH  AT  IT  │
  │ ┌─────────┐ │                       │                    │ ┌──────────┐ │
  │ │ Bronze  │ │───────────────────────▶│───────────────────▶│ │ Bronze   │ │
  │ │ Silver  │ │                       │                    │ │ Silver   │ │
  │ │ Gold    │ │                       │                    │ │ Gold     │ │
  │ └─────────┘ │                       │                    │ │ Platinum │ │
  └─────────────┘                       │                    │ └──────────┘ │
                                        │                    └──────────────┘
     │                                                               │
  Billing API                                              Executive Dashboard
  Customer Portal                                          ML Feature Store
  Regulatory Filing                                        Capacity Forecast
blocks.caption: The complete AlpineEnergy platform: PostgreSQL for operational workloads (billing, portal, regulatory), Spark SQL for analytical workloads (dashboards, ML, forecasting), connected by a CDC pipeline. Each country has its own database/catalog in both engines. The Discovery Catalog provides cross-team discoverability. The four-layer model (Bronze-Silver-Gold-Platinum) governs data flow in both engines.

blocks.content: Architecture Decision Records

Every architectural decision in this module — dual-engine topology, namespace convention, four-layer model, ownership enforcement, data product model — is recorded in an Architecture Decision Record (ADR): a short document that states the decision, the context, the alternatives considered, the trade-offs, and the conditions under which the decision should be revisited. AlpineEnergy maintains 24 ADRs in a Git repository alongside the schema registry. The ADRs serve two purposes: they document why decisions were made (preventing the common pattern of 'nobody knows why we do it this way'), and they define the expiration conditions (the circumstances under which the decision should be reconsidered — for example, 'revisit the dual-engine topology if the monthly cost exceeds EUR 50,000 or if a third engine is proposed').

The platform architecture is not static — it evolves as AlpineEnergy grows. When the Italian subsidiary was integrated 14 months ago, the architecture expanded from 2 countries to 3, from 6 teams to 10, and from 120 tables to 300. The namespace convention, the ownership model, and the dependency graph scaled without redesign because they were designed for extensibility: adding a country means adding a new database/catalog, not restructuring the hierarchy. The cost allocation model scaled because it measures proportional usage, not fixed allocations. The four-layer model scaled because the layer contracts are team-independent. The architecture that this module designed is the architecture that survives the next expansion — not just the current one.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: This module designed the platform's organizational architecture: who owns what, how teams coordinate, and how resources are governed. Module 2 goes inside the engines themselves: the PostgreSQL query planner's cost model and the Spark SQL Catalyst optimizer's rule phases. Understanding these internals is not academic — it is the diagnostic capability that lets you explain why a query plan changed after a PostgreSQL major version upgrade, why Catalyst chose a sort-merge join instead of a broadcast join despite the table being 10 MB, and why AQE's partition coalescing reduced 200 partitions to 8 when you expected 50. The platform architecture tells you where the data lives. The optimizer internals tell you how the data moves.

modules.title: Advanced Optimizer Internals
modules.description: Understand the PostgreSQL query planner and Spark SQL Catalyst optimizer at the implementation level — cost models, statistics propagation, plan selection algorithms, and the runtime adaptation mechanisms that determine how queries actually execute.

blocks.content: The mid-level track taught you to read EXPLAIN output and diagnose slow queries by identifying the bottleneck operator. That skill treats the optimizer as a black box: you observe its output (the plan) and infer its reasoning (why it chose a sequential scan instead of an index scan). The senior-level shift is from observing the output to understanding the mechanism. When the PostgreSQL planner produces a plan that seems wrong — a nested loop join on a 50-million-row table when a hash join would be 100x faster — the mid-level response is 'run ANALYZE and retry.' The senior-level response is 'the planner's cost estimate for the hash join was 4.2x higher than the nested loop because random_page_cost is set to 4.0 on SSD storage where 1.1 is appropriate.' Understanding the mechanism lets you fix the cause, not just retry and hope.
The Four-Phase Query Pipeline

blocks.title: PostgreSQL query processing pipeline
blocks.content:
SQL text
  │
  ▼
┌──────────┐     ┌───────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌───────────┐
│  Parser  │ ──▶ │ Rewriter  │ ──▶ │ Planner  │ ──▶ │ Executor  │
│          │     │           │     │          │     │           │
│ Syntax → │     │ Views →   │     │ Cost-    │     │ Runs the  │
│ parse    │     │ expanded  │     │ based    │     │ chosen    │
│ tree     │     │ Rules →   │     │ plan     │     │ plan      │
│          │     │ applied   │     │ selection│     │           │
└──────────┘     └───────────┘     └──────────┘     └───────────┘
                                        │
                                   The focus of
                                   this module
blocks.caption: The parser converts SQL text to a parse tree. The rewriter expands views and applies rules. The planner evaluates alternative execution strategies, estimates their cost, and selects the cheapest. The executor runs the selected plan. Modules 1-8 of the mid-level track focused on the executor's behavior. This module focuses on the planner — how it generates alternatives, how it estimates costs, and why it sometimes chooses wrong.

blocks.content: The planner phase is where the optimizer makes its decisions. It receives a rewritten query tree and must produce an executable plan. For a simple single-table scan, the decision is straightforward: sequential scan vs index scan, evaluated by cost. For a multi-table join, the decision space explodes combinatorially. A 5-table join has 5! = 120 possible join orderings. Each ordering has multiple strategy choices per join (nested loop, hash, merge). Each strategy has multiple scan choices per table input. The total search space for a 5-table query can exceed 10,000 candidate plans. The planner must enumerate enough of this space to find a good plan without spending more time planning than executing.
Join Enumeration and GEQO

PostgreSQL uses dynamic programming for join enumeration when the number of tables is small (default: 12 or fewer, controlled by geqo_threshold). Dynamic programming evaluates every possible pair of tables, finds the cheapest way to join each pair, then builds 3-table joins from the best 2-table results, and so on. This guarantees finding the optimal join order but scales as O(2^n) — which is tractable for 12 tables but catastrophic for 20. When the table count exceeds geqo_threshold, PostgreSQL switches to the Genetic Query Optimizer (GEQO): a randomized search that generates candidate orderings, evaluates their cost, and evolves better orderings through crossover and mutation. GEQO does not guarantee optimality but produces good plans in bounded time.

At AlpineEnergy, the Platinum enterprise dashboard joins 9 tables: 3 Gold fact tables (one per country), 3 dimension tables, 2 reference tables, and 1 calendar table. This is below the GEQO threshold, so PostgreSQL uses dynamic programming and finds the optimal ordering. But a proposed enhancement — adding 4 more reference tables for regulatory metadata — would push the count to 13 and trigger GEQO. The platform team tested both: the DP plan (with geqo = off, 2.3 seconds planning time) was 15% faster than the GEQO plan (0.1 seconds planning time). For a query that runs hourly, the 15% execution improvement justified the 2.3-second planning overhead. For an ad-hoc query that runs once, the GEQO plan is preferable — 0.1 seconds of planning beats 2.3 seconds even if the plan is slightly worse.

blocks.title: Planning time is part of total time
blocks.content: The optimal plan is not always the best plan. A plan that takes 5 seconds to find and 10 seconds to execute has a total cost of 15 seconds. A slightly worse plan that takes 0.1 seconds to find and 12 seconds to execute has a total cost of 12.1 seconds. For one-shot queries, faster planning with a slightly worse plan wins. For repeatedly executed queries (prepared statements, scheduled pipelines), the planning cost is amortized and the execution-optimal plan wins. AlpineEnergy's pipeline queries use prepared statements; the dashboard queries do not.

The planner evaluates candidate plans by estimating their cost. The cost model is the formula that converts physical operations (reading a page from disk, processing a tuple in CPU, performing a hash comparison) into a single numeric cost estimate. The plan with the lowest estimated cost wins. When the cost model is accurate, the planner chooses well. When it is inaccurate — because the cost parameters do not match the hardware, or because the statistics are stale — the planner chooses badly, and the 'cheapest' plan is actually the most expensive. Understanding the cost model is the key to understanding why the planner makes the decisions it does.
The Cost Parameters

rows: Cost of reading one page sequentially (the baseline unit)
Cost of reading one page randomly (relative to sequential)
CPU cost of processing one row
CPU cost of processing one index entry
CPU cost of evaluating one operator (e.g., a comparison)
Planner's estimate of total available cache (OS + shared_buffers)

blocks.content: The most consequential parameter is random_page_cost. At the default of 4.0, the planner believes a random page read is 4x more expensive than a sequential read — a reasonable assumption for spinning disks where the seek time dominates. On SSD storage, random reads are only 1.1-1.3x more expensive than sequential reads. With random_page_cost = 4.0 on SSD, the planner overestimates the cost of index scans (which are random reads) and underestimates the relative advantage of sequential scans. The result: the planner chooses sequential scans when index scans would be faster. AlpineEnergy's tuning of random_page_cost from 4.0 to 1.1 shifted 40+ query plans from sequential scans to index scans, reducing aggregate query time by 35%.
The Cost Formula

-- Sequential scan cost formula:
-- cost = (pages * seq_page_cost) + (rows * cpu_tuple_cost)

-- Example: meter_readings has 1,642,000 pages and 130,000,000 rows
-- Seq scan cost = (1,642,000 * 1.0) + (130,000,000 * 0.01)
--               = 1,642,000 + 1,300,000 = 2,942,000

-- Index scan cost formula (simplified):
-- cost = (index_pages * random_page_cost)
--      + (matching_rows * (cpu_index_tuple_cost + cpu_tuple_cost))
--      + (heap_fetches * random_page_cost)

-- Example: index scan for date = '2025-01-15' (selectivity ~0.03)
-- With random_page_cost = 4.0 (HDD default):
--   = (2,800 * 4.0) + (1,200,000 * 0.015) + (980,000 * 4.0)
--   = 11,200 + 18,000 + 3,920,000 = 3,949,200  ← MORE than seq scan

-- With random_page_cost = 1.1 (SSD-tuned):
--   = (2,800 * 1.1) + (1,200,000 * 0.015) + (980,000 * 1.1)
--   = 3,080 + 18,000 + 1,078,000 = 1,099,080   ← LESS than seq scan

-- The same query gets a DIFFERENT plan based on random_page_cost

The arithmetic reveals the mechanism. At random_page_cost = 4.0, the index scan's heap fetches (980,000 random page reads × 4.0) cost 3.9 million units — more than the entire sequential scan. The planner correctly chooses the sequential scan because, according to its cost model, the index scan is more expensive. At random_page_cost = 1.1, the same heap fetches cost 1.08 million units — less than the sequential scan's 2.94 million. The planner correctly chooses the index scan. The plan changed not because the data changed, but because the cost model was calibrated to match the actual hardware. This is why tuning cost parameters is the single highest-impact optimizer configuration change.
effective_cache_size and the Index Scan Decision

The planner uses effective_cache_size to estimate how much of the table and index data is already cached in memory. A higher effective_cache_size makes the planner believe that index scan heap fetches are more likely to hit the cache (avoiding disk I/O entirely), which reduces the estimated cost of index scans. At AlpineEnergy, the PostgreSQL server has 32 GB RAM with shared_buffers = 8 GB. The OS caches additional pages in filesystem cache. Setting effective_cache_size = 24 GB tells the planner that approximately 75% of the data is likely cached — making index scans on frequently accessed tables very cheap. Setting it too high (32 GB) overpromises cache availability and leads to index scans on cold data that actually hits disk. Setting it too low (8 GB) underpromises and favors sequential scans unnecessarily.

blocks.title: Default cost parameters assume spinning disks
blocks.content: PostgreSQL's default cost parameters were set when spinning disks were the norm. On modern SSD or NVMe storage, random_page_cost = 4.0 systematically biases the planner against index scans. On cloud-managed databases where storage is network-attached (EBS, Azure Managed Disks), the optimal random_page_cost depends on the storage tier — premium SSD may warrant 1.1, standard SSD may warrant 1.5, and network-throttled tiers may warrant 2.0. Measure the actual random vs sequential read ratio on your specific infrastructure before tuning.

PostgreSQL's planner is a cost-based optimizer that enumerates candidate plans and picks the cheapest. Spark SQL's Catalyst optimizer is a rule-based transformer that applies a sequence of transformation rules to the logical plan, progressively refining it into a physical plan. The two approaches have different failure modes: PostgreSQL's planner can choose a bad plan when the cost model is wrong (lesson 2), while Catalyst can produce a suboptimal plan when a necessary rule does not fire or rules interact in an unexpected order. Understanding Catalyst's rule architecture — how rules are organized into batches, how batches iterate, and why order matters — is the diagnostic foundation for Spark SQL plan issues.
The Four Optimization Phases

Catalyst processes a query through four sequential phases. Phase 1 (Analysis): resolves references — table names, column names, function signatures — against the catalog, producing an analyzed logical plan. Phase 2 (Logical Optimization): applies transformation rules that simplify the plan without changing its semantics — predicate pushdown, constant folding, column pruning, filter simplification. Phase 3 (Physical Planning): converts logical operators to physical operators — selecting hash join vs sort-merge join, choosing scan strategies, deciding broadcast thresholds. Phase 4 (Code Generation): compiles the physical plan into optimized Java bytecode using whole-stage code generation. Most optimizer issues occur in phases 2 and 3, where rule interactions determine the final plan structure.
Rule Batches and Fixed-Point Iteration

-- Catalyst's logical optimization batches (simplified from SparkOptimizer.scala)
-- Each batch runs repeatedly until the plan stops changing (fixed point)

Batch "Operator Optimizations" (maxIterations = 100):
  PushDownPredicates          -- push WHERE clauses into scan operators
  ColumnPruning               -- remove unused columns from intermediate results
  CollapseProject             -- merge adjacent SELECT lists
  CombineFilters              -- merge adjacent WHERE clauses
  ConstantFolding             -- evaluate constant expressions at plan time
  BooleanSimplification       -- simplify (x AND true) → x, (x OR false) → x
  SimplifyConditionals        -- simplify CASE WHEN with constant conditions
  RemoveRedundantAliases      -- remove unnecessary AS clauses
  ...

Batch "Join Reorder" (maxIterations = 1):
  CostBasedJoinReorder        -- reorder joins using CBO statistics (if enabled)

Batch "Decimal Optimizations" (maxIterations = 100):
  DecimalAggregates           -- optimize SUM/AVG on decimal types

The key insight: rules within a batch interact. PushDownPredicates may push a filter below a join, which changes the join's estimated output size, which changes whether CombineFilters can merge adjacent filters, which may enable another round of PushDownPredicates. The batch iterates until no rule changes the plan (fixed point) or the maximum iteration count is reached. In practice, most batches converge in 2-5 iterations. The maxIterations = 100 is a safety limit, not a target. If a batch reaches 100 iterations, it usually indicates a rule oscillation bug — two rules that undo each other's changes indefinitely.
Why Rule Order Matters

Within a batch, rules are applied in list order on each iteration. If ColumnPruning runs before PushDownPredicates, it may prune a column that PushDownPredicates needs for the pushdown — preventing the pushdown on this iteration. On the next iteration, PushDownPredicates runs first (because the plan changed from pruning), but the pruning has already removed the column. The batch iterates again, and eventually converges — but the convergence may produce a different plan than if the rules were ordered differently. Catalyst's rule ordering is carefully designed by the Spark team and is stable across minor versions, but it can change across major versions — which is why plan stability testing (lesson 6) is essential during Spark upgrades.
Inspecting Catalyst's Work

-- View the logical plan before and after optimization
EXPLAIN EXTENDED
SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'
  AND  customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
GROUP BY region;

-- The EXTENDED output shows:
-- == Parsed Logical Plan ==    (Phase 1: raw parse tree)
-- == Analyzed Logical Plan ==  (Phase 1: references resolved)
-- == Optimized Logical Plan == (Phase 2: rules applied)
-- == Physical Plan ==          (Phase 3: physical operators selected)

-- To see which rules fired:
-- SET spark.sql.planChangeLog.level = WARN
-- This logs every rule that changed the plan — invaluable for debugging

The EXPLAIN EXTENDED output shows the plan at each phase, letting you trace a specific decision to a specific optimization step. If PushDownPredicates did not push the date filter into the Delta scan, the Optimized Logical Plan will show the filter above the scan rather than inside it. The planChangeLog setting (when set to WARN) logs every rule invocation that changed the plan, including the before and after states — the most detailed diagnostic tool for understanding Catalyst's decisions. At AlpineEnergy, the platform team enables planChangeLog during Spark version upgrades to identify rules that fire differently in the new version.

blocks.title: PostgreSQL plans; Catalyst transforms
blocks.content: The fundamental difference: PostgreSQL's planner evaluates alternative complete plans and picks the cheapest (cost-based). Catalyst applies a sequence of local transformations and lets the rules converge to a plan (rule-based, with CBO for join ordering). PostgreSQL's failure mode is wrong cost estimates. Catalyst's failure mode is missing or non-firing rules. The diagnostic approach differs accordingly: for PostgreSQL, compare estimated vs actual row counts. For Catalyst, trace which rules fired and which did not.

Both optimizers depend on statistics — row counts, column cardinality, value distributions — to estimate the cost or selectivity of each operation. When statistics are accurate, the optimizer produces good plans. When they are stale or missing, the optimizer guesses — and the guesses compound across multiple operations. A 2x overestimate at the first join produces a 4x overestimate at the second join and an 8x overestimate at the third. At AlpineEnergy, a 6-table billing query produced a plan where Spark SQL estimated 12,000 output rows but the actual output was 14.8 million — a 1,233x underestimate that caused a broadcast join on a table that should have been sort-merge joined, exhausting driver memory.
PostgreSQL Statistics Collection

-- How PostgreSQL stores statistics: the pg_statistic catalog
-- ANALYZE samples the table and computes per-column statistics

-- View the statistics that the planner uses:
SELECT attname,
       n_distinct,      -- estimated distinct values (-1 = unique)
       most_common_vals, -- most frequent values
       most_common_freqs, -- frequency of each MCV
       correlation       -- physical ordering correlation (-1 to 1)
FROM   pg_stats
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver'
  AND  tablename  = 'meter_readings'
  AND  attname IN ('region', 'customer_type', 'date');

-- Example output for 'region' (10 sub-regions, uneven distribution):
-- n_distinct:       10
-- most_common_vals: {BERN-NORTH,ZURICH-EAST,BERN-SOUTH,...}
-- most_common_freqs: {0.135,0.130,0.128,...}
-- correlation:       0.92  (physically clustered by region)

PostgreSQL stores two primary statistical structures per column: the Most Common Values (MCV) list and the histogram. The MCV list contains the N most frequent values and their frequencies (where N = the statistics target, default 100). Values not in the MCV list are assumed to be uniformly distributed across the histogram buckets. The planner uses these statistics to estimate selectivity: WHERE region = 'BERN-NORTH' has selectivity 0.135 (from the MCV frequency). WHERE region = 'SOME-RARE-REGION' has selectivity estimated from the histogram. The correlation statistic tells the planner how well the physical row order matches the column's sort order — high correlation (> 0.9) means an index scan produces nearly sequential I/O, making it much cheaper than the random I/O assumption.
Catalyst Statistics and CBO

Spark SQL's Catalyst optimizer uses statistics differently depending on whether CBO is enabled (spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true). Without CBO, Catalyst uses only file-level statistics from Delta Lake's transaction log — row count and file size — to estimate sizes. It does not know column cardinality, value distribution, or selectivity. With CBO enabled, Catalyst reads column-level statistics computed by ANALYZE TABLE and uses them for join reordering and strategy selection. At AlpineEnergy, CBO is enabled for all pipeline queries (where statistics are fresh from pipeline-integrated ANALYZE) and disabled for ad-hoc notebook queries (where statistics may be stale and CBO's join reordering could produce worse plans than the user-specified ordering).
Statistics Degradation Through Operators

Selectivity estimates degrade through each plan operator. A filter with 3% selectivity on a 100-million-row table produces an estimated 3 million rows — accurate if the statistics are fresh. A subsequent join with another table multiplies the row estimate by the join selectivity — which depends on the join key's cardinality statistics in both tables. If the join key statistics are stale (the cardinality changed since the last ANALYZE), the join's output estimate compounds the error from the filter's estimate. After 3-4 operations, the cumulative error can be orders of magnitude. The AlpineEnergy 1,233x underestimate from the lesson opening was caused by exactly this compounding: three successive joins where each join's selectivity was underestimated by ~10x because the customer_type distribution had shifted after a bulk meter reclassification.
Extended Statistics for Correlated Columns

-- PostgreSQL 10+: extended statistics for correlated columns
-- Standard statistics assume column independence:
-- P(region='BERN-NORTH' AND customer_type='INDUSTRIAL')
--   = P(region='BERN-NORTH') * P(customer_type='INDUSTRIAL')
--   = 0.135 * 0.04 = 0.0054  (estimated)
--   Actual: 0.0012  (BERN-NORTH has fewer industrial meters)

-- Create extended statistics to capture the correlation
CREATE STATISTICS stat_region_type (dependencies)
  ON region, customer_type
  FROM silver.meter_readings;

ANALYZE silver.meter_readings;

-- Now the planner knows the functional dependency:
-- P(customer_type | region) is not uniform
-- The estimate for the combined filter is much more accurate

Standard per-column statistics assume independence: the planner multiplies individual selectivities to estimate combined selectivities. This assumption fails when columns are correlated — at AlpineEnergy, BERN-NORTH has 13.5% of all meters but only 3.1% of industrial meters (because the Bern region is predominantly residential). The independence assumption estimates 0.54% for the combined filter; the actual selectivity is 0.12% — a 4.5x overestimate that cascades through downstream joins. Extended statistics (CREATE STATISTICS with dependencies or ndistinct) capture these correlations and correct the estimates. At AlpineEnergy, extended statistics on (region, customer_type) improved the billing query's final row estimate from 1,233x error to 2.1x error — not perfect, but within the range where the planner makes correct strategy decisions.

blocks.title: Create extended statistics for every multi-column filter pattern
blocks.content: Identify the WHERE clause patterns in your top 20 queries. Any pattern that filters on two or more columns simultaneously is a candidate for extended statistics. At AlpineEnergy, the three multi-column patterns are (region, customer_type), (region, date), and (customer_type, date). Extended statistics on all three reduced the median row estimate error from 8.2x to 1.6x across the pipeline queries — the single most impactful statistics improvement after initial ANALYZE.

Catalyst's logical optimization (lesson 3) runs before execution begins, using statistics collected by ANALYZE. Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) runs during execution, using the actual intermediate results to re-optimize the remaining plan. This addresses the fundamental limitation of compile-time optimization: no matter how accurate the statistics are, they are estimates — and estimates can be wrong. AQE observes the actual data sizes after each shuffle stage and adjusts the downstream plan accordingly. It can coalesce partitions when there are too many small ones, convert a sort-merge join to a broadcast join when the actual post-filter size is small enough, and split skewed partitions when one partition is disproportionately large.
Partition Coalescing

After a shuffle, AQE inspects the actual partition sizes. If the shuffle produced 200 partitions but most contain less than 64 MB (the advisory target controlled by spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes), AQE coalesces adjacent small partitions into fewer, larger partitions. This reduces the task count and the per-task overhead. At AlpineEnergy, a filtered query on the Swiss residential meters produced 200 shuffle partitions (the default spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 200) with an average size of 4 MB per partition. AQE coalesced them to 14 partitions of ~57 MB each — reducing the task count by 93% and the total execution time by 40% (because 186 eliminated tasks each had 50ms of scheduling and serialization overhead).
Runtime Join Strategy Conversion

-- Catalyst compile-time estimate: join input = 1.2 GB → sort-merge join
-- AQE runtime observation: join input after filter = 8 MB → broadcast join

== Physical Plan (before AQE) ==
SortMergeJoin [meter_id]
├── Sort [meter_id]
│   └── Filter (customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL')
│       └── Scan meter_readings (estimated 1.2 GB)
└── Sort [meter_id]
    └── Scan meters (45 MB)

== Physical Plan (after AQE) ==
BroadcastHashJoin [meter_id]          ← AQE converted to broadcast
├── Filter (customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL')
│   └── Scan meter_readings (actual 8 MB)  ← actual << estimated
└── Scan meters (45 MB)

Catalyst estimated the meter_readings scan at 1.2 GB (based on table-level statistics before the filter). After the filter for INDUSTRIAL customer type (which is only 4% of the data), the actual output was 8 MB. AQE detected that the actual size (8 MB) is below the broadcast threshold (spark.sql.adaptive.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold, default 30 MB) and converted the sort-merge join to a broadcast hash join — eliminating the sort on both sides and the shuffle on the large side. The broadcast join completed in 2 seconds versus the sort-merge join's estimated 28 seconds. This is AQE's highest-value optimization: correcting join strategy choices that compile-time statistics got wrong.
Skew Splitting

AQE's skew join optimization detects partitions that are significantly larger than the median and splits them into sub-partitions. The detection threshold is controlled by spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes (default 256 MB) and spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor (default 5x median). When a skewed partition is detected, AQE splits it and replicates the matching partition from the other join side to each sub-partition. At AlpineEnergy, the ZURICH-EAST region contains 18% of all meters (versus the 10% average), causing a 1.8x skew on region-partitioned joins. AQE splits the ZURICH-EAST partition into 2 sub-partitions and replicates the corresponding dimension data, reducing the skewed task's runtime from 48 seconds to 26 seconds.
When AQE Does Not Activate

AQE requires a shuffle boundary to observe actual sizes. Queries without shuffles (single-partition scans, broadcast-only joins) do not benefit from AQE because there is no intermediate point where AQE can measure sizes and re-optimize. AQE also does not activate when spark.sql.adaptive.enabled = false (check this first during diagnosis), or when the query uses a cached DataFrame (the cache provides size information at compile time, so AQE considers the statistics already accurate). At AlpineEnergy, the ML team's feature engineering pipeline caches the meter dimension and joins it with readings — AQE does not re-optimize this join because the cached dimension's size is known at compile time.

blocks.title: AQE compensates for statistics errors, not for missing statistics
blocks.content: AQE can convert a sort-merge join to a broadcast join when the actual data is smaller than estimated. It cannot decide to use an index (Spark SQL does not have indexes) or to push a predicate into a scan (that is a compile-time decision). AQE's three features — partition coalescing, join strategy conversion, and skew splitting — all operate on shuffle boundaries. Compile-time optimizations (predicate pushdown, column pruning, filter simplification) must still be correct for AQE to have anything useful to adapt. The best plans come from accurate statistics (lessons 2-4) combined with AQE as a safety net for the residual estimation error.

A query that has been running in 8 seconds for 6 months suddenly takes 90 seconds. The data did not change. The query did not change. What changed was the plan: the optimizer selected a different execution strategy. Plan regressions occur after PostgreSQL major version upgrades (new planner heuristics), after Spark minor version upgrades (modified Catalyst rules), after ANALYZE runs on tables with changed distributions (new statistics shift cost estimates past tipping points), and after configuration changes (a DBA tuned random_page_cost, shifting the index scan threshold). Plan stability — detecting and preventing unwanted plan changes — is an operational discipline that the mid-level track did not cover because mid-level platforms rarely survive long enough to experience version-induced regressions.
The Plan Fingerprint

-- Capture a plan fingerprint for regression detection
-- The fingerprint captures the plan structure without runtime-specific values

-- PostgreSQL: extract the plan structure as JSON and hash it
SELECT md5(
  (EXPLAIN (FORMAT JSON) 
   SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw)
   FROM silver.meter_readings
   WHERE date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'
   GROUP BY region
  )::TEXT
) AS plan_hash;

-- Store the fingerprint alongside the performance log
INSERT INTO monitoring.performance_log
    (query_name, execution_ms, rows_affected, plan_hash)
VALUES ('billing_regional_summary', 8200, 10, 'a3f8c1d2...');

The plan fingerprint (MD5 hash of the JSON-formatted EXPLAIN output) changes when the plan structure changes — a different join algorithm, a different scan strategy, a different join ordering. It does not change when only the runtime metrics change (actual rows, actual time). By storing the fingerprint in the performance_log alongside execution time, the regression detection query (Module 10, lesson 3) can distinguish between two causes of slowdown: same plan on more data (plan_hash unchanged, execution time increased — likely data growth) versus different plan (plan_hash changed, execution time increased — plan regression).
Plan Regression Detection

-- Detect plan changes for SLA-bound queries
-- Compare today's plan hash against the last 30 days' most common hash
WITH recent_plans AS (
  SELECT query_name, plan_hash,
         COUNT(*) AS occurrences,
         AVG(execution_ms) AS avg_ms
  FROM   monitoring.performance_log
  WHERE  executed_at >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30 days'
  GROUP BY query_name, plan_hash
),
baseline AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT ON (query_name)
         query_name, plan_hash AS baseline_hash, avg_ms AS baseline_ms
  FROM   recent_plans
  ORDER BY query_name, occurrences DESC  -- most common = baseline
),
today AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT ON (query_name)
         query_name, plan_hash AS current_hash, execution_ms AS current_ms
  FROM   monitoring.performance_log
  WHERE  executed_at >= CURRENT_DATE
  ORDER BY query_name, executed_at DESC
)
SELECT b.query_name,
       b.baseline_hash, t.current_hash,
       b.baseline_ms, t.current_ms,
       CASE WHEN b.baseline_hash <> t.current_hash THEN 'PLAN CHANGED' ELSE 'STABLE' END AS status
FROM   baseline b JOIN today t ON b.query_name = t.query_name
WHERE  b.baseline_hash <> t.current_hash
   OR  t.current_ms > b.baseline_ms * 1.5;

The query identifies two types of regressions: plan changes (different hash) and performance degradation on the same plan (same hash but > 50% slower). Plan changes require investigation even if the new plan is faster — because a plan that is faster today on current data may be slower tomorrow on different data. At AlpineEnergy, the regression detection query runs daily and its output feeds a Slack notification to the platform team. A plan change on a platinum-tier query triggers a mandatory investigation; a plan change on a gold-tier query triggers a review in the next sprint.
Plan Pinning Strategies

When a plan regression is confirmed, the immediate fix is to pin the previous plan. In PostgreSQL, plan pinning is achieved through optimizer hints (via the pg_hint_plan extension) or by adjusting cost parameters to make the optimizer prefer the original strategy. In Spark SQL, plan pinning uses query hints (/*+ BROADCAST(t) */, /*+ MERGE(t) */) to force specific join strategies. At AlpineEnergy, the platform team pins plans only as a temporary measure — the permanent fix is to update statistics, adjust cost parameters, or file a Spark JIRA if a Catalyst rule change is the root cause. Permanent plan pinning via hints is an anti-pattern because it prevents the optimizer from adapting to future data changes.

blocks.title: Plan pinning is a bandage, not a fix
blocks.content: A hint that forces a broadcast join works today when the table is 20 MB. Next year, when the table grows to 500 MB, the forced broadcast will exhaust driver memory. The correct response to a plan regression is to fix the root cause: update statistics if they are stale, tune cost parameters if they are wrong, or report the Catalyst rule change if a Spark upgrade is responsible. Pin the plan only as a temporary measure while the root cause is being resolved — and set a calendar reminder to remove the hint.

Join ordering — the sequence in which tables are joined — is the highest-impact optimizer decision for multi-table queries. A 5-table join with the optimal ordering may produce 50,000 intermediate rows. The same 5-table join with the worst ordering may produce 50 million intermediate rows before the final filter reduces them back to 50,000. The difference is which joins are performed first: joining the two most selective tables first (producing small intermediates) versus joining two large tables first (producing enormous intermediates that are filtered only later). Both PostgreSQL and Catalyst have join ordering algorithms, but they differ in approach, capability, and failure modes.
PostgreSQL Dynamic Programming

PostgreSQL's dynamic programming approach evaluates all possible subsets of tables. For a 5-table join {A, B, C, D, E}, it first evaluates all 10 pairs: (A⋈B), (A⋈C), ... (D⋈E). For each pair, it selects the cheapest join strategy and records the cost. It then evaluates all 3-table subsets using the cheapest 2-table results as building blocks: (A⋈B)⋈C, (A⋈C)⋈B, and so on. This bottom-up construction guarantees finding the globally optimal ordering for the given cost estimates. The time complexity is O(2^n × n) — which is why PostgreSQL limits it to geqo_threshold tables (default 12). For 12 tables, DP evaluates ~49,000 subsets; for 20 tables, it would evaluate ~20 million.
Catalyst CBO Join Reorder

Catalyst's CBO join reordering (when spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled = true) uses a different algorithm: it evaluates star-schema-aware join reordering that prioritizes joining the fact table with the most selective dimension first. This heuristic works well for the common analytical pattern (one large fact table joined with multiple small dimension tables) but poorly for multi-hop pipelines where there is no single fact table. At AlpineEnergy, the billing pipeline's star-schema join benefits from CBO reordering, but the enrichment pipeline's 5-table chain (readings → meters → substations → regions → regulatory_zones) does not because no table is clearly the 'fact' table in the chain.
Manual Join Ordering

-- When the optimizer's ordering is wrong, manual ordering via CTEs
-- forces the execution sequence

-- Optimizer's ordering (wrong): joins readings with regions first (huge intermediate)
-- Manual ordering: join meters with regions first (small intermediate), then join readings

WITH enriched_meters AS (
  -- Step 1: small-to-small join (1.2M meters × 25 regions = 1.2M rows)
  SELECT m.meter_id, m.customer_type, r.region_name, r.country
  FROM   silver.meters AS m
    INNER JOIN silver.regions AS r ON m.region = r.region_id
),
final AS (
  -- Step 2: small-to-large join (enriched 1.2M × readings filtered to 1 month)
  SELECT em.region_name, em.country, em.customer_type,
         SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
    INNER JOIN enriched_meters AS em ON mr.meter_id = em.meter_id
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
  GROUP BY em.region_name, em.country, em.customer_type
)
SELECT * FROM final;

The CTE-based ordering forces a specific join sequence: first join meters with regions (1.2 million × 25 = 1.2 million rows, the small-to-small join), then join the enriched result with the filtered readings. If the optimizer had joined readings with regions first (37.2 million × 25 = 37.2 million rows, the large-to-small join), the intermediate result is 31x larger. The CTE materialization forces the optimizer to process the CTEs in order rather than merging them into a single optimization scope. This is a production pattern, not a hack — at AlpineEnergy, the enrichment pipeline uses CTE-ordered joins because the optimizer consistently misjudges the join order due to correlated selectivities that extended statistics do not fully capture.
When NOT to Override the Optimizer

Manual join ordering fixes one query at one point in time. It does not adapt when data distributions change, when new indexes are created, or when the query's filter conditions vary (a parameterized query with different region values has different optimal orderings). Override the optimizer only when the plan regression is reproducible, the root cause is understood (correlated selectivities, stale statistics, or a known optimizer limitation), and the override is documented with its expiration condition. At AlpineEnergy, every manual join ordering has an ADR (Architecture Decision Record) that states the reason, the measured improvement, and the condition for removal.

blocks.title: The optimizer is right 95% of the time
blocks.content: Most queries do not need manual join ordering. The optimizer's dynamic programming (PostgreSQL) or CBO heuristics (Catalyst) produce good orderings for the vast majority of query patterns. The 5% where manual intervention helps are typically: queries with 4+ joins and correlated filter columns, cross-engine federated queries where one side's statistics are unavailable, and queries with highly skewed key distributions. Diagnose with EXPLAIN ANALYZE before overriding — and remove the override when the root cause is fixed.

Predicate pushdown moves filter conditions as close to the data source as possible, reducing the amount of data that flows through the plan. You verified pushdown in the mid-level track by checking EXPLAIN for 'Pushed Filters' in the scan node. At senior level, you need to understand the mechanism: how PostgreSQL's qual pushdown differs from Catalyst's PushDownPredicates rule, which predicates can be pushed and which cannot, what happens when pushdown fails silently (the filter evaluates above the scan, scanning all data), and how to diagnose and fix pushdown failures in federated queries where the predicate must cross an engine boundary.
PostgreSQL Qual Pushdown

PostgreSQL's planner pushes WHERE clause conditions (quals) into subqueries and views during the rewriting and planning phases. A qual on a view's underlying column is pushed into the view's definition, allowing the scan to use an index on that column. The planner pushes quals through UNION ALL (applying the filter to each branch independently), through outer joins (when the filter is on the non-nullable side), and through window functions (when the filter does not reference the window result).
