At AlpineEnergy, the CI constraint verification tests (Module 6) check that all foreign keys required for join elimination are present — catching dropped constraints before they disable elimination in production.

The mid-level track used materialized views for pre-computed aggregations: a billing summary refreshed nightly, consumed by dashboards throughout the day. At senior level, materialized views serve a broader architectural purpose: pre-computing expensive joins so that downstream consumers avoid repeating the same join. When 8 pipelines all join meter_readings with meters, substations, and regions, each pipeline executes the same 3-table join independently — 8 repetitions of the same work. A materialized enrichment view that pre-joins the three dimension tables with the fact table eliminates 7 redundant joins. The materialized view is not just faster for each consumer — it is faster for the platform, reducing aggregate compute by 87.5%.
The Enrichment View Pattern

-- Materialized enrichment view: pre-join the 3 most commonly joined tables
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW gold.mv_enriched_readings AS
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region,
       m.customer_type, m.substation_id,
       s.substation_name, s.capacity_mw,
       r.region_name, r.country
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  INNER JOIN silver.substations AS s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
  INNER JOIN silver.regions AS r ON m.region = r.region_id
WITH DATA;

-- Index for the most common consumer access patterns
CREATE INDEX idx_mv_enriched_date_region
  ON gold.mv_enriched_readings (date, region);
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_mv_enriched_pk
  ON gold.mv_enriched_readings (meter_id, date);

-- Concurrent refresh: no downtime for consumers
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY gold.mv_enriched_readings;

-- Consumer query: reads from the materialized view — zero joins at query time
SELECT region_name, country, SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   gold.mv_enriched_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY region_name, country;

The materialized view pre-joins readings with meters, substations, and regions. The 8 consumer pipelines read from the view instead of executing the 3-table join. The CONCURRENTLY refresh (which requires the unique index) allows consumers to read the old data while the refresh runs — zero downtime. The refresh takes approximately 4 minutes for 150 million monthly rows. Without the materialized view, the 8 consumers would each execute a 4-minute join — 32 minutes of aggregate compute replaced by 4 minutes of refresh. At AlpineEnergy, 5 materialized enrichment views exist, serving 35 consumer pipelines and saving approximately 120 minutes of aggregate compute per day.
Refresh Strategy Architecture

The refresh must complete before the consumers that depend on it run. At AlpineEnergy, the enrichment view refresh is a pipeline step between Silver loading and Gold computation: Silver load (05:30) → ANALYZE (05:32) → materialized view refresh (05:36) → Gold pipeline (05:40). The 4-minute refresh window fits within the 06:00 SLA with 20 minutes of margin. If the refresh exceeds 4 minutes (because data volume grew), the Gold pipeline's start time shifts — consuming margin. The platform team monitors refresh duration as a performance metric, with a warning threshold at 6 minutes and a critical threshold at 8 minutes. The capacity planning review (Module 10) projects when refresh duration will exceed the SLA margin.
When NOT to Use Enrichment Views

Materialized enrichment views add storage (the view stores a copy of the joined data) and refresh latency (data is stale between refreshes). For joins that only one consumer executes, the materialized view adds overhead without benefit — the consumer could execute the join directly. For joins where the dimension changes frequently (hourly pricing, real-time grid status), the refresh latency is unacceptable — consumers need current dimension values, not last night's. At AlpineEnergy, the real-time grid operations dashboard joins readings with current_grid_status directly (no materialized view) because the grid status changes every 15 minutes and a nightly-refreshed view would show stale operational data.

blocks.title: The materialized-view-first policy
blocks.content: At AlpineEnergy, the join architecture review asks: 'Is this join executed by 3 or more consumers?' If yes, the default recommendation is a materialized enrichment view. The producing team owns the view, refreshes it as part of their pipeline, and the consuming teams read from it. This policy has reduced aggregate compute by 40% across the Gold layer. Exceptions require an ADR documenting why the materialized view is not appropriate (staleness requirements, single consumer, join too simple to justify the view overhead).

Join skew occurs when one join key value matches disproportionately more rows than others. AlpineEnergy's ZURICH-EAST region contains 18% of all meters (versus the 10% average) — a 1.8x skew on region-keyed joins. The BERN-NORTH region has 35% of all industrial meters (versus the 22% average) — a 1.6x skew on region + customer_type composite keys. At mid-level, skew was handled reactively with AQE's skew splitting (Module 2, lesson 5). At senior level, skew is handled architecturally — designing the join keys, the partition strategy, and the data model to prevent skew from occurring in the first place, rather than mitigating it after the optimizer encounters it.
Key Redesign: Composite Keys

-- Problem: join on region creates skewed partitions
-- ZURICH-EAST gets 1.8x more data than the average region

-- Solution: add a hash bucket to the join key, distributing each region
-- across N buckets for uniform partition sizes
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings
  ADD COLUMN join_bucket INTEGER
  GENERATED ALWAYS AS (abs(hashtext(meter_id)) % 16) STORED;

ALTER TABLE silver.meters
  ADD COLUMN join_bucket INTEGER
  GENERATED ALWAYS AS (abs(hashtext(meter_id)) % 16) STORED;

-- Now join on (region, join_bucket) instead of (region) alone:
-- ZURICH-EAST's 18% share is split across 16 buckets = 1.1% per bucket
-- Every partition is approximately equal in size
SELECT mr.region, SUM(mr.daily_kw)
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
   AND mr.join_bucket = m.join_bucket  -- distributes join across 16 buckets
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region;

The composite key (meter_id, join_bucket) distributes each region's data across 16 hash buckets. The join processes 16 smaller partitions instead of one large partition per region, and each partition is approximately 1/16th the size. The ZURICH-EAST skew is absorbed: instead of one partition with 1.8x the average data, there are 16 partitions with ~1.1x the average data. The GENERATED ALWAYS AS column is computed automatically on INSERT — no pipeline change required. The trade-off: the additional join condition on join_bucket adds complexity to every query that joins these tables. At AlpineEnergy, the join_bucket column is exposed through the enrichment view (lesson 5) and is invisible to consumers.
Pre-Aggregation to Reduce Skew

When the skew is on the join side that produces many-to-one matches (many readings per meter, many meters per region), pre-aggregating the many side before the join eliminates the skew. Instead of joining 37.2 million readings with 1.2 million meters (producing a 37.2 million row intermediate), pre-aggregate readings to 1.2 million rows (one per meter per month) and then join with meters. The aggregation reduces the join input by 31x, and the resulting join is balanced because both sides have approximately the same row count. At AlpineEnergy, the billing pipeline pre-aggregates daily readings to monthly meter totals before joining with the meters dimension — reducing the join from 37.2M × 1.2M to 1.2M × 1.2M and eliminating all join skew.
When NOT to Redesign for Skew

Key redesign and pre-aggregation change the data model or the query structure — they are permanent architectural decisions. If AQE's runtime skew splitting handles the skew adequately (the skewed task takes 48 seconds instead of 26 seconds, but the SLA is 120 seconds), the architectural change is unnecessary overhead. The decision threshold at AlpineEnergy: if skew causes a task to exceed 2x the median task duration and the pipeline's SLA margin is less than 30%, the architectural fix is justified. If the skew produces a 1.5x task duration with comfortable SLA margin, AQE's runtime mitigation is sufficient. The performance_log tracks per-task durations, enabling quantitative skew measurement.

blocks.title: Measure skew before fixing it
blocks.content: Skew that feels significant may be operationally irrelevant. A 2x skewed partition on a 10-second join adds 10 seconds — unlikely to breach an SLA. A 2x skewed partition on a 30-minute join adds 30 minutes — almost certainly an SLA problem. Quantify the skew's impact on pipeline duration before committing to an architectural fix. At AlpineEnergy, the join architecture review includes a skew impact analysis: the ratio of the slowest task to the median task, and the absolute time difference between skewed and non-skewed execution.

A multi-hop join chains 5 or more tables in sequence: readings → meters → substations → regions → regulatory_zones → compliance_rules. Each join's output feeds the next join's input. The optimization challenge is that the optimizer evaluates joins locally (each join against its immediate inputs) but the globally optimal strategy may require changing an upstream join to benefit a downstream one. The most impactful optimization for multi-hop joins is not at the join level — it is the materialization boundary: the point where an intermediate result is written to a table (or materialized CTE) before the next join reads it. Choosing the right boundary can reduce total pipeline time by 50-70%.
The Materialization Boundary Decision

Without materialization, the entire 6-table join runs as a single query. The optimizer must choose a join order for all 6 tables simultaneously — a search space that may exceed the DP planner's capacity (lesson 1 of Module 2), triggering GEQO with its random approximation. With a materialization boundary (writing the first 3 joins' result to a temp table, then joining the temp table with the remaining 3 tables), each sub-query is a 3-table join — well within the DP planner's capacity, with deterministic optimal ordering. At AlpineEnergy, the enrichment pipeline originally ran as a single 6-table query. Inserting a materialization boundary between join 3 (readings → meters → substations) and join 4 (→ regions → regulatory_zones → compliance_rules) reduced total execution time from 14 minutes to 5.5 minutes — a 61% improvement from splitting one query into two.
Where to Place the Boundary

-- The enrichment pipeline with a materialization boundary:
-- Step 1: First 3 joins → write to temp table
CREATE TEMP TABLE enriched_meters AS
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date,
       m.customer_type, s.substation_name, s.capacity_mw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  INNER JOIN silver.substations AS s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01';

-- Analyze the temp table so the planner has statistics for step 2
ANALYZE enriched_meters;

-- Step 2: Remaining 3 joins read from the temp table
SELECT em.meter_id, em.daily_kw, em.date,
       em.customer_type,
       r.region_name, r.country,
       rz.zone_classification,
       cr.compliance_rule
FROM   enriched_meters AS em
  INNER JOIN silver.regions AS r ON em.region = r.region_id
  INNER JOIN silver.regulatory_zones AS rz ON r.region_id = rz.region_id
  INNER JOIN silver.compliance_rules AS cr ON rz.zone_classification = cr.zone_id;

The boundary is placed where the intermediate result is smallest relative to the inputs — the point of maximum data reduction. Joins 1-3 reduce 37.2 million readings × 1.2 million meters × 200 substations to 37.2 million enriched rows (the readings determine the row count because the dimension joins are one-to-one). Joins 4-6 add attributes from 25 regions, 25 regulatory zones, and 80 compliance rules — all small dimension tables. The boundary captures the expensive work (scanning 37.2 million readings and joining with meters) and the subsequent joins are fast (joining 37.2 million rows with 25-row dimension tables). The ANALYZE between steps ensures the planner has accurate statistics for the temp table.
CTE Materialization vs Temp Tables

PostgreSQL offers two materialization mechanisms: CTEs (WITH ... AS MATERIALIZED) and temp tables. CTEs are simpler (no explicit CREATE/DROP) but do not support indexes or ANALYZE — the planner uses estimated statistics for the CTE's output. Temp tables support both indexes and ANALYZE, giving the planner accurate statistics for subsequent joins. For multi-hop pipelines where the second query's plan quality depends on accurate intermediate statistics, temp tables are preferable. At AlpineEnergy, all multi-hop pipelines with 5+ tables use temp tables with ANALYZE. Pipelines with 3-4 tables use CTEs because the planner's estimates are accurate enough for small join counts.

blocks.title: The boundary changes as data grows
blocks.content: The optimal boundary depends on the relative sizes of the joined tables — which change as data grows. Today's boundary between join 3 and join 4 may be suboptimal next year when a new dimension table makes join 4 expensive. At AlpineEnergy, the materialization boundary for each multi-hop pipeline is recorded in the pipeline's ADR with its rationale and the data sizes that justified it. The quarterly performance review evaluates whether boundaries need to shift based on updated table sizes and execution metrics.

A semi-join returns rows from the left side that have at least one match on the right side, without duplicating left rows for multiple matches: SELECT * FROM readings WHERE meter_id IN (SELECT meter_id FROM meters WHERE customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'). The naive execution fetches all readings and all industrial meter IDs, then hash-probes. Semi-join pushdown converts this into a filter that is pushed to the scan level: the right side's distinct values are extracted, encoded as a compact data structure (typically a Bloom filter), and sent to the left side's scan operator. The scan evaluates each row against the Bloom filter before reading it from storage — skipping rows that definitely do not match, potentially reducing I/O by 80% or more.
PostgreSQL Semi-Join Flattening

-- PostgreSQL flattens IN-subquery to a semi-join
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.meter_id IN (
    SELECT m.meter_id FROM silver.meters AS m
    WHERE  m.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'
  );

-- Plan shows: Hash Semi Join
--   Hash Cond: (mr.meter_id = m.meter_id)
--   → Seq Scan on meter_readings (filtered by date)
--   → Hash
--       → Seq Scan on meters (filtered by INDUSTRIAL)

-- The semi-join ensures no duplicate readings even if a meter
-- appears multiple times in the meters table (it does not here,
-- but the optimizer does not assume uniqueness without a constraint)

PostgreSQL's planner converts the IN-subquery to a Hash Semi Join: it hashes the industrial meter IDs, then probes each reading against the hash table. The semi-join differs from an inner join in that it never produces duplicate left rows — each reading appears at most once in the output, regardless of how many matches exist on the right side. At AlpineEnergy, the semi-join pattern is used for all 'filter by dimension attribute' queries: industrial meters, active meters, Swiss meters. The hash semi-join is efficient when the right side is small (the industrial meters hash table fits in memory) and the left side is large (37.2 million readings scanned sequentially).
Spark SQL DPP + Bloom Filter

Spark SQL extends semi-join pushdown with Bloom filters for partitioned tables. When a join between a partitioned fact table and a filtered dimension table is detected, Spark SQL extracts the distinct join key values from the dimension side, constructs a Bloom filter, and pushes it to the fact table's scan operator. The scan evaluates each row against the Bloom filter before the shuffle — skipping rows that the join will not match. Combined with Dynamic Partition Pruning (Module 3, lesson 4), the Bloom filter further reduces the scan within the surviving partitions. At AlpineEnergy, the SCADA telemetry pipeline filters 8 TB of turbine data to match only turbines in the Bern region. DPP prunes to 4 date partitions. The Bloom filter within those partitions skips 82% of rows that belong to non-Bern turbines — reducing the shuffle volume from 1.2 TB to 216 GB.
When Bloom Filters Do Not Help

Bloom filters have a false positive rate: some non-matching rows pass the filter and are processed by the join (where they are correctly eliminated). The false positive rate increases with the number of distinct values in the filter. For high-cardinality join keys (meter_id with 5 million distinct values), the Bloom filter is large and the false positive rate is high — potentially 10-20%, reducing the scan benefit to 80-90% instead of the 95-99% achieved with low-cardinality keys (region with 25 values). At AlpineEnergy, Bloom filter pushdown is effective for the region-filtered queries (25 distinct values, < 1% false positive rate) but provides minimal benefit for the meter_id-filtered queries (5 million distinct values, ~15% false positive rate). The platform team's guideline: expect significant benefit when the right side has < 10,000 distinct values; expect diminishing returns above 100,000.

blocks.title: Verify Bloom filter pushdown in the plan
blocks.content: In Spark SQL, check the physical plan for 'DynamicPruningExpression' in the scan node — this indicates that a Bloom filter or DPP expression was pushed to the scan. If the expression appears only as a Filter node above the scan, pushdown did not occur. The most common cause of failed Bloom filter pushdown: the join key is not the partition key, so DPP cannot activate. At AlpineEnergy, the CI test suite verifies Bloom filter pushdown for the 5 most expensive join queries.

Joins at terabyte scale face constraints that smaller joins do not: the hash table for a 500 GB build side does not fit in memory (hash spill to disk), the sort for a 1 TB sort-merge side requires multiple merge passes (external sort), and the network transfer for a shuffled join input can saturate the cluster's bandwidth (shuffle bottleneck). These constraints shift the optimization focus from choosing the right join algorithm to managing the physical resources — memory, disk, and network — that the algorithm requires. This lesson covers the resource planning and configuration decisions for joins on tables exceeding 1 TB.
PostgreSQL: Parallel Hash Join at Scale

PostgreSQL's parallel hash join (PostgreSQL 11+) distributes the hash table build across multiple workers: each worker hashes a portion of the build side, and the resulting shared hash table is probed by all workers in parallel. For a 500 GB build side with 4 workers, each worker hashes ~125 GB. The total hash table must fit in memory (work_mem × workers) or it spills to disk — and hash spill makes the join 5-10x slower. At AlpineEnergy, the quarterly historical archive join processes a 500 GB build side. With work_mem = 512 MB and 4 workers, the total available memory is 2 GB — far less than the 500 GB build. The join spills extensively. The solution: pre-filter the build side to the relevant subset (usually 1-5% of the archive), reducing the hash table to ~25 GB, and increase work_mem to 8 GB for the session (SET LOCAL work_mem = '8GB'). The filtered, high-work_mem configuration completes in 18 minutes; the unfiltered default completes in 4.5 hours.
Spark SQL: Sort-Merge at Scale

Spark SQL's sort-merge join sorts both sides by the join key and merges them. The sort happens within each partition, and partitions are processed independently across executors. The bottleneck is the shuffle: both sides must be repartitioned by the join key, transferring data across the network. For a 1 TB fact table joined with a 500 GB dimension, the shuffle moves 1.5 TB across the cluster. At AlpineEnergy's Spark cluster (10 nodes, 10 Gbps network), the theoretical shuffle throughput is 12.5 GB/s — so the 1.5 TB shuffle takes approximately 2 minutes in the best case. In practice, garbage collection pauses, disk spill during sort, and executor scheduling overhead increase this to 8-12 minutes. The external shuffle service (spark.shuffle.service.enabled = true) decouples shuffle data from executor lifecycle, preventing data loss when dynamic allocation deallocates executors.
Reducing Join Scale

-- Strategy 1: Pre-filter before join (reduce both sides)
-- Instead of: JOIN full_readings WITH full_meters
-- Do: JOIN filtered_readings WITH filtered_meters

-- Strategy 2: Pre-aggregate before join (reduce fact side)
-- Instead of: JOIN 37.2M daily readings WITH meters
-- Do: JOIN 1.2M monthly meter totals WITH meters

-- Strategy 3: Partition-aligned join (avoid shuffle)
-- If both tables are partitioned by the same key and partition count,
-- the join processes each partition pair locally — no network shuffle
-- Spark SQL: both tables bucketed by meter_id with 256 buckets
-- PostgreSQL: both tables partitioned by date with aligned boundaries

The three strategies reduce join scale at different levels. Pre-filtering reduces the data before the join (effective when the join is preceded by a highly selective filter). Pre-aggregation reduces the fact side's row count by aggregating to a coarser grain before joining with dimensions (effective for sum/count aggregations that do not need per-row detail). Partition alignment eliminates the shuffle entirely by co-locating matching data (effective for repeatedly joined tables — the alignment cost is paid once at partitioning time, and every subsequent join is local). At AlpineEnergy, all three strategies are used: the quarterly archive join uses pre-filtering, the billing pipeline uses pre-aggregation, and the ML feature pipeline uses Spark SQL bucketing for partition-aligned joins on meter_id.

blocks.title: The cheapest join is the join you do not execute
blocks.content: Every optimization in this module — materialized views, pre-aggregation, partition alignment, Bloom filter pushdown — reduces the amount of data the join operator must process. The most effective optimization eliminates the join entirely: the materialized enrichment view (lesson 5) pre-computes the join, and consumers read the result without any join at query time. When designing a TB+ pipeline, the first question is not 'which join algorithm?' — it is 'can this join be materialized, pre-aggregated, or eliminated?'

This module covered join architecture across both engines at enterprise scale: topology analysis for classifying multi-table join patterns, cross-engine federation with the replicate/federate/materialize decision framework, temporal joins with pre-resolution and bucketed optimization, join elimination through constraint-enabled view design, materialized enrichment views as join pre-computation for platform-wide compute reduction, architectural skew prevention through key redesign and pre-aggregation, multi-hop optimization with materialization boundaries, semi-join pushdown with Bloom filter verification, and resource planning for terabyte-scale joins. This final lesson codifies these into AlpineEnergy's join architecture playbook — the decision tree and audit process that governs every multi-table pipeline.
The Join Decision Tree

blocks.title: AlpineEnergy join architecture decision tree
blocks.content:
New multi-table pipeline
  │
  ├── Is this join executed by 3+ consumers?
  │     YES → Materialize as enrichment view (lesson 5)
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  ├── Does the join cross engine boundaries?
  │     YES → Replicate (slow-change dim) / Federate (fast-change) / Materialize (complex)
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  ├── Is the join temporal (SCD Type 2, point-in-time)?
  │     YES → Pre-resolve at load time (billing) / Bucket temporal (regulatory ad-hoc)
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  ├── Does the join chain 5+ tables?
  │     YES → Insert materialization boundary at max-reduction point
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  ├── Is there measurable skew (> 2x median task duration)?
  │     YES → Key redesign / Pre-aggregate / AQE skew split (if marginal)
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  └── Standard join: let the optimizer choose
        Star topology → broadcast dimensions
        Chain topology → sort-merge with partition alignment if possible
blocks.caption: The decision tree is evaluated top-to-bottom. The first matching branch determines the join strategy. Each branch references the lesson that covers the pattern in depth. The tree is reviewed during the join architecture audit for every new multi-table pipeline.

blocks.content: The Join Architecture Audit

Every multi-table pipeline at AlpineEnergy undergoes a join architecture audit before production deployment. The audit checklist has seven items: the topology is classified (star, chain, or cross-engine) and documented in the pipeline's ADR; the decision tree has been evaluated and the chosen strategy justified; cross-engine joins have pushdown verification tests; temporal joins specify the resolution strategy (pre-resolved, bucketed, or query-time) with retroactive correction cost documented; multi-hop pipelines with 5+ tables have materialization boundaries with ANALYZE between steps; skew analysis has been performed on the primary join keys; and the performance baseline has been recorded in the performance_log for regression detection.
The Extensibility Phase Complete

Modules 4 and 5 form the extensibility phase. Module 4 covered how data is physically combined across the platform: the topology analysis, cross-engine patterns, temporal resolution, materialization strategies, and skew prevention that determine join performance at scale. Module 5 covers how data is governed across organizational boundaries: schema registries, data contracts, breaking change negotiation, contract testing, and the cross-engine type mapping that ensures data integrity when tables move between PostgreSQL and Spark SQL. Together, these modules address the two defining challenges of a multi-team, multi-engine platform: making data combine correctly (joins) and making data evolve safely (schemas).

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 5 shifts from physical data combination to organizational data governance. The schema registries and data contracts from Module 5 protect the joins from Module 4: a schema change that renames a join key column breaks every pipeline that joins on it. The contract system ensures that producers cannot change join-critical columns without coordinating with every consumer — the organizational complement to the technical join architecture. The cross-engine type mapping addresses the subtle correctness problem: a NUMERIC(18,4) in PostgreSQL becomes a DecimalType(18,4) in Spark SQL, but the rounding behavior differs — and a billing pipeline that loses precision at the engine boundary produces wrong totals.

modules.title: Schema Registries and Data Contracts
modules.description: Implement cross-team schema governance at enterprise scale — schema registries, producer-consumer contracts, breaking change negotiation across organizational and national boundaries, contract testing, and the processes that prevent schema changes from breaking downstream pipelines.

blocks.content: The mid-level track's deployment module (Module 7) taught the deprecation protocol: when a column is renamed, the producing team announces the change, adds the new column alongside the old one, waits 2 weeks for consumers to migrate, then drops the old column. At HydroAlpes with 3 teams, this worked — Lena posted in the team Slack channel, Markus and the analytics lead acknowledged, and the migration completed in a week. At AlpineEnergy with 10 teams across 3 countries, the same rename broke 4 pipelines: the Italian DE team did not see the Slack announcement (they use a different channel), the Austrian analytics team acknowledged but missed the deadline (their sprint was fully committed), and two ML pipelines had hardcoded column references in feature engineering notebooks that nobody knew about. The deprecation protocol failed not because it was wrong, but because it assumed a communication topology that does not exist at 10-team scale.
Why Informal Governance Fails

Informal schema governance — Slack announcements, verbal agreements, email threads — has three failure modes at enterprise scale. First, notification asymmetry: the announcement reaches the teams that the producer knows about, not the teams that actually consume the table. A notebook-based consumer that reads the table directly (without registering as a consumer in any system) is invisible to the producer. Second, commitment asymmetry: acknowledging a change notice is not the same as implementing the migration — teams with full sprint commitments defer the migration until it becomes an incident. Third, discovery failure: when a new team member joins and writes a query against a table, they have no way to know which columns are stable, which are deprecated, and which are about to change. Formal schema governance replaces informal coordination with machine-enforced contracts.
What Formal Governance Provides

A formal schema governance system has four components. The registry: a version-controlled repository of every table's declared schema, stored as machine-readable YAML alongside the migration scripts. The contract: a formal agreement between each producer-consumer pair specifying the columns the consumer depends on, the compatibility guarantees, and the change notification protocol. The enforcement: CI hooks that validate every schema change against the registry and block changes that violate contracts. The evolution policy: rules that classify changes (additive, breaking, type-widening) and specify the required process for each class. This module builds all four components and shows how they interact at AlpineEnergy's scale.

blocks.title: Schema governance is organizational infrastructure, not tooling
blocks.content: The registry is a Git repository. The contracts are YAML files. The enforcement is a CI hook. None of these tools are novel. The value is in the process they enforce: every schema change is reviewed against every consumer contract before it is applied. The tooling makes the process automatic and auditable. Without the process, the tooling is unused configuration files. Without the tooling, the process depends on human compliance. AlpineEnergy needs both — the process defines what must happen, and the tooling ensures it does.

A schema registry is a centralized store of every table's declared schema: the columns, their types, their constraints, and their semantic descriptions. The registry serves three audiences: producers use it to declare what their tables look like, consumers use it to discover and depend on specific columns, and the CI system uses it to validate that schema changes do not violate consumer dependencies. The architectural choice is where to store the registry and how to version it. Three approaches exist: a catalog-based registry (stored in the database's own information_schema), a file-based registry (YAML or JSON files in a Git repository), and a dedicated registry service (a standalone API). Each has different trade-offs for versioning, cross-engine support, and operational complexity.
The Git-Based Registry

# File: schema-registry/alpineenergy_ch/silver/meter_readings.yml
schema:
  catalog: alpineenergy_ch
  schema_name: silver
  table_name: meter_readings
  engine: postgresql
  spark_mirror: alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings
  version: 14
  last_modified: 2025-11-15
  owner: swiss-de

columns:
  - name: meter_id
    type: VARCHAR(20)
    nullable: false
    description: Unique meter identifier (EEDA standard)
    classification: pii  # affects masking policy

  - name: daily_kw
    type: DOUBLE PRECISION
    nullable: false
    description: Daily energy consumption in kilowatts
    valid_range: [0, 5000]

  - name: date
    type: DATE
    nullable: false
    description: Measurement date
    partition_key: true

  - name: region
    type: VARCHAR(30)
    nullable: false
    description: Geographic sub-region identifier
    foreign_key: silver.regions.region_id

  - name: customer_type
    type: VARCHAR(20)
    nullable: false
    description: Customer classification (RESIDENTIAL, COMMERCIAL, INDUSTRIAL)
    scd_type: 2  # tracked via dim_meters, not changed in this table

  - name: loaded_at
    type: TIMESTAMPTZ
    nullable: false
    description: Pipeline ingestion timestamp
    default: CURRENT_TIMESTAMP

The YAML file declares the table's complete schema: every column with its type, nullability, description, and semantic annotations (PII classification, valid range, partition key, foreign key, SCD type). The version field increments on every schema change. The file lives in a Git repository alongside the migration scripts — changes to both are reviewed in the same PR. At AlpineEnergy, the schema registry repository contains 320 YAML files (one per table across both engines), organized in the same catalog/schema/table hierarchy as the namespace convention (Module 1, lesson 3). Git provides versioning (every change is a commit with author, timestamp, and diff), auditability (the complete change history is preserved), and review (changes require PR approval).
Cross-Engine Registry

AlpineEnergy's dual-engine architecture requires the registry to describe tables in both PostgreSQL and Spark SQL. The spark_mirror field in the YAML links the PostgreSQL table to its Delta Lake counterpart — enabling the CI system to validate that both schemas are aligned. When the PostgreSQL schema changes (a column is added), the CI hook checks whether the Spark SQL mirror has the corresponding column. If not, the PR is annotated with a warning: 'The Spark SQL mirror alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings is missing the new column. Add a migration for the Delta table or document why the column is PostgreSQL-only.' This cross-engine validation prevents the silent schema divergence that accumulates when changes are applied to one engine but not the other.
When NOT to Use a Schema Registry

A schema registry adds maintenance overhead: every table needs a YAML file, every schema change requires updating the YAML alongside the migration, and the CI validation adds seconds to every PR. For a platform with fewer than 20 tables and fewer than 3 teams, the overhead may exceed the governance benefit — the information_schema itself serves as a sufficient registry, and informal Slack-based change coordination works. At AlpineEnergy, the registry was introduced when the table count exceeded 100 and the third team (Italian DE) was onboarded. The first 6 months with 2 teams and 60 tables used information_schema queries and Slack announcements — which was adequate at that scale.

blocks.title: Start with YAML in Git, not a dedicated service
blocks.content: A dedicated schema registry service (like Confluent Schema Registry for Kafka) adds operational infrastructure: a service to deploy, monitor, and upgrade. A Git-based registry adds only files to an existing repository. The Git approach provides 90% of the governance value (versioning, review, CI validation) with near-zero operational overhead. AlpineEnergy evaluated a dedicated registry service and concluded that the Git-based approach was sufficient for 320 tables and 10 teams. The service alternative would be reconsidered if the table count exceeds 1,000 or if programmatic schema querying (not just CI validation) becomes a frequent need.

The schema registry declares what a table looks like. A data contract declares what a consumer depends on. The distinction matters: a table with 12 columns may have a consumer that depends on only 3 of them. If the producer renames a column that no consumer uses, the contract system knows it is safe — no consumer dependency is violated. If the producer renames a column that 5 consumers depend on, the contract system knows exactly which consumers are affected and blocks the change until all 5 acknowledge the migration plan. Contracts make the producer-consumer dependency graph explicit and machine-readable, replacing the implicit 'someone might be using this column' assumption with a precise 'these 5 consumers depend on this column.'
The Contract Format

# File: contracts/austrian_analytics__meter_readings.yml
contract:
  consumer: austrian-analytics
  consumer_pipeline: at_dashboard_refresh
  producer_table: alpineenergy_ch.silver.meter_readings
  producer_team: swiss-de
  registered: 2025-03-15

depends_on:
  columns:
    - meter_id      # used as join key
    - daily_kw      # aggregated in dashboard
    - date          # filtered in WHERE clause
    - region        # grouped in dashboard
  constraints:
    - meter_id IS NOT NULL
    - daily_kw >= 0
    - date IS NOT NULL

sla:
  freshness: 120 minutes  # data must be no more than 2 hours old
  completeness: 99.5%     # at least 99.5% of expected rows present

notification:
  channel: '#austrian-analytics-data'
  escalation: thomas.gruber@alpineenergy.at

The contract declares that the Austrian analytics team's dashboard pipeline depends on 4 specific columns from the Swiss meter_readings table, with specific constraint expectations (non-null meter_id, non-negative daily_kw) and SLA expectations (freshness within 2 hours, completeness above 99.5%). The producer (Swiss DE team) is obligated to maintain these guarantees. If the producer wants to rename region to sub_region, the CI hook identifies this contract, determines that region is a dependency, and blocks the PR with: 'Breaking change: column region is depended on by austrian-analytics (contract: austrian_analytics__meter_readings.yml). Deprecation protocol required.' The producer must negotiate the change with the Austrian analytics team before proceeding.
Contract Registration

At AlpineEnergy, contract registration is part of the onboarding process for new consumer pipelines. When a team writes a pipeline that reads from another team's table, they create a contract YAML file, submit it as a PR to the schema registry repository, and the producing team reviews and approves it. The approval confirms that the producer acknowledges the dependency and accepts the SLA obligation. Without a registered contract, the consumer has no formal claim on the table's schema or freshness — the producer can change it without notice. This creates a strong incentive to register: unregistered consumers discover breaking changes through pipeline failures instead of through coordinated migrations.
Contract Lifecycle

Contracts are not permanent. Consumer pipelines are decommissioned, column dependencies change as queries are refactored, and SLA requirements evolve. At AlpineEnergy, contracts are reviewed annually by both the producing and consuming teams. Stale contracts (where the consumer pipeline no longer exists or no longer references the declared columns) are deprecated and removed. The annual review also identifies unregistered consumers — pipelines that read the table without a contract — by comparing the contract registry against the query log (pg_stat_statements shows which roles query which tables). Unregistered consumers are asked to register or accept that they have no schema stability guarantee.

blocks.title: A contract is only as strong as its enforcement
blocks.content: A contract YAML file that the CI system does not check is documentation, not governance. At AlpineEnergy, the CI hook is the enforcement mechanism: it reads every contract that references the changed table, checks whether the change violates any contract dependency, and blocks the PR if a violation is detected. The hook runs on every PR to the schema registry and every PR to the migration repository. Without the hook, the contract is a gentleman's agreement — useful for documentation but insufficient for preventing breaking changes at 10-team scale.

The mid-level deprecation protocol (Module 7, lesson 4) defined a 2-week add-migrate-remove cycle for breaking changes. At AlpineEnergy, the 2-week cycle is insufficient for two reasons. First, 10 teams have different sprint cadences — the Swiss DE team runs 2-week sprints, the Austrian analytics team runs 3-week sprints, and the Italian DE team runs a kanban flow. A 2-week migration window does not align with any of these cadences. Second, cross-country changes require regulatory review: renaming a column that appears in the Swiss regulatory filing triggers a compliance review by the legal team, which has a 4-week SLA. The enterprise deprecation protocol extends the mid-level pattern with formal change requests, automated impact analysis, and team-specific migration windows.
The Change Request Process

A breaking change at AlpineEnergy follows a five-step process. Step 1 (Propose): the producing team files a Schema Change Request (SCR) in the governance repository — a Markdown document that describes the change, its rationale, the affected columns, and the proposed timeline. Step 2 (Impact): the CI system automatically runs the impact analysis — querying every contract that references the affected columns and generating a list of affected consumers, their SLA tiers, and their notification channels. Step 3 (Notify): automated notifications are sent to every affected consumer's channel with the SCR details and the proposed timeline. Step 4 (Negotiate): affected consumers acknowledge or request a timeline extension. The producing team adjusts the timeline to accommodate the longest consumer migration. Step 5 (Execute): the add-migrate-remove cycle runs with the negotiated timeline, tracked in the SCR document.
Automated Impact Analysis

-- Impact analysis: find all contracts that depend on the changed column
-- This query powers the CI hook that runs on every SCR
WITH changed_columns AS (
  -- The SCR specifies which columns are being changed
  VALUES ('region'), ('customer_type')
),
affected_contracts AS (
  SELECT c.consumer,
         c.consumer_pipeline,
         c.producer_table,
         cc.column1 AS affected_column,
         c.sla_tier,
         c.notification_channel
  FROM   platform.contracts AS c
    CROSS JOIN changed_columns AS cc
  WHERE  c.producer_table = 'alpineenergy_ch.silver.meter_readings'
    AND  cc.column1 = ANY(c.depends_on_columns)
)
SELECT affected_column,
       COUNT(DISTINCT consumer) AS affected_teams,
       COUNT(DISTINCT consumer_pipeline) AS affected_pipelines,
       string_agg(DISTINCT consumer, ', ') AS team_list,
       MAX(sla_tier) AS highest_sla_tier
FROM   affected_contracts
GROUP BY affected_column
ORDER BY affected_teams DESC;

affected_column | affected_teams | affected_pipelines | team_list                              | highest_sla_tier
----------------+----------------+--------------------+----------------------------------------+-----------------
region          |              6 |                  9 | austrian-analytics, italian-de, ml-... | platinum
customer_type   |              4 |                  5 | swiss-billing, austrian-analytics, ... | platinum

The impact analysis reveals that renaming region affects 6 teams and 9 pipelines, with the highest-affected SLA tier being platinum (billing). Renaming customer_type affects 4 teams and 5 pipelines, also at platinum tier. The platinum tier means the change requires a minimum 4-week migration window (the platinum SLA for change coordination). If the highest tier were silver, a 2-week window would suffice. The automated analysis replaces the manual effort of identifying affected consumers — which at 10-team scale is both time-consuming and error-prone. At AlpineEnergy, the impact analysis has identified 3 previously unknown consumers (unregistered notebook queries) that manual analysis would have missed.
The Escalation Path

When a consumer team cannot migrate within the proposed timeline, the escalation path provides structured resolution. First, the producing team evaluates whether the change can be deferred to accommodate the consumer's sprint cycle. If not, the consumer team evaluates whether a temporary compatibility shim (a view that maps old names to new names) can bridge the gap. If neither works, the governance committee (Module 10, lesson 4) arbitrates — balancing the producer's need for the change against the consumer's capacity constraint. At AlpineEnergy, the governance committee has arbitrated 4 breaking changes in 18 months. In 3 cases, the producer deferred the change by one sprint. In 1 case, the committee mandated that the consumer allocate a developer to the migration because the change was regulatory-required and could not be deferred.

blocks.title: Most breaking changes are avoidable
blocks.content: Of the 12 breaking changes proposed at AlpineEnergy in 18 months, 8 were column renames (cosmetic, no semantic change), 2 were type changes (precision widening), and 2 were column removals (unused columns). The 8 renames could have been avoided by choosing descriptive column names at design time. The governance committee's retrospective concluded: invest 30 minutes in column naming during table design to avoid 40+ hours of cross-team coordination during column renames. The schema registry now includes a naming review as part of the new-table approval process.

Contracts declare dependencies. Contract tests verify that the declaration matches reality. A producer-side test verifies that the table's actual schema matches the declared schema in the registry (columns exist, types match, constraints are present). A consumer-side test verifies that the consumer's pipeline handles the declared schema correctly (the query parses, the join keys match, the expected columns are present in the output). Together, both sides verify that the contract is honored — not just declared. At AlpineEnergy, contract tests run on every PR that modifies a migration script, a schema YAML file, or a pipeline query file.
Producer-Side Contract Tests

-- Producer-side test: verify the actual schema matches the registry declaration
-- Runs after every migration is applied to the test database
WITH declared AS (
  -- Loaded from the YAML registry file by the test harness
  VALUES
    ('meter_id', 'character varying', false),
    ('daily_kw', 'double precision', false),
    ('date', 'date', false),
    ('region', 'character varying', false),
    ('customer_type', 'character varying', false),
    ('loaded_at', 'timestamp with time zone', false)
),
actual AS (
  SELECT column_name, data_type,
         (is_nullable = 'YES') AS nullable
  FROM   information_schema.columns
  WHERE  table_schema = 'silver' AND table_name = 'meter_readings'
)
SELECT d.column1 AS declared_column,
       d.column2 AS declared_type,
       a.data_type AS actual_type,
       CASE
         WHEN a.column_name IS NULL THEN 'MISSING COLUMN'
         WHEN a.data_type <> d.column2 THEN 'TYPE MISMATCH'
         WHEN a.nullable <> d.column3 THEN 'NULLABILITY MISMATCH'
       END AS violation
FROM   declared AS d(column1, column2, column3)
  LEFT JOIN actual AS a ON d.column1 = a.column_name
WHERE  a.column_name IS NULL
   OR  a.data_type <> d.column2
   OR  a.nullable <> d.column3;

The test compares the declared schema (from the YAML registry) against the actual schema (from information_schema) and reports any mismatches. A MISSING COLUMN violation means the migration dropped or renamed a column that the registry still declares. A TYPE MISMATCH means the migration changed a column's type without updating the registry. A NULLABILITY MISMATCH means a NOT NULL constraint was dropped or added without registry update. The test runs in CI after applying all migrations to a fresh test database — catching schema drift before it reaches staging or production. At AlpineEnergy, this test has caught 7 registry-migration mismatches in 12 months — all caused by engineers who modified the migration but forgot to update the YAML.
Consumer-Side Contract Tests

Consumer-side tests verify that the consumer's pipeline works against the declared schema. The test loads the registry's schema definition into a test database, populates the table with fixture data that satisfies the declared constraints, and runs the consumer's pipeline query against it. If the query succeeds and produces the expected output shape (correct columns, correct types, non-empty result), the consumer's pipeline is compatible with the declared schema. At AlpineEnergy, consumer-side tests are owned by the consuming team and run in the consuming team's CI pipeline — not in the producer's pipeline. This separation ensures that each team is responsible for verifying their own compatibility.
The Bidirectional Contract Test Suite

The complete contract test suite runs bidirectionally on every schema change PR. The producer-side test verifies that the new schema matches the updated registry. The consumer-side tests (for every registered contract) verify that the consumer pipelines still work against the new schema. If any consumer-side test fails, the PR is blocked — the schema change would break that consumer. This bidirectional testing is the enforcement mechanism that makes contracts more than documentation: a breaking change that passes the impact analysis notification but fails a consumer-side test is still blocked. The test does not care whether the consumer acknowledged the change — it cares whether the consumer's code is compatible.

blocks.title: Consumer-side tests must be maintained by the consumer
blocks.content: If the consuming team does not maintain their contract test, it becomes stale — testing against a query that no longer matches the actual pipeline. A stale test produces false positives (passing when the pipeline would actually break) or false negatives (failing when the pipeline would actually work). At AlpineEnergy, the annual contract review includes a test freshness check: does the consumer-side test use the same query that the actual pipeline uses? Stale tests are flagged for immediate update. Teams with untestable pipelines (ad-hoc notebook queries) must either convert them to testable scripts or accept that their contract has no enforcement.

Not every schema change is a breaking change. Adding a column with a default value is backward-compatible — existing consumer queries continue to work because they do not reference the new column. Widening a type (INTEGER to BIGINT) is forward-compatible — existing data fits in the new type, and consumers that expect INTEGER will still receive valid values. Renaming or removing a column is a breaking change — existing consumer queries that reference the old name will fail. Schema evolution strategies classify changes into compatibility categories and define the process required for each, enabling additive changes to proceed quickly while breaking changes follow the full negotiation protocol.
The Compatibility Classification

rows: Add column with DEFAULT or NULL
ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN quality_score DOUBLE PRECISION DEFAULT 0
Registry update + PR review. No consumer notification required.
ALTER TABLE ALTER COLUMN meter_id TYPE VARCHAR(30)
Registry update + consumer notification. No migration deadline.
ALTER TABLE ALTER COLUMN daily_kw TYPE REAL
Full SCR + impact analysis + negotiated migration timeline.
ALTER TABLE RENAME COLUMN region TO sub_region
Full SCR + add/migrate/remove cycle with negotiated timeline.
Full SCR. Verify zero contract dependencies before dropping.
ALTER TABLE ALTER COLUMN quality_score SET NOT NULL
SCR + verify all upstream sources provide non-null values.

blocks.content: The classification creates a fast path for non-breaking changes: adding a nullable column requires only a registry update and a PR review — no consumer notification, no migration window, no governance committee. This fast path handles approximately 70% of schema changes at AlpineEnergy (most changes are additive: new columns for new features, new metrics, new audit fields). The remaining 30% are breaking changes that require the full SCR process. The classification is encoded in the CI hook: the hook compares the old and new YAML files, determines the compatibility class, and applies the appropriate review requirements automatically.
AlpineEnergy's Evolution Policy

AlpineEnergy's default policy is backward-compatible evolution: producers may add columns freely but may not rename, remove, or narrow-type existing columns without the SCR process. Forward-compatible changes (type widening) are permitted with consumer notification but without a formal migration deadline — consumers are informed but not required to change their code. This policy reflects the reality that most consumers are more fragile than most producers: a producer can add a column in seconds, but a consumer whose GROUP BY or JOIN references a removed column requires a code change, a test update, and a deployment. The asymmetry justifies a policy that optimizes for consumer stability.

blocks.title: Encode the policy in CI, not in documentation
blocks.content: The evolution policy is effective only if the CI system enforces it. At AlpineEnergy, the CI hook reads the old and new YAML files, classifies the change, and applies the rules: backward-compatible changes are auto-approved for merge. Forward-compatible changes are approved with a notification comment. Breaking changes are blocked until the SCR document is linked in the PR description. The hook has prevented 11 uncoordinated breaking changes in 12 months — changes that would have proceeded directly to production if the enforcement were manual.

AlpineEnergy's dual-engine architecture means that many tables exist in both PostgreSQL and Spark SQL. The Swiss meter_readings table is the source of truth in PostgreSQL and is replicated to Delta Lake for analytical consumption. The schemas should be identical — but they are not, because the two engines have different type systems. PostgreSQL's NUMERIC(18,4) maps to Spark SQL's DecimalType(18,4), but the rounding behavior differs for certain edge cases. PostgreSQL's TIMESTAMPTZ stores timestamps in UTC and converts on display; Spark SQL's TimestampType has no timezone awareness by default. PostgreSQL's VARCHAR(20) maps to Spark SQL's StringType — but StringType has no length constraint, so a value that is valid in Spark SQL may violate the PostgreSQL constraint when written back.
The Canonical Type Mapping

rows: Rounding behavior differs for division results
Cast to NUMERIC in PostgreSQL queries; test rounding at boundary
Spark has no length constraint — over-length values possible
Add CHECK constraint validation in CDC pipeline before PostgreSQL write
Spark loses timezone; UTC assumed but not enforced
Store as UTC in both engines; validate timezone on cross-engine transfer
Spark has no native JSON type; validation lost
Validate JSON structure in Spark SQL pipeline before PostgreSQL write

blocks.content: The canonical type mapping is stored in the schema registry as a reference document and enforced by the CDC pipeline that replicates data between engines. When the CDC pipeline writes a Spark SQL StringType value to a PostgreSQL VARCHAR(20) column, it validates the length before the INSERT — rejecting values that exceed the PostgreSQL constraint rather than letting the INSERT fail with a database error. At AlpineEnergy, the type mapping has prevented 3 data truncation incidents (Spark SQL produced strings longer than the PostgreSQL VARCHAR limit) and 1 precision incident (a DecimalType division produced a value with more decimal places than the NUMERIC precision allowed).
The Cross-Engine Validation Pipeline

The cross-engine validation runs as a step in the CDC pipeline (Module 9). After replicating data from PostgreSQL to Delta Lake, the pipeline compares the row counts and checksums of key columns to verify that the replication did not introduce data quality issues. After writing Spark SQL results back to PostgreSQL, the pipeline validates type constraints (VARCHAR length, NUMERIC precision, NOT NULL) before the INSERT — quarantining rows that violate PostgreSQL constraints rather than letting them fail. At AlpineEnergy, the cross-engine validation adds approximately 15 seconds to the CDC pipeline — negligible overhead compared to the hours of debugging that a type mismatch incident would require.

blocks.title: Type mapping bugs are billing bugs
blocks.content: A NUMERIC(18,4) value of 142.3567 in PostgreSQL becomes 142.3567 as DecimalType(18,4) in Spark SQL. If the Spark SQL pipeline divides this value and produces 7.17835, and the result is written back to PostgreSQL as NUMERIC(18,4), the value is rounded to 7.1784 — losing the final digit. If this value is a billing rate, the rounding difference multiplied across 5 million meters produces a billing error that is individually invisible (sub-cent per meter) but collectively significant (thousands of EUR per month). AlpineEnergy's policy: every cross-engine data flow that involves NUMERIC/Decimal arithmetic includes a precision reconciliation test that compares aggregated values across engines.

The contract system tells you which consumers depend on which columns. Column-level lineage tells you where those columns come from — which upstream tables, transformations, and pipelines produced the values in each column. When the Swiss billing team proposes renaming region to sub_region in the Silver meter_readings table, the impact analysis (lesson 4) identifies the 6 downstream consumers. But where does the region column come from? If it is derived from the Bronze ingestion (parsed from the source CSV), the rename must be coordinated with the ingestion pipeline. If it is enriched from the regions reference table (looked up during Silver processing), the rename must be coordinated with the regions table owner. Column-level lineage traces the provenance from consumer to producer through every transformation step.
Extracting Lineage from SQL

-- PostgreSQL: extract column-level dependencies from view definitions
-- This shows which base table columns each view column depends on
SELECT v.table_name    AS view_name,
       vc.column_name   AS view_column,
       dc.table_name    AS source_table,
       dc.column_name   AS source_column
FROM   information_schema.view_column_usage AS vc
  JOIN information_schema.views AS v
    ON vc.view_schema = v.table_schema AND vc.view_name = v.table_name
  JOIN information_schema.columns AS dc
    ON vc.table_name = dc.table_name AND vc.column_name = dc.column_name
WHERE  v.table_schema = 'gold'
ORDER BY v.table_name, vc.column_name;

PostgreSQL's information_schema.view_column_usage tracks which base table columns each view column depends on. For direct column references (SELECT mr.region FROM meter_readings mr), the mapping is one-to-one. For computed columns (SELECT mr.daily_kw * 0.001 AS daily_mwh), the mapping traces back to the source column (daily_kw). For joined views, the mapping shows cross-table dependencies. This catalog-based lineage is limited to views — it does not trace lineage through pipeline queries that INSERT INTO ... SELECT. At AlpineEnergy, the view-based lineage covers the Gold and Platinum layers (which are defined as views or materialized views), and the pipeline-based lineage is extracted by parsing the INSERT ... SELECT statements in the migration repository.
The Lineage Graph

The complete lineage graph connects every column in the platform: from the Bronze CSV source columns, through the Silver validation pipeline, through the Gold business transformations, to the Platinum cross-domain views and the consumer-facing dashboards. At AlpineEnergy, the lineage graph is stored as a table in the platform schema: platform.column_lineage with columns (target_table, target_column, source_table, source_column, transformation_type). The graph is rebuilt nightly by parsing the SQL in every migration and pipeline script. The impact analysis query (lesson 4) joins against this graph to trace upstream provenance: 'this column rename in Silver affects these Gold views which affect these Platinum tables which affect these dashboard queries.' The full upstream + downstream trace is the complete impact picture for any schema change.
When NOT to Invest in Column-Level Lineage

Column-level lineage extraction requires parsing SQL — which is complex, fragile (query refactoring can break the parser), and incomplete (dynamic SQL, UDFs, and cross-engine queries are difficult to parse). Table-level lineage (which tables does each pipeline read from and write to) is much simpler and covers 80% of impact analysis needs. At AlpineEnergy, the platform team invested in column-level lineage only after 3 incidents where table-level lineage was insufficient — specifically, incidents where a column rename in Silver affected only 2 of the 8 Gold tables that read from it (because only 2 referenced the renamed column). If breaking changes are rare (< 5 per year), table-level lineage with manual column-level investigation is sufficient.

blocks.title: Table-level lineage first, column-level on demand
blocks.content: Table-level lineage (which pipelines read and write which tables) can be extracted from pipeline configuration files and dependency registries without any SQL parsing. It answers 80% of impact analysis questions: 'which pipelines are affected by this table's schema change?' Column-level lineage answers the remaining 20%: 'which of those pipelines actually reference the changed column?' At AlpineEnergy, table-level lineage was built in month 3 (from the dependency registry). Column-level lineage was built in month 14 (when the third breaking-change incident justified the investment).

The previous lessons defined the governance components: the registry, contracts, change process, evolution policy, type mapping, and lineage. This lesson covers the automation that ties them together into a system that runs without human intervention. At AlpineEnergy, the schema compliance system is implemented as a set of CI hooks, cron jobs, and monitoring queries that collectively enforce every governance rule — validating schemas against the registry, checking contracts before schema changes, verifying cross-engine alignment, and detecting unregistered consumers. The automation ensures that governance is not an optional practice that depends on human discipline but a mandatory gate that blocks non-compliant changes.
The CI Hook Architecture

# .github/workflows/schema-governance.yml (simplified)
name: Schema Governance
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'migrations/**'
      - 'schema-registry/**'
      - 'contracts/**'

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres: { image: 'postgres:16', ... }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Apply all migrations
        run: flyway migrate

      - name: Validate schema against registry
        run: python scripts/validate_registry.py
        # Compares information_schema against YAML declarations

      - name: Check contract compatibility
        run: python scripts/check_contracts.py
        # Identifies any contract dependencies violated by this change

      - name: Classify change and enforce policy
        run: python scripts/classify_change.py
        # Backward-compatible → auto-approve
        # Forward-compatible → approve with notification
        # Breaking → block until SCR linked

      - name: Cross-engine alignment check
        run: python scripts/check_cross_engine.py
        # Verifies Spark SQL mirror schema matches PostgreSQL

The CI workflow runs four validation steps on every PR that touches migrations, registry files, or contracts. Step 1 applies all migrations to a fresh database and validates the resulting schema against the registry YAML files. Step 2 checks every contract that references the changed table and identifies violated dependencies. Step 3 classifies the change (backward-compatible, forward-compatible, or breaking) and enforces the corresponding policy. Step 4 verifies that the Spark SQL mirror schema is aligned with the PostgreSQL schema. Each step produces a clear pass/fail result with diagnostic output. A failure at any step blocks the PR with a message explaining the violation and the remediation steps.
Drift Detection

CI hooks catch schema changes at PR time. Drift detection catches schema changes that bypass the PR process — direct ALTER TABLE commands in production, ad-hoc migrations applied by DBAs during incidents, or schema changes made by external tools (Spark SQL MERGE creating a new column via schema evolution). At AlpineEnergy, a daily drift detection job compares the production information_schema against the registry YAML files and reports any discrepancies. A discrepancy means the production schema has diverged from the declared schema — either the registry needs updating (the change was legitimate but not recorded) or the production schema needs reverting (the change was unauthorized). The drift detection job has caught 5 unauthorized changes in 12 months, all from ad-hoc DBA interventions during incidents that were not followed by registry updates.
Unregistered Consumer Detection

The contract system works only for registered consumers. Unregistered consumers — notebooks, ad-hoc queries, scripts that read directly from tables without a contract — are invisible to the impact analysis and will break silently when a schema changes. At AlpineEnergy, unregistered consumer detection queries pg_stat_statements for roles that accessed a table but do not have a registered contract. The query runs monthly and produces a report: 'These 12 roles queried silver.meter_readings without a registered contract.' The report is sent to team leads for action: register a contract (if the access is ongoing) or stop accessing the table (if it was a one-time ad-hoc query).

blocks.title: Automation is the enforcement, not the policy
blocks.content: The governance policy (evolution rules, change process, contract lifecycle) is documented in Markdown and reviewed by the governance committee. The automation (CI hooks, drift detection, consumer detection) enforces the policy mechanically. Changing the policy requires updating the documentation and the automation together — a policy change that is not reflected in the CI hooks creates a gap between what the team thinks is enforced and what is actually enforced. At AlpineEnergy, every policy change produces a paired PR: one to the governance documentation, one to the CI scripts.

This module built a complete schema governance system for a multi-engine, multi-team platform: the Git-based registry that declares every table's schema with semantic annotations, the contract system that makes producer-consumer dependencies explicit and machine-readable, the breaking change negotiation protocol with automated impact analysis, the bidirectional contract testing that verifies both producer and consumer compliance, the evolution policy that classifies changes by compatibility and enforces the appropriate process, the cross-engine type mapping that prevents precision and constraint mismatches, the column-level lineage graph for full upstream and downstream impact tracing, and the compliance automation that enforces every rule through CI hooks and drift detection.
The Governance Maturity Model

headers: When to Move to Next Stage
rows: When the 3rd team joins or the table count exceeds 50
Git-based YAML declarations, CI validation against information_schema
When the first breaking change incident occurs
Consumer dependency declarations, automated impact analysis
When cross-engine data flows begin or cross-team breaking changes exceed 2/quarter
Evolution policy, contract testing, drift detection, lineage, compliance automation

blocks.content: The maturity model reflects AlpineEnergy's actual evolution: Stage 1 for the first 6 months (2 teams, 60 tables), Stage 2 at month 7 (3rd team onboarded, first schema incident), Stage 3 at month 12 (Italian expansion, cross-engine flows), Stage 4 at month 16 (regulatory audit required full traceability). Each stage adds governance overhead — the investment is justified only when the scale or the incident rate demands it. A 2-team platform that adopts Stage 4 governance is over-engineering; a 10-team platform without Stage 3 contracts is under-governing.
The Extensibility Phase Complete

Modules 4 and 5 form the extensibility phase. Module 4 covered how data is physically combined: join topology analysis, cross-engine federation, temporal resolution, materialization strategies, and the join architecture that determines pipeline performance. Module 5 covered how data evolves safely: schema registries, contracts, change negotiation, evolution policies, and the compliance automation that prevents schema changes from breaking downstream consumers. Together, these modules address the two challenges of growing a platform: making data combine correctly across engines and teams (joins), and making data evolve without breaking the consumers who depend on it (schemas). The deep internals phase (Modules 6-7) goes inside the engines themselves.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 6 goes inside the PostgreSQL execution engine: MVCC, WAL, buffer management, VACUUM, lock internals, and index internals. These are the mechanisms beneath the optimizer decisions from Module 2 and the partition strategies from Module 3. Understanding them enables diagnosis of problems invisible from EXPLAIN alone: MVCC bloat that causes 40% storage inflation, transaction ID wraparound that threatens a forced database shutdown, lock storms that freeze concurrent queries, and buffer cache misses that make indexed queries slower than sequential scans. The platform architecture tells you where the data lives. The optimizer tells you how it moves. The engine internals tell you why it sometimes does not move at all.

modules.title: SQL Engine Internals
modules.description: Understand the PostgreSQL execution engine at the implementation level — process architecture, MVCC, WAL, buffer management, vacuum mechanics, lock internals, and index internals — enabling diagnosis of problems that are invisible from the EXPLAIN output alone.

blocks.content: The mid-level track treated PostgreSQL as a service: you send it SQL, it returns results. The connection pooler manages connections, the EXPLAIN output reveals the plan, and pg_stat_activity shows running queries. This surface-level understanding is sufficient for query tuning and pipeline operations. It is not sufficient for diagnosing problems like: why is the database consuming 40 GB of RAM when shared_buffers is set to 8 GB? Why does autovacuum run continuously but never finish on one specific table? Why did 10 concurrent queries complete in 2 seconds each but 50 concurrent queries take 30 seconds each? These questions require understanding the process architecture — the internal structure of the PostgreSQL server, how it manages memory, how it spawns workers, and how each component contributes to query execution and system health.
The Postmaster and Backend Processes

blocks.title: PostgreSQL process architecture
blocks.caption: The postmaster is the parent process that manages the cluster. Each client connection gets a dedicated backend process. Background workers handle maintenance: autovacuum, WAL writing, checkpointing, statistics collection, and background buffer writing. All processes communicate through shared memory: the buffer pool, WAL buffers, lock tables, and process state arrays.

blocks.content: Each client connection is handled by a dedicated backend process — a separate OS process with its own memory allocation, not a thread within a shared process. This process-per-connection model provides strong isolation (a crashed backend does not affect other connections) but has significant overhead: each backend consumes 5-10 MB of private memory for its query plan cache, sort buffers, and hash tables. At AlpineEnergy with 38 active connections (via PgBouncer), the backend memory overhead is approximately 300 MB. Without PgBouncer, the 200+ logical connections from 10 teams would consume 1.5 GB in backend overhead alone — which is why the mid-level track (Module 8, lesson 9) made connection pooling mandatory.
The Background Workers

Five background worker types run continuously. The autovacuum launcher monitors table modification rates and spawns autovacuum worker processes when a table's dead tuple count exceeds the threshold (lesson 5). The WAL writer flushes WAL buffers to disk at configurable intervals, ensuring durability without requiring each backend to perform its own fsync (lesson 3). The checkpointer periodically writes all dirty shared buffers to disk, creating a consistent recovery point (lesson 3). The stats collector aggregates per-table and per-function statistics from all backends into the pg_stat_* views that the monitoring queries rely on. The background writer evicts dirty buffers from the buffer pool during idle periods, reducing the eviction burden during peak query load.
Diagnosing Process Issues

-- View all PostgreSQL processes and their current activity
SELECT pid, usename, application_name,
       backend_type,  -- 'client backend', 'autovacuum worker', 'walwriter', etc.
       state,         -- 'active', 'idle', 'idle in transaction'
       wait_event_type, wait_event,
       backend_start,
       NOW() - query_start AS query_duration,
       LEFT(query, 80) AS query_preview
FROM   pg_stat_activity
ORDER BY backend_type, state, query_duration DESC NULLS LAST;

The pg_stat_activity view shows every process — backends, autovacuum workers, WAL writer, checkpointer. The backend_type column distinguishes client backends from system processes. The state column reveals idle connections (consuming memory but not executing queries) and 'idle in transaction' connections (holding locks without doing work — a common cause of lock contention). At AlpineEnergy, the operational dashboard monitors three process-level metrics: the count of 'idle in transaction' connections (threshold: > 5 triggers investigation), the count of autovacuum workers (threshold: if all autovacuum_max_workers slots are occupied, tables are competing for vacuum resources), and the checkpointer's checkpoint_write_time (threshold: > 30 seconds indicates I/O pressure from dirty buffer writes).

blocks.title: Every PostgreSQL problem is visible in the process model
blocks.content: Memory pressure manifests as backend processes consuming excessive private memory (sort spills) or shared buffers being evicted too frequently (low cache hit ratio). CPU pressure manifests as too many active backends competing for cores. I/O pressure manifests as the checkpointer or WAL writer spending excessive time on disk writes. Lock pressure manifests as backends in 'Lock' wait_event_type. The process architecture is the diagnostic entry point for every performance problem that is not visible in EXPLAIN. The remaining lessons in this module explore each subsystem in depth.

Multi-Version Concurrency Control is PostgreSQL's mechanism for allowing concurrent readers and writers without locking. When a transaction updates a row, PostgreSQL does not overwrite the row in place — it creates a new version of the row and marks the old version as dead. Readers see the version that was current when their transaction started (snapshot isolation), not the version being written by a concurrent transaction. This means readers never block writers and writers never block readers. The trade-off: dead row versions accumulate until VACUUM removes them, and every row carries versioning metadata (xmin, xmax, infomask) that consumes storage and affects scan performance. Understanding MVCC at the implementation level explains three production phenomena: table bloat (dead versions accumulating), index bloat (index entries pointing to dead versions), and the critical transaction ID wraparound problem.
Tuple Headers and Visibility

-- View the internal tuple versioning metadata
-- Requires the pageinspect extension
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pageinspect;

SELECT lp,          -- line pointer (position within page)
       t_xmin,       -- transaction ID that created this version
       t_xmax,       -- transaction ID that deleted/updated this version (0 = live)
       t_ctid,       -- current tuple ID (self-reference = latest version)
       t_infomask::bit(16) AS infomask  -- visibility flags
FROM   heap_page_items(get_raw_page('silver.meter_readings', 0))
LIMIT  5;

Each row version (tuple) carries a header with versioning metadata. The t_xmin field records the transaction that created this version. The t_xmax field records the transaction that invalidated it (by UPDATE or DELETE) — a value of 0 means the version is still live. The t_ctid field is a pointer to the next version of the same logical row: for a live tuple, it points to itself; for an updated tuple, it points to the new version. When a transaction reads the table, PostgreSQL checks each tuple's xmin and xmax against the transaction's snapshot to determine visibility: if xmin is committed and xmax is either 0 or not yet committed, the tuple is visible. This per-tuple visibility check is the core of MVCC — and it runs for every row in every scan.
MVCC Bloat

When a row is updated, the old version remains in the table until VACUUM removes it. If the table receives 100,000 updates per day and VACUUM runs nightly, approximately 100,000 dead tuples accumulate during the day. Each dead tuple consumes the same storage as a live tuple — the same page space, the same index entries. A table with 1 million live rows and 100,000 dead rows is 10% larger than necessary. At AlpineEnergy, the meter_status table (which tracks each meter's current operational status and is updated whenever a meter goes online/offline) accumulated 2.3 million dead tuples over 6 months because the autovacuum threshold was set too conservatively. The table grew from 850 MB (live data) to 1.2 GB (live + dead) — a 40% bloat that degraded sequential scan performance by 40% because every scan reads dead tuples and discards them.
Transaction ID Wraparound

PostgreSQL uses 32-bit transaction IDs, allowing approximately 4.2 billion transactions. The ID space wraps around: after reaching the maximum, the next transaction gets ID 3 (IDs 0-2 are reserved). Without intervention, a transaction with ID 3 billion would not be able to see rows created by transaction ID 1 billion — because the wraparound makes old transactions appear to be in the future. PostgreSQL prevents this by requiring VACUUM to 'freeze' old tuples: marking them as visible to all transactions regardless of their xmin. If VACUUM fails to keep up — because autovacuum is blocked, or the table is too large to vacuum within the maintenance window — PostgreSQL enters a protective shutdown state at 40 million transactions before the wraparound point. This shutdown is not a graceful degradation — it is a forced read-only mode that requires manual intervention to resolve.
