-- Monitor transaction ID age: how close is each table to the wraparound limit?
SELECT relname,
       age(relfrozenxid) AS xid_age,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(oid)) AS table_size,
       ROUND(age(relfrozenxid)::NUMERIC / 2000000000 * 100, 1) AS pct_to_wraparound
FROM   pg_class
WHERE  relkind = 'r' AND relnamespace = 'silver'::regnamespace
ORDER BY age(relfrozenxid) DESC
LIMIT  10;

The query shows each table's transaction ID age — how many transactions have occurred since the table was last frozen. A healthy table has an age under 200 million (10% of the warning threshold). At AlpineEnergy, the autovacuum_freeze_max_age is set to 200 million transactions, triggering an aggressive anti-wraparound vacuum when any table exceeds this threshold. The monitoring dashboard tracks the maximum xid_age across all tables with a warning at 150 million and a critical alert at 180 million. In 18 months of operation, no table has exceeded 120 million — but the monitoring exists because the consequence of reaching the limit is a forced database shutdown that would breach every SLA simultaneously.

blocks.title: Transaction ID wraparound is the most dangerous PostgreSQL failure mode
blocks.content: Most PostgreSQL failures are recoverable: a crashed backend is restarted, a corrupt index is rebuilt, a slow query is killed and retried. Transaction ID wraparound forces the entire database into read-only mode until a manual VACUUM completes — which on a terabyte table can take hours. At AlpineEnergy, the wraparound monitor is the single highest-priority monitoring alert. It is tested quarterly by artificially advancing a test database's transaction counter to verify that the alert fires at the configured threshold.

The Write-Ahead Log is PostgreSQL's durability mechanism: every change to the database is written to the WAL before it is written to the data files. If the server crashes, the WAL replay recovers the database to its last committed state by re-applying the logged changes to the data files. WAL is also the foundation of replication (streaming the WAL to a replica), point-in-time recovery (replaying the WAL to a specific timestamp), and CDC (decoding the WAL into change events via logical replication). Understanding WAL internals — the record format, the write path, the checkpoint cycle, and the configuration trade-offs — is necessary for tuning durability, replication lag, and recovery time.
The WAL Write Path

When a backend executes an INSERT, it writes the change to the WAL buffer (a shared memory region), then writes the row to the shared buffer pool. The WAL buffer is flushed to disk when a transaction commits (ensuring durability), when the buffer fills, or when the WAL writer background process runs (every wal_writer_delay, default 200ms). The critical point: the WAL is written sequentially to a single file, which is fast on all storage types. The data files are written randomly (scattered across table pages), which is slow on spinning disks and moderate on SSD. By writing the WAL first and the data files later (during checkpoints), PostgreSQL converts random writes into sequential writes — the fundamental performance trick of write-ahead logging.
Checkpoints

-- View checkpoint activity and timing
SELECT checkpoints_timed,       -- checkpoints triggered by checkpoint_timeout
       checkpoints_req,          -- checkpoints triggered by max_wal_size
       checkpoint_write_time,    -- time spent writing dirty buffers (ms)
       checkpoint_sync_time,     -- time spent syncing to disk (ms)
       buffers_checkpoint,       -- buffers written during checkpoints
       buffers_backend,          -- buffers written by backends (BAD if high)
       buffers_clean             -- buffers written by background writer
FROM   pg_stat_bgwriter;

A checkpoint writes all dirty shared buffers to their data files and records the WAL position — creating a recovery point. After a crash, PostgreSQL replays the WAL from the last checkpoint forward, which is faster than replaying from the beginning of the WAL. Checkpoints are triggered by time (checkpoint_timeout, default 5 minutes) or by WAL volume (max_wal_size, default 1 GB). The trade-off: frequent checkpoints reduce recovery time (less WAL to replay) but increase I/O (more frequent full-buffer writes). Infrequent checkpoints reduce I/O but increase recovery time. At AlpineEnergy, checkpoint_timeout = 15 minutes and max_wal_size = 4 GB — tuned for the nightly pipeline's write volume while keeping recovery time under 2 minutes.
WAL Configuration for Replication

Streaming replication sends WAL records from the primary to the replica in real time. The replica applies the records to stay synchronized. The replication lag — the time between a write on the primary and its visibility on the replica — depends on the WAL write rate, the network latency, and the replica's apply rate. At AlpineEnergy, the primary generates approximately 2 GB of WAL per hour during the nightly pipeline window. The cross-region replica (in the disaster recovery site) has 15ms network latency, producing a typical replication lag of 50-200ms. The wal_keep_size parameter (set to 8 GB at AlpineEnergy) determines how much WAL the primary retains for a lagging replica — if the replica falls behind by more than 8 GB of WAL, the primary recycles the WAL segments and the replica must be rebuilt from a base backup.
Monitoring WAL Generation Rate

-- WAL generation rate: how much WAL is being produced?
SELECT pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), '0/0') / (1024*1024*1024) AS total_wal_gb,
       pg_wal_lsn_diff(
         pg_current_wal_lsn(),
         stat_reset_time::TEXT::pg_lsn  -- approximate: WAL position at stats reset
       ) / (1024*1024) AS wal_since_reset_mb
FROM   pg_stat_bgwriter;

-- Per-table WAL contribution (PostgreSQL 13+)
-- Identifies which tables generate the most WAL
SELECT relname,
       n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del AS total_modifications,
       pg_size_pretty((n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del) * 200) AS estimated_wal
FROM   pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver'
ORDER BY total_modifications DESC
LIMIT  10;

The WAL generation rate determines three operational parameters: the checkpoint frequency (more WAL = more frequent checkpoints), the replication bandwidth requirement (the replica must receive WAL at least as fast as the primary generates it), and the PITR storage cost (WAL archives consume disk proportional to the generation rate × retention period). At AlpineEnergy, the Silver meter_readings table generates approximately 60% of all WAL (due to its 4.8 million daily inserts). The platform team monitors WAL generation rate per table to detect anomalies: a sudden 3x increase in WAL from the meter_readings table indicates either a bulk reprocessing event (expected) or a pipeline bug that is inserting duplicate data (unexpected).

blocks.title: buffers_backend should be near zero
blocks.content: In pg_stat_bgwriter, the buffers_backend counter shows how many buffers were written to disk by backend processes (query execution) rather than by the background writer or checkpointer. A high buffers_backend count means backends are doing I/O that should be handled by background processes — typically because shared_buffers is too small (not enough room for dirty buffers) or the background writer is too slow (bgwriter_lru_maxpages too low). At AlpineEnergy, buffers_backend is < 1% of total buffer writes — indicating that the background writer and checkpointer are handling the write workload adequately.

Every page of data that PostgreSQL reads or writes passes through the shared buffer pool — a fixed-size region of shared memory (configured by shared_buffers) that caches table and index pages. When a query needs a page, it first checks the buffer pool. If the page is there (a buffer hit), no disk I/O occurs. If not (a buffer miss), the page is read from disk into the buffer pool, potentially evicting another page to make room. The buffer hit ratio — the fraction of page requests satisfied from the buffer pool — is the single most important performance metric for the storage layer. A ratio above 99% means almost all data access is served from memory. A ratio below 95% means significant disk I/O is occurring, and queries are likely I/O-bound.
The Clock-Sweep Eviction Algorithm

When the buffer pool is full and a new page must be loaded, PostgreSQL evicts a page using the clock-sweep algorithm: a pointer rotates through the buffer pool, decrementing each buffer's usage count. When it finds a buffer with usage count zero, it evicts that buffer. Frequently accessed buffers have higher usage counts and survive multiple sweeps. Infrequently accessed buffers are evicted quickly. The algorithm approximates LRU (Least Recently Used) without the overhead of maintaining a sorted list. At AlpineEnergy, the clock-sweep adequately manages the buffer pool for the OLTP workload (frequently accessed dimension tables stay cached, infrequently accessed historical partitions are evicted). The exception: a large sequential scan (the quarterly archive query) can flood the buffer pool with pages that are accessed once and then evicted — displacing the frequently accessed pages. PostgreSQL mitigates this with ring buffers for sequential scans.
Ring Buffers for Large Scans

When PostgreSQL detects a sequential scan on a table that is larger than shared_buffers / 4, it uses a ring buffer — a small, private buffer (typically 256 KB, or 32 pages) that recycles pages within the ring instead of loading them into the shared buffer pool. This prevents the large scan from evicting other pages from the shared pool. The ring buffer mechanism is automatic and invisible — no configuration required. At AlpineEnergy, the quarterly archive scan reads 3.5 TB through a ring buffer. Without the ring buffer, this scan would evict every other page from the 8 GB shared buffer pool, causing cache misses for all concurrent queries. With the ring buffer, the scan reads into its private 256 KB ring, and the shared pool's hit ratio for other queries remains above 99%.
Beyond the Naive Hit Ratio

-- The standard cache hit ratio formula (oversimplified)
SELECT ROUND(
  SUM(heap_blks_hit)::NUMERIC /
  NULLIF(SUM(heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read), 0) * 100, 2
) AS heap_hit_pct,
ROUND(
  SUM(idx_blks_hit)::NUMERIC /
  NULLIF(SUM(idx_blks_hit + idx_blks_read), 0) * 100, 2
) AS index_hit_pct
FROM   pg_statio_user_tables
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver';

-- Per-table hit ratio (reveals which tables are cache-cold)
SELECT relname,
       heap_blks_hit, heap_blks_read,
       ROUND(heap_blks_hit::NUMERIC /
             NULLIF(heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read, 0) * 100, 1) AS hit_pct
FROM   pg_statio_user_tables
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver'
  AND  heap_blks_read > 0
ORDER BY hit_pct ASC
LIMIT  10;

The aggregate hit ratio includes the OS page cache — pages that miss the PostgreSQL buffer pool may still be cached by the operating system. The heap_blks_read counter increments when a page is not found in shared_buffers, but the actual disk I/O may be zero if the OS cache has the page. A true I/O miss (requiring physical disk read) is visible only through OS-level metrics (iostat) or the BUFFERS option in EXPLAIN ANALYZE. At AlpineEnergy, the PostgreSQL-level hit ratio is 98.7%, but the effective hit ratio (including OS cache) is 99.6% — because the OS caches the most recent 16 GB of data files in addition to the 8 GB shared_buffers. The per-table hit ratio reveals which tables are cache-cold: the 3-year-old archive partitions have 72% hit ratio (frequently evicted from both caches), while the current month's partition has 99.9% (always cached).
Sizing shared_buffers

The standard recommendation — 25% of total RAM for shared_buffers — is a starting point, not an answer. At AlpineEnergy, the 32 GB server has shared_buffers = 8 GB (25%). The hot working set (current month's meter_readings partition + all dimension tables + all indexes on hot tables) is approximately 6.5 GB — fitting comfortably within the buffer pool with 1.5 GB of headroom for concurrent queries' sort and hash buffers. If the hot working set were 12 GB, increasing shared_buffers to 12 GB would be justified — even though it exceeds 25% of RAM — because the OS needs less page cache when PostgreSQL caches more. The sizing decision: measure the hot working set, set shared_buffers to contain it with 20% headroom, and verify that the remaining RAM is sufficient for the OS cache, the backend private memory, and the operating system.

blocks.title: The buffer pool is a cooperative cache with the OS
blocks.content: PostgreSQL's shared_buffers and the OS page cache form a two-tier caching system. Hot data lives in shared_buffers (fastest access — no system call). Warm data lives in the OS cache (fast access — system call but no disk I/O). Cold data lives on disk (slow access — physical read). The optimal shared_buffers size keeps hot data in the first tier without over-allocating memory that the OS cache needs for warm data. Over-sizing shared_buffers can actually reduce performance by starving the OS cache — a 24 GB shared_buffers on a 32 GB server leaves only 8 GB for the OS cache, backend memory, and other processes.

VACUUM is PostgreSQL's garbage collector: it removes dead tuple versions left behind by UPDATE and DELETE operations, reclaims their space for reuse, and updates the visibility map and free space map. Without VACUUM, every UPDATE creates a dead version that permanently consumes space. A table that is updated 100,000 times per day accumulates 100,000 dead versions per day — and after a year, the dead space exceeds the live data. VACUUM is not an optimization — it is a fundamental maintenance operation. A database that does not vacuum eventually fills its disk, degrades its scan performance (scanning dead tuples alongside live ones), and approaches the transaction ID wraparound limit (lesson 2).
The Lazy VACUUM Algorithm

Standard VACUUM (also called lazy VACUUM) makes two passes over the table. Pass 1 scans each page, identifies dead tuples (where xmax is a committed transaction that is older than the oldest active transaction), and collects their tuple IDs into an internal list. Pass 2 visits the indexes to remove index entries pointing to the dead tuples, then returns to the heap pages and marks the dead tuple space as available in the free space map. The two-pass approach is necessary because a dead heap tuple may be referenced by multiple indexes — the index entries must be removed before the heap space is reclaimed, or a concurrent index scan could follow a dangling pointer.
The Visibility Map

The visibility map (VM) tracks which table pages contain only tuples visible to all transactions — pages with no dead tuples and no recently-inserted tuples that might not be visible to old snapshots. VACUUM skips VM-marked pages entirely (no dead tuples to remove), making subsequent VACUUM runs faster as fewer pages need scanning. Index-only scans also use the VM: a page marked as all-visible does not need a heap fetch to verify tuple visibility, because the VM guarantees all tuples on that page are visible. At AlpineEnergy, the meter_readings table has 98% of its pages marked as all-visible (historical data that is never updated) — making both VACUUM and index-only scans extremely efficient on historical partitions.
Autovacuum Tuning for Large Tables

-- Current autovacuum settings and backlog per table
SELECT relname,
       n_live_tup,
       n_dead_tup,
       ROUND(n_dead_tup::NUMERIC / NULLIF(n_live_tup, 0) * 100, 1) AS dead_pct,
       last_autovacuum,
       last_autoanalyze,
       -- How many dead tuples until autovacuum triggers?
       (reltuples * current_setting('autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor')::NUMERIC
        + current_setting('autovacuum_vacuum_threshold')::NUMERIC) AS trigger_threshold
FROM   pg_stat_user_tables
  JOIN pg_class ON relname = pg_class.relname
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver'
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC
LIMIT  10;

-- Per-table autovacuum tuning for high-update tables
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_status
  SET (autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.01,  -- 1% instead of default 20%
       autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay = 2);       -- 2ms instead of default 20ms

The default autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor of 0.20 triggers VACUUM after 20% of the table's rows are dead. For a 1-million-row table, that is 200,000 dead tuples — potentially 200 MB of wasted space accumulated before VACUUM runs. For high-update tables (like meter_status, which is updated for every meter online/offline event), the 20% threshold allows excessive bloat. At AlpineEnergy, meter_status has a per-table override: scale_factor = 0.01 (1%) triggers VACUUM after 10,000 dead tuples instead of 200,000. The cost_delay = 2 (instead of the default 20) makes the vacuum work faster by reducing the sleep time between I/O operations — acceptable for this table because it is small (850 MB) and the vacuum completes in under 30 seconds.
VACUUM FULL vs Standard VACUUM

Standard VACUUM reclaims dead space within existing pages but does not shrink the table file. A table that grew from 1 GB to 1.5 GB due to bloat and then was vacuumed remains 1.5 GB — the dead space is marked as reusable for future INSERTs, but the file does not shrink. VACUUM FULL rewrites the entire table into a new file, compacting it to the minimum size. The trade-off: VACUUM FULL requires an AccessExclusiveLock (blocking all reads and writes) and needs enough disk space for both the old and new copies simultaneously. At AlpineEnergy, VACUUM FULL is never run on production tables during business hours. It is scheduled during the quarterly maintenance window for tables with > 30% bloat ratio — which has occurred only once (the meter_status table before the autovacuum tuning was applied).

blocks.title: Long-running transactions block VACUUM
blocks.content: VACUUM can only remove dead tuples that are older than the oldest active transaction. An 'idle in transaction' connection that has been open for 6 hours prevents VACUUM from reclaiming any dead tuples created in the last 6 hours — even if those tuples were deleted by other, long-since-committed transactions. At AlpineEnergy, the operational dashboard monitors the oldest active transaction age with a critical alert at 1 hour. The idle_in_transaction_session_timeout is set to 30 minutes — automatically terminating connections that hold transactions open without executing queries.

The mid-level track (Module 10, lesson 5) covered lock contention at the operational level: identifying blocking sessions, terminating blockers, and scheduling DDL to avoid conflicts. This lesson goes inside the lock manager to explain the implementation: how locks are represented in shared memory, why different lock types are compatible or incompatible, how deadlocks are detected, and how advisory locks provide application-level locking without the overhead of table-level locks. Understanding the lock manager internals enables diagnosis of lock-related problems that are invisible from pg_stat_activity alone: lightweight lock contention on internal buffers, predicate lock escalation in serializable transactions, and advisory lock leaks.
Lock Types and the Compatibility Matrix

rows: Minimal — readers do not block each other
Low — row-level locking for concurrent updates
Low — multiple writers to same table are fine (row-level conflicts resolved separately)
Moderate — blocks writes during index creation
ALTER TABLE, DROP TABLE, TRUNCATE, VACUUM FULL
Maximum — blocks all concurrent access including SELECT

blocks.content: The compatibility matrix determines which operations can proceed concurrently. The key insight: INSERTs and UPDATEs (RowExclusiveLock) do not block each other at the table level — row-level conflicts are handled by a separate mechanism (tuple locks). This means multiple pipelines can write to the same table simultaneously. ALTER TABLE (AccessExclusiveLock) blocks everything — including SELECTs — because it modifies the table's structure. At AlpineEnergy, the most common lock conflict is between ALTER TABLE (during a migration deployment) and concurrent SELECT queries (from dashboard connections). The resolution: deploy migrations during the 03:00-04:00 window when no dashboard connections are active.
Lightweight Locks (lwlocks)

Heavyweight locks (the table above) protect database objects — tables, indexes, rows. Lightweight locks (lwlocks) protect internal data structures — buffer pool entries, WAL buffers, shared hash tables. Lwlocks are invisible to pg_locks but visible in pg_stat_activity's wait_event column: a backend waiting on 'BufferContent' is waiting for a lightweight lock on a specific buffer page, typically because another backend is writing to that page. At AlpineEnergy, lwlock contention occurs during peak write periods when multiple pipeline backends write to the same table partition simultaneously. The contention is visible as 'LWLock:BufferMapping' or 'LWLock:BufferContent' wait events. The resolution: partition the write workload across different partitions (each partition's pages have separate buffer locks) or spread the writes across time.
Deadlock Detection

-- Monitor deadlock frequency
SELECT datname,
       deadlocks,
       conflicts
FROM   pg_stat_database
WHERE  datname = 'alpineenergy_ch';

-- Check deadlock detection timeout
SHOW deadlock_timeout;  -- Default: 1s
-- PostgreSQL checks for deadlocks only after a lock wait exceeds this timeout
-- Lower = faster deadlock detection but more CPU overhead for the check
-- Higher = slower detection but less overhead

PostgreSQL's deadlock detection runs a cycle-detection algorithm on the lock wait graph: it finds cycles (A waits for B, B waits for A) and terminates one of the transactions to break the cycle. The detection runs only after a lock wait exceeds deadlock_timeout (default 1 second) — before this timeout, the backend simply waits, because most lock waits are resolved quickly without being deadlocks. At AlpineEnergy, the deadlock count is tracked as a monitoring metric. Any deadlock triggers a post-incident review because deadlocks indicate a design problem: two pipelines accessing the same tables in different orders. The fix is always at the application level — ensuring consistent table access ordering — not at the database level.
Advisory Locks

Advisory locks are application-defined locks that do not correspond to any database object. The pipeline acquires an advisory lock (pg_advisory_lock(12345)) before starting, and releases it when complete. A second instance of the pipeline checks for the lock (pg_try_advisory_lock(12345)) — if it is held, the second instance knows the first is still running and exits. At AlpineEnergy, advisory locks prevent concurrent execution of the same pipeline: the Airflow scheduler occasionally launches overlapping runs during retry scenarios, and the advisory lock ensures that only one instance writes to the target table. The lock ID (12345) is derived from the pipeline name via a hash, documented in the pipeline's configuration.

blocks.title: Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY to avoid AccessExclusiveLock on reads
blocks.content: Standard CREATE INDEX acquires a ShareLock that blocks writes. CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY acquires only a ShareUpdateExclusiveLock — which does not block reads or writes. The concurrent variant takes longer (it builds the index in two passes) and requires more WAL, but it avoids blocking any concurrent operations. At AlpineEnergy, all production index creation uses CONCURRENTLY. The non-concurrent variant is used only during initial schema deployment when no concurrent access exists.

The mid-level track covered index design: which columns to index, composite vs single-column, partial and covering indexes. This lesson covers how B-tree indexes work at the implementation level: the page structure, how inserts and splits maintain balance, how PostgreSQL avoids unnecessary index updates for non-indexed columns (HOT — Heap-Only Tuples), and how deduplication reduces index size. Understanding index internals explains phenomena that index design alone cannot: why an index on a monotonically increasing column (like a serial ID or a timestamp) creates a 'right-edge hot spot,' why index bloat can exceed table bloat, and why reindexing a large index takes hours even though creating a new index on the same data takes minutes.
B-Tree Page Structure

A B-tree index in PostgreSQL is a balanced tree of 8 KB pages. The root page contains pointers to internal pages, internal pages contain pointers to other internal or leaf pages, and leaf pages contain (key value, heap tuple pointer) pairs. Leaf pages are linked in a doubly-linked list — enabling range scans to walk from one leaf to the next without returning to the root. For AlpineEnergy's meter_readings date index (2.8 GB), the tree has approximately 3 levels: 1 root page, ~340 internal pages, and ~360,000 leaf pages. An index lookup traverses 3 pages (root → internal → leaf) — 3 random reads in the worst case, or 3 buffer pool lookups in the best case. The tree's shallow depth (3-4 levels for most real-world indexes) is why index lookups are fast regardless of table size.
HOT Updates

-- Check HOT update effectiveness per table
SELECT relname,
       n_tup_upd                    AS total_updates,
       n_tup_hot_upd                AS hot_updates,
       ROUND(n_tup_hot_upd::NUMERIC / NULLIF(n_tup_upd, 0) * 100, 1) AS hot_pct
FROM   pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver'
  AND  n_tup_upd > 0
ORDER BY hot_pct ASC;

When an UPDATE changes only non-indexed columns, PostgreSQL can create the new tuple version on the same page as the old version and chain them together without updating any index. This is a HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) update — it avoids the index maintenance cost entirely. HOT updates are possible when two conditions are met: the updated columns are not in any index, and the new version fits on the same page as the old version (there is free space on the page). At AlpineEnergy, the meter_status table updates is_active and last_seen_at (neither is indexed), achieving a 94% HOT update rate. The meters table updates customer_type (which is indexed for the SCD pipeline), achieving only 12% HOT rate — every customer_type change requires index maintenance.
Index Deduplication (PostgreSQL 13+)

Before PostgreSQL 13, a B-tree index stored one entry per heap tuple, even for duplicate key values. An index on customer_type (3 distinct values across 1.2 million rows) stored 1.2 million index entries, most pointing to the same key value. PostgreSQL 13 introduced deduplication: multiple heap tuple pointers with the same key value are stored in a single index entry (a posting list). The customer_type index shrinks from 1.2 million entries to approximately 3 entries with 400,000 heap pointers each. At AlpineEnergy, deduplication reduced the region index size by 68% (from 1.4 GB to 450 MB) and the customer_type index size by 73%. Index-only scans on these columns also became faster because fewer index pages need to be read.
Index Bloat and REINDEX

B-tree indexes can bloat when pages are split by inserts and then partially emptied by deletes. A half-empty page consumes the same 8 KB as a full page, and the index does not automatically merge half-empty pages. Over time, a heavily updated index may use 2-3x the space of a freshly built index. At AlpineEnergy, the date index on meter_readings bloats minimally (inserts are monotonically increasing, so page splits occur only at the rightmost leaf — no fragmentation of existing pages). The meter_id index on the meters dimension table bloats moderately (random inserts and deletes from meter activation/deactivation). REINDEX CONCURRENTLY rebuilds the index without blocking reads or writes — the recommended approach for production deduplication. At AlpineEnergy, REINDEX CONCURRENTLY runs quarterly for indexes with > 30% estimated bloat.

blocks.title: Fill factor controls the HOT trade-off
blocks.content: The fill factor (ALTER TABLE SET (fillfactor = N)) reserves space on each heap page for future updates. A fillfactor of 90 fills each page to 90%, leaving 10% free for HOT updates. Lower fill factor = more HOT updates but more pages (more storage, slower sequential scans). Higher fill factor = fewer HOT updates but fewer pages. At AlpineEnergy, meter_status has fillfactor = 80 (frequently updated, HOT optimization is valuable) and meter_readings has fillfactor = 100 (append-only, no updates, HOT is irrelevant).

The mid-level track introduced PgBouncer as a connection pooler that reduces the number of backend processes (Module 8, lesson 9). This lesson covers the connection lifecycle at the implementation level: what happens when a client connects, the cost of creating a backend process, the authentication overhead, how PgBouncer's pooling modes differ in their routing behavior, and the interaction between pooling and prepared statements. Understanding these internals explains why connection pooling is not optional at enterprise scale and why the pooling mode choice affects application behavior.
The Connection Cost

When a client connects directly to PostgreSQL (without a pooler), the postmaster forks a new backend process. The fork costs approximately 5-10 MB of private memory allocation, 10-50ms of OS process creation time, and a TLS handshake if SSL is enabled (another 5-20ms). For a dashboard that opens a connection, runs one query (100ms), and closes the connection, the connection overhead (50ms fork + 15ms TLS = 65ms) represents 39% of the total time. At AlpineEnergy's scale (200+ logical connections from 10 teams), creating and destroying backend processes for each query would generate 2,000+ forks per minute — consuming significant CPU and memory in process management overhead. PgBouncer eliminates this overhead by maintaining a pool of pre-forked backends and routing client requests to idle backends.
PgBouncer Pooling Modes

rows: Backend assigned for entire client session
Applications that use session-level state (SET, temp tables, cursors)
Backend assigned per transaction, returned to pool at COMMIT
Web applications and pipelines with short transactions (AlpineEnergy default)
Backend assigned per statement, returned after each statement
Not supported (each statement may use a different backend)
Simple query workloads with no multi-statement transactions

blocks.content: AlpineEnergy uses transaction pooling as the default: each client transaction is assigned an idle backend from the pool, and the backend is returned to the pool when the transaction commits. This allows 200+ logical connections to share 30 physical backends. The trade-off: prepared statements (PREPARE / EXECUTE) do not work across transactions because different transactions may use different backends — and a prepared statement is specific to the backend that created it. The pipeline circumvents this by using PgBouncer's prepared statement protocol-level handling (PgBouncer 1.21+, which transparently re-prepares statements on backend assignment) or by using parameterized queries instead of explicit PREPARE.
Idle Connection Management

An idle connection in PgBouncer's client pool consumes minimal resources (a TCP socket and a small state structure in PgBouncer's memory). An idle backend in the server pool consumes significant resources (5-10 MB of private memory in PostgreSQL). PgBouncer's server_idle_timeout (set to 300 seconds at AlpineEnergy) closes backends that have been idle for 5 minutes, returning the memory to the OS. During peak hours (06:00-18:00), 25-30 backends are active. During off-hours (18:00-06:00), the pool shrinks to 5-8 backends. This dynamic sizing keeps PostgreSQL's memory footprint proportional to the actual workload — not to the maximum connection count.
When NOT to Use Connection Pooling

Connection pooling interferes with features that depend on session-level state: temporary tables (created by one transaction, used by a later transaction on a different backend), LISTEN/NOTIFY (notifications are delivered to the backend, not the client), and SET commands (parameter changes apply to the backend, which may be assigned to a different client next). At AlpineEnergy, the CDC pipeline uses LISTEN/NOTIFY for change detection and connects directly to PostgreSQL (bypassing PgBouncer) through a dedicated connection reserved for this purpose. The monitoring system also connects directly to access pg_stat_activity and pg_locks without PgBouncer's overhead. All other connections route through PgBouncer.

blocks.title: Monitor pool saturation, not connection count
blocks.content: The critical metric is not how many client connections exist — it is whether the server pool has idle backends available for incoming transactions. If all 30 server backends are active and a 31st transaction arrives, it waits in PgBouncer's queue until a backend becomes available. At AlpineEnergy, PgBouncer's cl_waiting metric (clients waiting for a backend) is monitored with a warning threshold at 5 and a critical threshold at 15. A sustained cl_waiting above 5 indicates the server pool is undersized and needs more backends — or the application is holding transactions open too long.

PostgreSQL's extension system allows adding functionality to the database without modifying the core code: new data types, new index methods, new functions, new operators, and hooks into the planner, executor, and storage manager. AlpineEnergy uses 7 extensions in production: pg_stat_statements (query performance tracking), pageinspect (tuple-level inspection), pgcrypto (column encryption), pg_trgm (fuzzy text matching for meter address search), btree_gist (GiST index support for exclusion constraints on temporal ranges), pg_hint_plan (optimizer hint injection), and a custom extension for cross-border query routing. Understanding the extension architecture enables the platform team to evaluate, deploy, and maintain extensions safely — and to build custom extensions when no existing extension meets a platform need.
The Extension Lifecycle

-- View installed extensions and their versions
SELECT extname, extversion, extrelocatable
FROM   pg_extension
ORDER BY extname;

-- Install an extension
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_stat_statements;

-- Upgrade an extension after a PostgreSQL major version upgrade
ALTER EXTENSION pg_stat_statements UPDATE;

-- The extension's control file defines metadata:
-- /usr/share/postgresql/16/extension/pg_stat_statements.control
-- comment = 'track planning and execution statistics of all SQL statements'
-- default_version = '1.10'
-- module_pathname = '$libdir/pg_stat_statements'
-- schema = pg_catalog
-- superuser = true

An extension is a package containing SQL scripts (creating functions, types, operators), a shared library (.so file with C code), and a control file (metadata). CREATE EXTENSION runs the SQL script and loads the shared library. ALTER EXTENSION UPDATE applies upgrade scripts when a new version is available. At AlpineEnergy, extension installation is managed through migration scripts (Module 7) — CREATE EXTENSION is a migration like any other DDL change. Extension upgrades are tested in staging before production because the upgrade script may alter function signatures or behavior. The platform team maintains a compatibility matrix: which extensions are compatible with which PostgreSQL versions, tested quarterly during the version upgrade evaluation.
pg_stat_statements Deep Dive

pg_stat_statements is the most important extension for data platform operations. It records cumulative statistics for every distinct SQL statement: execution count, total and mean execution time, rows returned, shared buffer hits and reads, and planning time. The mid-level track used it for cost allocation (Module 10, lesson 8). At senior level, it is the primary tool for identifying performance regression candidates, detecting inefficient queries, and measuring the impact of optimization changes. At AlpineEnergy, pg_stat_statements tracks approximately 2,400 distinct query patterns. The platform team reviews the top 20 by total execution time weekly, and the top 50 by mean execution time quarterly. The weekly review has identified 8 optimization opportunities in 12 months, each producing 20-60% improvement in the identified query.
Custom Extensions

AlpineEnergy's custom extension (alpine_routing) implements cross-border query routing: it hooks into the planner to intercept queries on partitioned tables and adds country-specific partition pruning based on the connecting role's assigned country. Swiss users' queries automatically prune to Swiss partitions; Austrian users' queries prune to Austrian partitions. The extension uses PostgreSQL's planner hook API (set_rel_pathlist_hook) to inject the pruning condition after the standard planner runs. The alternative — implementing the routing in application code or in views — would require every query to include a country filter, which is error-prone and unenforceable. The extension makes the routing transparent and mandatory.
When NOT to Use Extensions

Extensions add operational complexity: they must be installed on every instance (including replicas), upgraded during PostgreSQL major version upgrades, and monitored for compatibility with new PostgreSQL releases. Some extensions (like pg_hint_plan) modify the optimizer's behavior globally, which can produce unexpected plan changes for queries that do not use hints. At AlpineEnergy, the extension policy requires: the extension must be maintained by an active open-source community (or be a custom AlpineEnergy extension), it must be tested against the target PostgreSQL version in staging, and it must have a documented rollback plan (DROP EXTENSION). The platform team has rejected 3 extension proposals in 18 months — two because the extension was unmaintained, and one because it conflicted with pg_stat_statements.

blocks.title: Extensions run inside the PostgreSQL process
blocks.content: A bug in an extension can crash the PostgreSQL backend process — or worse, corrupt shared memory and crash the entire cluster. Extensions written in C have no memory safety guarantees — a buffer overflow in an extension function can overwrite arbitrary memory. At AlpineEnergy, custom extensions undergo the same code review and testing process as production pipeline code. Third-party extensions are evaluated for code quality (repository activity, issue history, test coverage) before installation. The platform team's policy: no extension is installed on production without 2 weeks of staging validation.

This module covered the PostgreSQL engine at the implementation level: the process architecture that determines memory and CPU usage, MVCC that enables concurrent access at the cost of dead tuple accumulation, the WAL that provides durability and replication, the buffer manager that caches data pages with clock-sweep eviction, VACUUM that reclaims dead space and prevents transaction ID wraparound, the lock manager with heavyweight and lightweight locks, B-tree index internals with HOT updates and deduplication, connection pooling at the protocol level, and the extension architecture that enables platform-specific customization. This final lesson synthesizes these into the diagnostic playbook that AlpineEnergy's platform team uses for engine-level production issues.
The Engine Diagnostic Decision Table

rows: pg_statio: cache hit ratio; pg_stat_bgwriter: buffers_backend
Check shared_buffers sizing; check for large sequential scans displacing cache
Table growing despite stable row count
Check autovacuum settings; check for long-running transactions blocking VACUUM
Run manual VACUUM FREEZE on affected tables; investigate autovacuum_freeze_max_age
Check WAL generation rate; check replica I/O capacity; increase wal_keep_size
Identify blocking query; terminate or wait; schedule DDL during quiet window
High CPU with many active backends
Process architecture — too many concurrent queries
Check PgBouncer pool sizing; reduce max_connections; optimize slow queries
pg_stat_user_indexes: idx_scan count; pgstattuple: index bloat
Check bloat ratio; REINDEX CONCURRENTLY if > 30% bloat

blocks.content: The Diagnostic Stored Function

-- AlpineEnergy's consolidated diagnostic function
-- Returns a single-page health summary for the on-call engineer
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION platform.engine_health_check()
RETURNS TABLE (
    check_name TEXT,
    status     TEXT,
    value      TEXT,
    threshold  TEXT
) AS $$
BEGIN
  -- Cache hit ratio
  RETURN QUERY
  SELECT 'Buffer cache hit ratio'::TEXT,
         CASE WHEN ratio > 0.99 THEN 'OK' WHEN ratio > 0.95 THEN 'WARNING' ELSE 'CRITICAL' END,
         ROUND(ratio * 100, 2)::TEXT || '%',
         '> 99%'
  FROM (SELECT SUM(heap_blks_hit)::NUMERIC / NULLIF(SUM(heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read), 0) AS ratio
        FROM pg_statio_user_tables) sub;

  -- Max XID age
  RETURN QUERY
  SELECT 'Max XID age',
         CASE WHEN max_age < 150000000 THEN 'OK' WHEN max_age < 180000000 THEN 'WARNING' ELSE 'CRITICAL' END,
         max_age::TEXT,
         '< 150M'
  FROM (SELECT MAX(age(relfrozenxid)) AS max_age FROM pg_class WHERE relkind = 'r') sub;

  -- Dead tuple ratio (worst table)
  RETURN QUERY
  SELECT 'Worst dead tuple ratio (' || relname || ')',
         CASE WHEN dead_pct < 10 THEN 'OK' WHEN dead_pct < 30 THEN 'WARNING' ELSE 'CRITICAL' END,
         ROUND(dead_pct, 1)::TEXT || '%',
         '< 10%'
  FROM (SELECT relname, n_dead_tup::NUMERIC / NULLIF(n_live_tup, 0) * 100 AS dead_pct
        FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY dead_pct DESC NULLS LAST LIMIT 1) sub;

  -- Active connections
  RETURN QUERY
  SELECT 'Active backends',
         CASE WHEN cnt < 30 THEN 'OK' WHEN cnt < 50 THEN 'WARNING' ELSE 'CRITICAL' END,
         cnt::TEXT,
         '< 30'
  FROM (SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active') sub;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

The function produces a 4-row health summary that the on-call engineer can run in 2 seconds: buffer cache hit ratio, maximum transaction ID age, worst dead tuple ratio, and active backend count. Each check has a status (OK, WARNING, CRITICAL) and a threshold. The function does not fix anything — it provides the diagnostic data that the engineer needs to choose the correct resolution path from the decision table above. At AlpineEnergy, the operational dashboard runs this function every 60 seconds and displays the results on the engine health panel alongside the table freshness and quality metrics from Module 9.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: This module covered the engine that executes SQL. Module 7 covers the storage that holds the data: PostgreSQL's heap storage format with TOAST and fill factor tuning, Parquet's columnar internals with encoding and compression, Delta Lake's transaction log and data skipping mechanics, the cloud storage performance characteristics that affect both engines, and the row-store vs columnar decision that determines which engine stores each table. The engine internals from this module and the storage internals from Module 7 together provide the complete implementation-level understanding of where data lives, how it is accessed, and why specific access patterns are fast or slow.

modules.title: Storage and I/O Internals
modules.description: Understand the storage layer at the implementation level across both row-store and columnar formats — PostgreSQL heap storage, Parquet internals, Delta Lake mechanics, cloud storage optimization, and the table format decisions that determine I/O performance at terabyte scale.

blocks.content: Module 6 covered how PostgreSQL processes queries (process architecture, buffer management, locking). This lesson covers how PostgreSQL stores data on disk: the heap file format that determines how rows are written, read, updated, and reclaimed. Every performance characteristic of PostgreSQL's storage layer — why sequential scans are fast, why random lookups are slower, why updates create dead tuples, why TOAST is needed for large values — derives from the heap format's physical structure. Understanding this structure explains why certain table design decisions (fill factor, column ordering, TOAST configuration) have measurable performance impact and why the same logical data can have dramatically different storage footprints depending on how it is physically arranged.
The Page Layout

blocks.title: PostgreSQL heap page structure (8 KB)
blocks.caption: Each heap page is 8 KB. The page header tracks metadata (LSN for WAL, checksum for corruption detection). Line pointers are a fixed-size array at the top, pointing to tuples stored at the bottom. Tuples grow upward from the page end; line pointers grow downward from the header. Free space is the gap between them. When the gap closes, the page is full.

blocks.content: The line pointer array provides indirection: index entries point to a line pointer (not directly to a tuple), and the line pointer points to the tuple's current position within the page. This indirection enables HOT updates (Module 6, lesson 7): when a tuple is updated to a new position on the same page, the line pointer is updated to point to the new position without modifying any index entry. Without line pointers, every update would require updating every index that references the row — dramatically increasing the write amplification for indexed tables.
Tuple Layout and Alignment

-- Measure the actual on-disk size of a tuple
-- pg_column_size includes alignment padding and the 23-byte tuple header
SELECT pg_column_size(ROW(
  'CH-BERN-00142'::VARCHAR(20),  -- meter_id: 13 chars + 4-byte varlena header = 17 bytes
  1842.50::DOUBLE PRECISION,      -- daily_kw: 8 bytes
  '2025-01-15'::DATE,             -- date: 4 bytes
  'BERN-NORTH'::VARCHAR(30),      -- region: 10 chars + 4-byte varlena header = 14 bytes
  'RESIDENTIAL'::VARCHAR(20),     -- customer_type: 11 chars + 4 = 15 bytes
  NOW()::TIMESTAMPTZ              -- loaded_at: 8 bytes
)) AS tuple_bytes;
-- Result: ~90 bytes per tuple (including 23-byte header + alignment padding)
-- 8192 bytes per page / ~90 bytes per tuple ≈ 91 tuples per page
-- 130M rows / 91 tuples per page ≈ 1.43M pages ≈ 10.9 GB

PostgreSQL aligns column data to machine word boundaries for CPU efficiency: an 8-byte DOUBLE PRECISION must start at an 8-byte boundary, a 4-byte INTEGER at a 4-byte boundary. Alignment padding between columns wastes space. Column ordering within a CREATE TABLE affects padding: placing 8-byte columns first, then 4-byte columns, then variable-length columns minimizes padding. At AlpineEnergy, the meter_readings table was reordered from the logical grouping (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, loaded_at) to the alignment-optimal grouping (daily_kw, loaded_at, date, meter_id, region, customer_type) — saving 6 bytes per tuple, which at 1.4 billion rows reduces the table size by approximately 8 GB.
TOAST — The Oversized Attribute Storage Technique

PostgreSQL requires that every tuple fit within a single 8 KB page. Values larger than approximately 2 KB are compressed and/or moved to a separate TOAST table — a hidden companion table that stores oversized values out-of-line. The main tuple stores a pointer to the TOAST table instead of the value itself. TOAST is transparent: SELECT returns the full value by following the pointer, and the application never knows the value was stored externally. At AlpineEnergy, the meter_readings table has no TOAST-eligible columns (the largest column is VARCHAR(30)). The audit_log table, which stores JSON payloads of 5-50 KB, uses TOAST heavily — each row's json_payload is compressed and stored in the TOAST table, reducing the main heap page's tuple size from ~25 KB to ~100 bytes and enabling efficient sequential scans that skip the large payloads when the query does not select them.

blocks.title: Row-store pays for every column on every scan
blocks.content: In PostgreSQL's heap format, a sequential scan reads every byte of every tuple on every page — including columns that the query does not need. A query that selects only meter_id and daily_kw from a tuple with 6 columns still reads the entire 90-byte tuple. This is the fundamental I/O disadvantage of row-store for analytical queries that access few columns from wide tables — and the fundamental reason why AlpineEnergy uses Spark SQL with columnar storage for analytical workloads (lesson 7). Row-store is optimal when queries access most columns (OLTP point lookups), and columnar is optimal when queries access few columns (analytical aggregations).

Parquet is the columnar file format that underlies Delta Lake, Iceberg, and Hudi — the three table formats used for analytical data lakes. Understanding Parquet's internal structure explains why columnar storage is fast for analytical queries (column pruning eliminates I/O for unneeded columns), why min/max statistics enable row group skipping (the query can skip entire row groups where the filter value is outside the statistics range), and why encoding and compression are so effective on columnar data (values within a column share the same type and often share patterns). Every Spark SQL scan, every Delta Lake data skipping decision, and every OPTIMIZE operation is ultimately an operation on Parquet files.
The Parquet File Structure

blocks.title: Parquet file internal structure
blocks.caption: A Parquet file contains one or more row groups, each containing column chunks for every column in the schema. Each column chunk is divided into pages (typically 1 MB). The footer at the end of the file stores the schema, row group boundaries, and per-column-chunk statistics (min, max, null count, distinct count). Readers read the footer first, then use statistics to skip entire row groups.

blocks.content: The row group is the unit of parallelism: Spark SQL assigns one task per row group. A 1 GB file with 8 row groups of 128 MB each produces 8 tasks. The column chunk is the unit of column pruning: a query that selects only meter_id and daily_kw reads only those two column chunks from each row group, skipping the column chunks for date, region, customer_type, and loaded_at. For a table with 6 columns where the query selects 2, columnar storage reads 33% of the data — a 3x I/O reduction compared to the row-store format that reads 100% of the data regardless of which columns are selected.
Row Group Statistics and Skipping

-- Spark SQL: verify that statistics-based skipping is active
-- The 'number of files read' vs 'number of files scanned' reveals skipping
SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY region;

-- In the Spark UI's SQL tab, check the scan metrics:
-- 'number of files read'    = files that were actually opened and read
-- 'number of files pruned'  = files that were skipped by statistics
-- If the table has 365 daily partitions and the query filters to 1 day,
-- 364 partition directories are pruned (partition pruning, Module 3)
-- Within the surviving partition, row group statistics prune further:
-- if a row group's min(date) > '2025-01-15' or max(date) < '2025-01-15',
-- the entire row group is skipped without reading any data

Statistics-based skipping operates at two levels. Partition pruning (Module 3) eliminates entire directories based on the partition column value. Row group skipping (within a surviving partition) eliminates entire row groups based on the min/max statistics stored in the Parquet footer. At AlpineEnergy, a query filtering on date = '2025-01-15' first prunes to the January partition directory (partition pruning removes 11 of 12 monthly partitions), then within the January partition, row group statistics prune row groups whose date range does not include January 15 — typically eliminating 90% of the remaining row groups. The combined pruning reads approximately 0.3% of the total table data.
Row Group Sizing

The row group size (controlled by parquet.block.size, default 128 MB) determines the granularity of both parallelism and statistics-based skipping. Larger row groups (256 MB) produce fewer tasks and coarser statistics — each row group spans a wider value range, reducing the effectiveness of min/max skipping. Smaller row groups (32 MB) produce more tasks and finer statistics — better skipping but more task scheduling overhead. At AlpineEnergy, the default 128 MB row group size is used for most tables. The SCADA telemetry table (queried with high-selectivity timestamp filters) uses 32 MB row groups because the finer statistics improve skipping effectiveness by 40% for point-in-time queries — worth the 4x increase in task count.

blocks.title: Z-ORDER improves statistics effectiveness
blocks.content: Row group statistics are most effective when values within a row group are clustered — the min/max range is narrow, enabling precise skipping. If a row group contains dates spanning an entire month, the statistics cannot skip any query that filters within that month. Z-ORDER (Delta Lake's OPTIMIZE command) rearranges data within files to co-locate similar values, tightening the min/max range. After Z-ORDERing by date, each row group spans 1-2 days instead of 30 — and min/max statistics can skip row groups for 28-29 of those days. At AlpineEnergy, Z-ORDER on (date, region) reduced the average row group date range from 31 days to 1.5 days, improving skipping effectiveness by 20x for date-filtered queries.

Columnar storage enables two powerful data reduction techniques that row-store cannot use effectively: encoding (representing values in a compact form) and compression (eliminating redundancy across encoded values). These techniques operate within each column chunk independently, and they exploit the fact that values within a single column are the same type, often share patterns (repeated values, sequential numbers, short strings from a small dictionary), and frequently exhibit local sorting. A well-encoded, well-compressed Parquet file can be 5-10x smaller than the equivalent row-store representation — and smaller files mean proportionally less I/O, which directly translates to faster queries.
Encoding Types

rows: Maps each distinct value to a small integer; stores integers instead of values
Low cardinality: < 10,000 distinct values per column chunk
Stores (value, count) pairs for consecutive repeated values
Sorted or clustered data with long runs of the same value
region after Z-ORDER: 'BERN-NORTH' × 45,000 rows → 1 pair = 45,000x reduction
Stores the first value and deltas (differences) for subsequent values
Monotonically increasing values: timestamps, serial IDs, dates
date column: deltas are 0 or 1 (consecutive days) → 1-bit per value
Packs small integers into minimal bits (3-bit values packed 10 per 32-bit word)
Integer columns with narrow value ranges
Dictionary-encoded customer_type: values 0-2 need only 2 bits each

blocks.content: Parquet applies encoding per column chunk automatically based on the data characteristics. Dictionary encoding is the default for low-cardinality string columns (Parquet falls back to plain encoding when the dictionary exceeds a size threshold). RLE and bit-packing are applied to integer columns and to dictionary-encoded columns. Delta encoding is applied to integer and timestamp columns that are sorted or nearly sorted. The encoding is chosen at write time (during Spark SQL's write stage or Delta Lake's OPTIMIZE) and cannot be changed without rewriting the file. At AlpineEnergy, the meter_readings table achieves an overall encoding ratio of 4.2x — the encoded column chunks are 4.2x smaller than the raw data before compression is applied.
Compression Codecs

After encoding, Parquet applies a compression codec to each page (the smallest I/O unit within a column chunk). The codec choice is a trade-off between compression ratio (smaller files = less I/O), compression speed (slower writes), and decompression speed (slower reads). Snappy (the Spark SQL default) provides fast compression and decompression with moderate compression ratio (~2x). Zstd provides better compression ratio (~3-4x) with slightly slower decompression. LZ4 provides the fastest decompression with the lowest compression ratio (~1.5x). Gzip provides high compression (~4x) with slow decompression — rarely used for analytical workloads where read speed matters more than file size.
Codec Selection

-- Spark SQL: set the compression codec for writes
SET spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec = zstd;

-- Write with Zstd compression
CREATE TABLE alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings_zstd
USING DELTA
AS SELECT * FROM alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings;

-- Compare file sizes:
-- Snappy: 8.0 TB (default codec)
-- Zstd:   5.2 TB (35% smaller)
-- The 2.8 TB savings reduces storage cost by ~EUR 840/year at cloud storage rates
-- Read performance: Zstd decompression adds ~5% overhead vs Snappy
-- The 35% I/O reduction more than compensates for the 5% CPU overhead

At AlpineEnergy, the Silver layer uses Zstd compression (best ratio for archival data that is read frequently but written once per day). The Gold layer uses Snappy (fastest decompression for dashboard queries where latency matters more than storage cost). The Bronze layer uses Zstd (raw data is rarely read, so storage cost dominates). The codec choice is documented in the table's data product manifest (Module 5) and enforced by the OPTIMIZE command's configuration — changing codecs requires rewriting the entire table, which is a maintenance window operation.
The Combined Encoding + Compression Pipeline

The total data reduction is the product of encoding and compression ratios. AlpineEnergy's meter_readings table: raw row-store size = ~25 TB (1.4 billion rows × ~18 bytes per row). After columnar encoding: ~6 TB (4.2x encoding ratio). After Zstd compression: ~5.2 TB (1.15x additional compression on the already-encoded data — encoding already eliminated most redundancy). The total reduction is ~4.8x from the raw row-store equivalent. For a query that selects 2 of 6 columns with a filter that prunes to 1% of rows: the actual I/O is approximately 5.2 TB × 0.33 (column pruning) × 0.01 (row group skipping + partition pruning) = ~17 GB. The same query on the PostgreSQL heap would read approximately 10.9 GB of the relevant partition — but with all 6 columns, producing 3.3x more I/O than the columnar path.

blocks.title: Encoding is free performance; compression is a trade-off
blocks.content: Encoding reduces data size without any CPU overhead at read time — dictionary lookup is a single array access, RLE expansion is trivial, and delta reconstruction is an addition. Compression adds CPU overhead proportional to the data volume read. The optimal strategy: maximize encoding (sort data before writing to enable RLE and delta encoding) and choose compression based on the read/write ratio (Zstd for read-heavy Silver/Gold, Snappy for latency-sensitive dashboards). At AlpineEnergy, Z-ORDER before write improved encoding ratios by 2.3x — because co-locating similar values creates longer RLE runs and tighter delta sequences.

Delta Lake adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel to Parquet files. The mechanism is a transaction log — a sequence of JSON files stored alongside the Parquet data files that records every change to the table: files added, files removed, schema changes, and metadata updates. Every Delta Lake operation (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, OPTIMIZE) is a transaction that appends a new entry to the log. Readers construct the table's current state by reading the log and determining which Parquet files are currently active (added but not removed). This log-based architecture enables concurrent reads and writes, snapshot isolation, and the data skipping that makes Delta Lake competitive with traditional databases for analytical queries.
The Transaction Log

# Delta Lake table directory structure
alpha_ch/silver/meter_readings/
├── _delta_log/
│   ├── 00000000000000000000.json    # Version 0: initial table creation
│   ├── 00000000000000000001.json    # Version 1: first data insert
│   ├── 00000000000000000002.json    # Version 2: second insert
│   ├── ...
│   ├── 00000000000000000010.checkpoint.parquet  # Checkpoint at version 10
│   └── 00000000000000001428.json    # Latest version
├── date=2025-01-01/
│   ├── part-00000-abc123.snappy.parquet
│   └── part-00001-def456.snappy.parquet
├── date=2025-01-02/
│   ├── part-00000-ghi789.snappy.parquet
│   └── ...
└── ...

# Each JSON log entry contains:
# { "add": { "path": "date=2025-01-15/part-00000-xyz.parquet",
#             "size": 134217728,
#             "stats": "{\"numRecords\":1200000,
#                        \"minValues\":{\"daily_kw\":0.0},
#                        \"maxValues\":{\"daily_kw\":4821.5}}" } }

Each log entry is a JSON file containing add and remove actions. An INSERT adds new Parquet files (add actions). An UPDATE or DELETE adds new files (with the updated/remaining rows) and removes old files (remove actions marking the original files as deleted). The remove action does not physically delete the Parquet file — it marks it as logically removed. Physical deletion occurs later during VACUUM. This copy-on-write mechanism means that reads always see a consistent snapshot: they read the log up to a specific version and construct the list of active files for that version. Concurrent writes resolve conflicts using optimistic concurrency control — the second writer checks whether its changes conflict with the first writer's changes and retries if necessary.
Checkpoints

Reading 1,428 JSON log files to construct the current table state would be slow. Delta Lake solves this with checkpoints: every 10 versions (configurable), a checkpoint file is written that consolidates all active file actions into a single Parquet file. Readers find the latest checkpoint, load it, and then read only the JSON log files after the checkpoint. For version 1,428, the reader loads the checkpoint at version 1,420 and reads 8 JSON files — instead of 1,428 files. At AlpineEnergy, the meter_readings table has approximately 1,400 log versions (one per daily pipeline run × 3 years + OPTIMIZE runs). Without checkpoints, listing the table state would require reading 1,400 JSON files from cloud storage (approximately 15 seconds). With checkpoints, it requires reading 1 checkpoint file + 8 JSON files (approximately 0.5 seconds).
Data Skipping with File Statistics

Each add action in the transaction log includes per-file statistics: numRecords (row count), minValues and maxValues for the first 32 columns (by default), and nullCount per column. When a query filters on a column with statistics, Delta Lake evaluates the filter against each file's statistics without opening the file. If the file's min/max range does not overlap with the filter value, the file is skipped entirely. This is the Delta Lake equivalent of Parquet row group skipping — but operates at the file level using the transaction log's statistics rather than requiring a Parquet footer read. At AlpineEnergy, data skipping on the date column eliminates 97% of files for single-day queries, and skipping on (date, region) after Z-ORDER eliminates 99.4% of files.

blocks.title: Statistics are only collected for the first 32 columns by default
blocks.content: Delta Lake's delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols defaults to 32. For tables with more than 32 columns, columns beyond position 32 have no statistics and no data skipping. At AlpineEnergy, the SCADA telemetry table has 48 columns. The platform team reordered the columns in the schema definition so that the 8 most frequently filtered columns appear in the first 8 positions — ensuring they have statistics. The alternative: increase dataSkippingNumIndexedCols to 48, at the cost of larger transaction log entries and slower log reads.

Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, and Apache Hudi are the three open table formats that add ACID transactions to Parquet files. All three solve the same fundamental problem: Parquet files alone have no concept of transactions, schema enforcement, or concurrent access. Each format adds a metadata layer that provides these capabilities — but the implementations differ in architecture, capability, and ecosystem integration. The format choice is a long-term architectural commitment: migrating between formats requires rewriting all data files and metadata, which at terabyte scale is a multi-week project. Understanding the differences enables an informed choice — and prevents the format lock-in that occurs when the choice is made based on ecosystem familiarity rather than architectural fit.
The Three Architectures

rows: Metadata files + manifest files in metadata/ directory; catalog integration via REST, Hive, or Nessie
Timeline + metadata in .hoodie/ directory; Hive metastore integration
Add/rename/drop columns; type widening. Enforced at write time.
Full schema evolution including column reordering and type promotion. Catalog-level enforcement.
Schema evolution via Avro schema. Add columns only without special config.
Partitions are immutable once set. Repartitioning requires table rewrite.
Partition evolution: change partition scheme without rewriting data. Hidden partitioning.
Partitions tied to storage layout. Repartitioning requires clustering.
Via version number or timestamp. Retention controlled by VACUUM.
Via snapshot ID or timestamp. Snapshot expiration independent of data files.
Via timeline. Savepoints for specific versions.
Strongest in Databricks/Spark. Growing support in Trino, Flink, Presto.
Engine-agnostic by design. Strong in Spark, Trino, Flink, Dremio, Snowflake.
Strongest in Spark and Flink. Upsert/CDC optimized.

blocks.content: The most architecturally significant difference is partition evolution. Delta Lake's partition scheme is fixed at table creation — changing it requires rewriting the entire table (Module 3, lesson 7). Iceberg supports hidden partitioning: the partition scheme is defined in metadata, not in the directory structure, and can be changed without rewriting data. A date-partitioned Iceberg table can be re-partitioned by (date, country) by updating the metadata — new data is written with the new scheme, and old data remains in the old scheme. Queries seamlessly span both partition schemes. This capability eliminates the most operationally expensive migration in data platform engineering.
AlpineEnergy's Format Decision

AlpineEnergy standardized on Delta Lake for three reasons. First, ecosystem alignment: the Spark SQL cluster runs on Databricks, which provides native Delta Lake integration with optimizations (predictive I/O, liquid clustering) not available for Iceberg or Hudi. Second, operational maturity: Delta Lake has been in production at Databricks customers for 5+ years, and the operational tooling (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, DESCRIBE HISTORY) is well-documented. Third, team expertise: the engineering team has 3 years of Delta Lake experience and zero Iceberg experience — the migration cost exceeds the partition evolution benefit at current scale. The decision is recorded in ADR-007 with the expiration condition: 'Revisit if AlpineEnergy migrates off Databricks, or if partition evolution becomes a quarterly operational burden exceeding 40 engineering hours.'
When to Choose Iceberg Instead

Iceberg is the stronger choice for engine-agnostic platforms that use Spark, Trino, and Flink interchangeably (Iceberg's catalog API provides consistent access across engines). It is also the stronger choice for tables that require frequent partition evolution (changing partition schemes without rewrites), and for organizations that want to avoid Databricks vendor lock-in. At AlpineEnergy, the Italian subsidiary evaluated Iceberg for their analytical tables (they use Trino, not Spark) — the evaluation concluded that Iceberg's Trino integration was superior to Delta Lake's, and the subsidiary's new tables use Iceberg. The cross-format interoperability is managed through UniForm (Delta Lake's Iceberg compatibility layer) for tables that both teams consume.

blocks.title: Format convergence is happening
blocks.content: Delta Lake's UniForm generates Iceberg metadata alongside Delta metadata, enabling Iceberg-compatible engines to read Delta tables. Iceberg's broad engine support is making it the lingua franca for multi-engine platforms. The practical implication: choose based on your primary engine's native support, and use compatibility layers for cross-engine access. The formats are converging in capability — the differences that matter most are ecosystem integration and operational tooling, not fundamental architecture.

Both PostgreSQL and Spark SQL at AlpineEnergy store data on cloud storage: PostgreSQL on Azure Managed Disks (block storage attached to the VM) and Spark SQL on Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS, an object store). The two storage types have fundamentally different performance characteristics. Block storage provides low-latency random access (~1ms per read) and consistent throughput — behaving like a local SSD. Object storage provides high-throughput sequential access (multi-GB/s aggregate throughput) but high-latency per-request access (~50-100ms per GET request) and no random access within an object. These characteristics determine why PostgreSQL (which relies on random page access) runs on block storage and why Spark SQL (which performs large sequential reads) runs on object storage.
Object Store I/O Characteristics

rows: 50-100ms first byte; proportional to object size after
PostgreSQL index lookups need low latency → block storage
500 MB/s - 2 GB/s depending on disk tier
60 MB/s per stream (ADLS); scales with parallelism
Spark SQL's parallel reads aggregate to multi-GB/s on object storage
50-200ms per LIST request; 1,000 objects per page
Delta Lake checkpoint avoids listing; raw Parquet listing is expensive
Strong (reads see all prior writes immediately)
Strong (ADLS Gen2, S3 since 2020; GCS always)
Delta Lake's optimistic concurrency relies on strong consistency
Provisioned: pay for capacity whether used or not
Consumption: pay per GB stored + per request
Object storage is 3-5x cheaper per GB for large datasets

blocks.content: The key implication: object storage throughput scales with parallelism, not with single-stream speed. A single Spark SQL task reading one Parquet file from ADLS gets 60 MB/s. Ten tasks reading 10 files simultaneously get 600 MB/s aggregate. One hundred tasks get 6 GB/s. This is why Spark SQL's partition-parallel architecture (one task per row group or per partition) is fundamentally matched to object storage: more partitions = more parallel reads = higher aggregate throughput. PostgreSQL, which uses a single backend process per query, cannot exploit this parallelism — it reads one page at a time from block storage, which provides low-latency single-page access that object storage cannot match.
Optimizing Spark SQL on Object Storage

Three optimizations reduce object storage overhead for Spark SQL. First, file size: larger files (128 MB-1 GB) reduce the per-request overhead by amortizing the 50-100ms first-byte latency over more data. Small files (< 10 MB) make the first-byte latency a significant fraction of the total read time. Delta Lake's OPTIMIZE command compacts small files into larger ones — the primary maintenance operation for object storage performance. Second, listing avoidance: Delta Lake's transaction log eliminates the need for directory listing (which costs 50-200ms per LIST request on object storage). Third, predicate pushdown to the scan: reading only the relevant row groups and column chunks from each file minimizes the bytes transferred from object storage.
When NOT to Use Object Storage for SQL

Object storage is inappropriate for low-latency point lookups (50-100ms first-byte latency is unacceptable for a customer portal query that must respond in 20ms), for workloads that perform many small random reads (each read incurs the full first-byte latency), and for PostgreSQL (which requires the block-level random access that object storage does not provide). At AlpineEnergy, the customer portal's meter lookup (SELECT * FROM meters WHERE meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142') runs on PostgreSQL with block storage — returning in 2ms. The same query on Spark SQL with object storage would take 200ms minimum (first-byte latency + Parquet footer read + column chunk read). The 100x latency difference is the fundamental reason for the dual-engine architecture.

blocks.title: Monitor object storage request costs separately from compute costs
blocks.content: Object storage charges per-request (GET, PUT, LIST) in addition to per-GB. A Spark SQL query that opens 10,000 small Parquet files incurs 10,000 GET requests — which at cloud pricing can cost more than the compute time. At AlpineEnergy, the monthly object storage bill is 35% storage and 65% requests — because the small-file problem (before OPTIMIZE was automated) generated millions of unnecessary GET requests. After automating OPTIMIZE (Module 3, lesson 6), the request cost dropped by 70% and the total storage bill by 45%.

Module 1 (lesson 2) introduced the dual-engine architecture: PostgreSQL for OLTP, Spark SQL for analytics. This lesson addresses the storage-level question underneath that decision: why row-store (PostgreSQL heap) is optimal for some workloads and columnar (Parquet/Delta) is optimal for others. The answer is not 'row-store for writes, columnar for reads' — it is more nuanced. Row-store is optimal for queries that access most columns of a few rows (point lookups, narrow-range scans). Columnar is optimal for queries that access few columns of many rows (aggregations, wide scans). The crossover point depends on the column count, the selectivity, and the access pattern.
The I/O Analysis

-- Scenario: meter_readings table, 6 columns, 130M rows in one month's partition

-- Query A: Point lookup (1 meter, 1 day)
-- Columns accessed: all 6
-- Rows accessed: 1
-- Row-store: 1 index lookup + 1 page read = 2 random reads (~2ms)
-- Columnar:  1 footer read + 6 column chunk reads = 7 sequential reads (~400ms)
-- Winner: Row-store (200x faster)

-- Query B: Analytical aggregation (all meters, 1 month, 2 columns)
-- Columns accessed: 2 of 6 (region, daily_kw)
-- Rows accessed: 37.2M
-- Row-store: scan all 6 columns × 37.2M rows = 3.0 GB I/O
-- Columnar:  scan 2 columns × 37.2M rows = 0.6 GB I/O (after encoding: ~0.15 GB)
-- Winner: Columnar (4-20x faster depending on encoding effectiveness)

-- Query C: Narrow scan with most columns (100 meters, 1 month, 5 of 6 columns)
-- Columns accessed: 5 of 6
-- Rows accessed: 3,100 (100 meters × 31 days)
-- Row-store: 3,100 index lookups + 3,100 page reads (~600ms with caching)
-- Columnar:  row group skipping to ~10 row groups + 5 column chunks = ~500 MB scan
-- Winner: Row-store (the narrow selectivity makes index access efficient)

The I/O analysis reveals the crossover: row-store wins when the query is selective (few rows) and accesses most columns. Columnar wins when the query is wide (many rows) and accesses few columns. The crossover depends on the column count: with 6 columns, columnar's 2-column scan reads 33% of the data. With 50 columns (like the SCADA telemetry table), a 3-column scan reads 6% — and the columnar advantage increases to 16x. At AlpineEnergy, the engine-routing decision matrix from Module 1 is grounded in this I/O analysis: queries that access < 30% of columns and > 1 million rows are routed to Spark SQL; queries that access > 50% of columns or < 10,000 rows are routed to PostgreSQL.
The Write Path Difference

Row-store supports efficient single-row writes: INSERT appends a tuple to the current page, UPDATE creates a new version on the same or a nearby page (HOT), DELETE marks the tuple as dead. Each operation modifies a few pages and writes a small WAL record. Columnar storage supports efficient batch writes: INSERT appends a batch of rows as a new Parquet file, but single-row inserts are inefficient (each insert creates a new file, fragmenting the table). UPDATE and DELETE in Delta Lake use copy-on-write: the entire file containing the modified rows is rewritten. A single-row DELETE on a 128 MB file rewrites 128 MB. At AlpineEnergy, the pipeline writes to Spark SQL in daily batches (4.8 million rows per batch = efficient columnar writes). The customer portal writes to PostgreSQL in single-row operations (meter status updates = efficient row-store writes).
The Hybrid Architecture

AlpineEnergy's hybrid approach is not a compromise — it is the optimal architecture for their workload mix. PostgreSQL handles the OLTP workload: point lookups, narrow-range scans, single-row writes, and transactional consistency across related tables. Spark SQL handles the analytical workload: wide aggregations, multi-terabyte scans, batch transformations, and columnar-efficient compression. Data flows from PostgreSQL to Spark SQL via the CDC pipeline (Module 9) for analytical consumption. Data flows from Spark SQL to PostgreSQL via materialized writes for operational consumption (the customer portal reads pre-computed summaries from PostgreSQL). The hybrid is justified at AlpineEnergy's scale (3.5 TB PostgreSQL + 8 TB Delta Lake). Below 500 GB total, PostgreSQL alone handles both workloads adequately with read replicas and parallel query.

blocks.title: Storage format determines engine choice, not the reverse
blocks.content: The question is not 'should we use PostgreSQL or Spark SQL?' — it is 'should this table be row-store or columnar?' If the primary access pattern is point lookups and narrow-range scans, the table should be row-store (PostgreSQL). If the primary access pattern is wide aggregations and multi-column scans, the table should be columnar (Delta Lake on Spark SQL). The engine follows the storage format. AlpineEnergy's engine-routing matrix is fundamentally a storage-format-routing matrix: it routes queries to the engine that stores the data in the format optimal for that query's access pattern.

AlpineEnergy stores personally identifiable information (PII) in multiple tables: meter addresses, customer names, and billing details. Swiss FDPA, Austrian DSG, and EU GDPR require that PII is protected at rest (encrypted on disk), in transit (encrypted over the network), and in use (masked or restricted based on the querying user's role). Column-level encryption and dynamic masking are the storage-layer mechanisms that implement these requirements. This lesson covers the implementation across both engines: PostgreSQL's pgcrypto extension and column-level privileges, Parquet Modular Encryption for Delta Lake, and the dynamic masking views that present different data to different roles.
PostgreSQL Column Encryption

-- Column-level encryption using pgcrypto
-- Encrypt sensitive columns at write time, decrypt at read time
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pgcrypto;

-- Encrypt the meter address at INSERT
INSERT INTO silver.meter_details (meter_id, address_encrypted)
VALUES (
  'CH-BERN-00142',
  pgp_sym_encrypt('Kramgasse 15, 3011 Bern', current_setting('app.encryption_key'))
);

-- Decrypt for authorized roles
SELECT meter_id,
       pgp_sym_decrypt(address_encrypted, current_setting('app.encryption_key')) AS address
FROM   silver.meter_details
WHERE  meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142';

-- The encryption key is stored in PostgreSQL's session variables
-- (set by the application at connection time, never stored in the database)
-- SET app.encryption_key = 'key-from-vault';

pgcrypto provides symmetric encryption (pgp_sym_encrypt / pgp_sym_decrypt) using AES-256.
