The encryption key is stored in an external vault (Azure Key Vault at AlpineEnergy) and injected into the PostgreSQL session via a SET command at connection time. The encrypted column is stored as BYTEA — indexes on the encrypted column do not support range queries or equality lookups (the encrypted values are opaque). At AlpineEnergy, the meter_details table encrypts address and customer_name. The meter_id (the join key) is not encrypted because it must be joinable. The trade-off: encrypting join keys prevents efficient joins, so PII that appears in join keys (rare at AlpineEnergy) is pseudonymized instead of encrypted.
Dynamic Masking via Views

-- Dynamic masking: different roles see different data
-- The billing team sees full addresses; the analytics team sees masked addresses

CREATE VIEW gold.v_meter_details_masked AS
SELECT meter_id,
       CASE
         WHEN current_user IN ('swiss_billing', 'platform_admin')
           THEN pgp_sym_decrypt(address_encrypted, current_setting('app.encryption_key'))
         ELSE LEFT(pgp_sym_decrypt(address_encrypted, current_setting('app.encryption_key')), 3)
              || '***'
       END AS address,
       customer_type,
       region
FROM   silver.meter_details;

The masking view presents full PII to authorized roles (billing, platform admin) and masked PII to everyone else. The analytics team sees 'Kra***' instead of 'Kramgasse 15, 3011 Bern' — enough to verify data quality (the address starts with 'Kra') but not enough to identify the customer. At AlpineEnergy, masking views exist for every table containing PII columns. The views are the only access path for non-privileged roles — direct table access is denied via REVOKE. The masking logic is enforced by the database, not by the application — preventing the scenario where a notebook or ad-hoc query bypasses application-level masking.
Parquet Modular Encryption

Parquet Modular Encryption (PME) encrypts individual columns within Parquet files, allowing different columns to be encrypted with different keys. A file can have meter_id in plaintext (for data skipping and join pushdown), daily_kw encrypted with one key (accessible to the billing team), and address encrypted with a different key (accessible only to the privacy team). PME is natively supported by Spark SQL 3.2+ and Delta Lake. At AlpineEnergy, PME is applied to the Silver meter_details Delta Lake table: address and customer_name columns are encrypted with a key managed by Azure Key Vault. The encryption is transparent to Spark SQL queries — authorized sessions decrypt automatically; unauthorized sessions see NULL values for encrypted columns.
Performance Impact of Encryption

Column encryption adds CPU overhead for every encrypt (at write time) and decrypt (at read time) operation. For AES-256, the overhead is approximately 3-5% of total query time for small columns (strings under 100 bytes). For large columns (JSON payloads, documents), the overhead can reach 15-20% because the encryption processes every byte. At AlpineEnergy, the performance impact of encrypting address and customer_name (average 40 bytes each) is 2.1% — measured by comparing identical queries with and without encryption on the staging database. The platform team's encryption policy: encrypt all PII columns regardless of performance impact (regulatory requirement takes precedence), but monitor the performance overhead quarterly and evaluate hardware acceleration (AES-NI instructions) if the overhead exceeds 10%.

blocks.title: Encryption does not replace access control
blocks.content: Encrypted columns are decryptable by any session that has the encryption key. If the key is injected into every session (for convenience), every user can decrypt every column — and the encryption provides only at-rest protection, not in-use protection. At AlpineEnergy, the encryption key is injected only into sessions belonging to roles that are authorized to see PII. Analytics sessions (which do not need PII) do not receive the key and see encrypted columns as opaque BYTEA — even if they query the base table directly. The masking view provides a human-readable masked alternative; the key restriction provides the cryptographic enforcement.

Storage format migration — converting data from one physical format to another — occurs when a table moves between engines (PostgreSQL to Delta Lake for analytical offloading), when a table changes formats within an engine (CSV Bronze to Delta Silver), or when an organization standardizes on a new format (the Italian subsidiary's MySQL tables migrating to the AlpineEnergy standard). Each migration involves three concerns: data fidelity (preserving every value without precision loss or type conversion errors), consumer continuity (downstream pipelines must continue functioning during and after the migration), and operational feasibility (the migration must complete within the maintenance window without disrupting production workloads).
PostgreSQL to Delta Lake Migration

-- Migration pattern: PostgreSQL table → Delta Lake table
-- Step 1: Initial snapshot via Spark JDBC read
CREATE TABLE alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings
USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (date)
AS SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type,
         loaded_at
FROM   postgresql_source.silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2022-01-01';  -- 3 years of history

-- Step 2: Verify data fidelity
-- Compare row counts per partition
SELECT 'postgresql' AS source, date, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM   postgresql_source.silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2022-01-01'
GROUP BY date
UNION ALL
SELECT 'delta', date, COUNT(*)
FROM   alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings
GROUP BY date;
-- Any date with mismatched counts indicates a migration error

-- Step 3: Verify type preservation
-- Spot-check that NUMERIC precision, TIMESTAMPTZ timezone, and VARCHAR length
-- survived the cross-engine transfer (Module 5, lesson 7 type mapping)

The initial snapshot reads the entire PostgreSQL table via JDBC and writes it to Delta Lake. For AlpineEnergy's 3.5 TB table, this takes approximately 6 hours with 32 Spark SQL executor cores reading in parallel (each partition is read by a separate task). The verification step compares per-partition row counts between the source and target — any mismatch indicates a data transfer error (typically a timeout or a type conversion failure). The type mapping from Module 5 (lesson 7) is applied during the transfer: NUMERIC(18,4) → DecimalType(18,4), TIMESTAMPTZ → TimestampType with explicit UTC conversion, VARCHAR(N) → StringType with length validation.
Ongoing Synchronization

After the initial snapshot, ongoing changes to the PostgreSQL table must be replicated to Delta Lake. Three approaches exist: nightly full-table overwrite (simplest but most expensive — rewrites the entire Delta table daily), incremental append (uses the loaded_at timestamp to identify new rows since the last sync), and CDC-based replication (uses logical replication to capture INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE events and apply them to Delta Lake). At AlpineEnergy, the meter_readings table uses incremental append (new daily readings are appended; historical readings are immutable). The meters dimension uses CDC-based replication (dimension changes must be captured as SCD Type 2 events). The choice is driven by the table's update pattern: append-only tables use incremental append; mutable tables use CDC.
Consumer Migration

The data migration is the mechanical part. The consumer migration is the organizational part: every pipeline, dashboard, and notebook that reads from the PostgreSQL table must be updated to read from the Delta Lake table — or a compatibility layer must bridge the two. At AlpineEnergy, the consumer migration follows the dual-write pattern from Module 3 (lesson 7): during the transition period, both the PostgreSQL table and the Delta Lake table contain the same data. Consumers migrate at their own pace within a 60-day window. After all consumers have migrated, the PostgreSQL table is either decommissioned (if no OLTP workload remains) or retained as the operational source with CDC feeding the analytical copy.
The Italian Subsidiary Migration

The most complex migration at AlpineEnergy: converting the Italian subsidiary's MySQL database to the AlpineEnergy standard (PostgreSQL for OLTP, Delta Lake for analytics). The migration involved: schema translation (MySQL AUTO_INCREMENT → PostgreSQL SERIAL, MySQL DATETIME → PostgreSQL TIMESTAMPTZ with timezone correction, MySQL ENUM → PostgreSQL CHECK constraint), data transfer (mysqldump → CSV → PostgreSQL COPY for the initial snapshot), and consumer migration (Italian dashboards migrated from MySQL Workbench to the shared Grafana platform). The total migration took 14 weeks: 4 weeks for schema translation and testing, 2 weeks for initial data transfer and verification, and 8 weeks for consumer migration across the Italian team's 15 pipelines.

blocks.title: Test type mapping before migrating production data
blocks.content: Every cross-engine migration introduces type mapping risks: precision loss in numeric types, timezone shift in timestamp types, and constraint loss in string types. At AlpineEnergy, the Italian migration's staging test revealed that MySQL's DATETIME (no timezone) was being stored as local Italian time (CET/CEST), while PostgreSQL's TIMESTAMPTZ expects UTC. Without the staging test, 14 months of Italian meter readings would have been shifted by 1-2 hours — producing billing errors for every Italian customer. The fix: explicit timezone conversion during the transfer (CONVERT_TZ(datetime_col, 'Europe/Rome', 'UTC')).

This module covered the storage layer across both engines: PostgreSQL's heap format with page layout, tuple alignment, and TOAST; Parquet's columnar structure with row groups, column chunks, and statistics-based skipping; the encoding and compression pipeline that reduces columnar data to 20-25% of its row-store equivalent; Delta Lake's transaction log with checkpoints, data skipping, and optimistic concurrency; the table format landscape with Delta Lake, Iceberg, and Hudi; cloud storage performance characteristics and optimization; the row-store vs columnar decision framework; column encryption and dynamic masking for PII protection; and storage format migration across engines and formats. This final lesson synthesizes these into AlpineEnergy's storage architecture.
The Storage Architecture Summary

rows: Point lookups, narrow scans, single-row writes, transactional consistency
Alignment-optimal column ordering, fill factor tuning, HOT updates
Partition by ingestion date, minimal OPTIMIZE (data is rewritten to Silver)
Validated, conformed data, EEDA standard compliance
Z-ORDER on (date, region), monthly OPTIMIZE, statistics on first 32 columns
Delta Lake (Parquet + Snappy) or PostgreSQL materialized views
Snappy for read latency, frequent OPTIMIZE, consumer-specific partitioning
Encrypted PII columns, dynamic masking views
Key rotation quarterly, masking views as the only access path

blocks.content: The Deep Internals Phase Complete

Modules 6 and 7 form the deep internals phase. Module 6 covered the PostgreSQL execution engine: processes, MVCC, WAL, buffers, VACUUM, locks, indexes, connections, and extensions. Module 7 covered the storage layer: heap format, Parquet format, encoding and compression, Delta Lake transactions, table format comparison, cloud storage characteristics, the row-store vs columnar decision, encryption, and format migration. Together, these modules provide the implementation-level understanding that enables diagnosis of problems invisible from EXPLAIN: buffer cache misses, MVCC bloat, WAL generation spikes, lock manager contention, row group statistics ineffectiveness, and the I/O characteristics that determine why the same logical query has dramatically different performance on different storage formats.

The enterprise operations phase (Modules 8-10) shifts from understanding the engines to governing the platform. Module 8 covers multi-tenant security with cross-border regulatory compliance — the policies and mechanisms that control who can access what data across 3 countries and 10 teams. Module 9 covers CDC and streaming SQL architecture — the data flows that keep the two engines synchronized and enable near-real-time analytics. Module 10 covers enterprise governance and capacity planning — the organizational frameworks that sustain the platform over years as data grows, teams evolve, and regulatory requirements change.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 8 applies the engine internals from Modules 6-7 to the security domain. Row-level security in PostgreSQL uses the same visibility rules as MVCC (a row is visible only if the current user's RLS policy allows it). Column encryption uses the storage mechanisms from lesson 8 at the implementation level. Audit logging uses the WAL and extension hooks from Module 6. The internals knowledge from this phase is not academic — it is the foundation for every security, compliance, and governance mechanism in the enterprise operations phase.

modules.title: Multi-Tenant Operations and Security
modules.description: Design and operate a secure, multi-tenant SQL platform — row-level security, column-level masking, audit logging, cross-border data residency, and the compliance frameworks that govern data access in regulated energy industries across three national jurisdictions.

blocks.content: At mid-level, HydroAlpes had one PostgreSQL database serving 3 teams. Security was straightforward: 3 roles with GRANT/REVOKE on schemas. At AlpineEnergy with 10 teams across 3 countries, each country's energy regulator mandates different access controls. The Swiss FDPA requires that personal data is accessible only to Swiss-authorized personnel. The Austrian DSG requires audit logs for every PII access. The Italian GDPR implementation requires explicit consent records for data processing. These requirements cannot be satisfied by GRANT/REVOKE alone — they require architectural isolation between tenants (countries and teams) at the database, schema, and row level. The multi-tenancy model determines how that isolation is achieved and what it costs in operational complexity.
The Three Multi-Tenancy Models

rows: Maximum: separate PostgreSQL instances per country
Highest: 3 instances to deploy, monitor, backup, upgrade
Used for Italian regulatory isolation (separate PostgreSQL instance in Italy)
Moderate: separate schemas within one instance
Moderate: one instance but N schemas with separate GRANT policies
Used for country-level data separation (alpineenergy_ch, alpineenergy_at schemas)
Minimum: one schema, row-level policies filter per user/role
Lowest: one schema, one set of tables, policy-based filtering
Used for team-level access within a country (analytics vs billing vs ML)

blocks.content: AlpineEnergy's Hybrid Model

AlpineEnergy uses all three models simultaneously, layered by isolation requirement. At the top level, the Italian subsidiary has a separate PostgreSQL instance (database-per-tenant) — required by the Italian GDPR implementation's data residency mandate that personal data must reside on Italian infrastructure. Within the Swiss/Austrian instance, each country has its own database (alpineenergy_ch, alpineenergy_at) — providing schema-per-tenant isolation that satisfies Swiss FDPA's requirement for segregated access control. Within each country's database, teams access data through RLS policies that filter rows based on the querying role — providing shared-schema isolation between the billing team, the analytics team, and the ML team without duplicating tables.

The hybrid model reflects a fundamental principle: the isolation mechanism should match the isolation requirement. Regulatory data residency (data must not leave the country) requires physical isolation — separate instances on separate infrastructure. Regulatory access control (authorized personnel only) requires logical isolation — separate schemas with separate GRANT policies. Team-level access control (billing sees different rows than analytics) requires row-level isolation — RLS policies within shared tables. Using stronger isolation than required (separate instances for team-level separation) wastes resources. Using weaker isolation than required (RLS for data residency) violates regulations.
When NOT to Use Multi-Tenancy

Multi-tenancy adds operational complexity at every level: more roles to manage, more policies to maintain, more audit logs to review, and more failure modes to diagnose (a query returns zero rows — is it a data issue or an RLS policy issue?). For platforms with fewer than 3 teams, no cross-border operations, and no regulatory access control requirements, a single-tenant architecture with GRANT/REVOKE is simpler and sufficient. At AlpineEnergy, the Swiss-only platform operated as single-tenant for 18 months before the Austrian expansion introduced the first multi-tenancy requirement. The Italian expansion 6 months later introduced the second. Multi-tenancy was adopted incrementally — not preemptively.

blocks.title: Multi-tenancy is a regulatory response, not an architectural preference
blocks.content: No platform architect would choose to operate 3 PostgreSQL instances, 6 schemas with separate GRANT policies, and 15 RLS policies if a single database with simple permissions sufficed. Multi-tenancy exists because regulators require it: data residency mandates physical isolation, access control mandates logical isolation, and audit requirements mandate traceability. The architecture follows the regulation — not the other way around. Understanding each regulator's actual requirements (not just the general intent) determines the minimum viable isolation level.

PostgreSQL's Row-Level Security (RLS) adds invisible WHERE clauses to every query based on the querying role. An analytics team member querying silver.meter_readings sees only the rows that their RLS policy permits — typically rows for their assigned country or region. The policy is enforced by the database engine, not by the application or the view layer — meaning no query, no notebook, and no ad-hoc psql session can bypass it. RLS is the mechanism that converts AlpineEnergy's team-level access rules into database-enforced reality. At enterprise scale, the challenge is not enabling RLS — it is managing the complexity of multiple overlapping policies, measuring their performance impact, and diagnosing query behavior when policies interact unexpectedly.
Policy Architecture

-- Enable RLS on the meter_readings table
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
-- FORCE ensures that table owners are also subject to RLS

-- Country-level policy: Swiss roles see only Swiss data
CREATE POLICY country_isolation ON silver.meter_readings
  FOR ALL
  USING (
    CASE current_user
      WHEN 'swiss_billing'     THEN region LIKE 'BERN%' OR region LIKE 'ZURICH%'
      WHEN 'austrian_analytics' THEN region LIKE 'INNSBRUCK%' OR region LIKE 'SALZBURG%'
      WHEN 'platform_admin'     THEN TRUE  -- platform admin sees all
      ELSE FALSE  -- deny by default
    END
  );

-- Team-level policy: billing sees all customer types; analytics sees aggregated only
CREATE POLICY team_access ON silver.meter_readings
  FOR SELECT
  USING (
    CASE
      WHEN current_user IN ('swiss_billing', 'austrian_billing') THEN TRUE
      WHEN current_user IN ('swiss_analytics', 'austrian_analytics')
        THEN customer_type <> 'INDUSTRIAL'  -- industrial data restricted for analytics
      ELSE TRUE
    END
  );

Two policies are active simultaneously: country_isolation restricts by geography, team_access restricts by data sensitivity. PostgreSQL combines multiple policies with AND by default (restrictive mode) or OR (permissive mode). AlpineEnergy uses the default AND combination: a Swiss analytics user must satisfy both policies — seeing only Swiss data (country policy) AND only non-industrial data (team policy). The platform_admin role bypasses both policies (TRUE in both USING clauses). The ELSE FALSE default-deny ensures that any role not explicitly listed sees zero rows — failing closed rather than open.
Performance Impact of RLS

Each RLS policy adds a filter condition to every query against the table. The filter is evaluated per-row during the scan — identical in cost to a WHERE clause in the query itself. For policies that reference indexed columns (region LIKE 'BERN%'), the optimizer can use the index to accelerate the filter. For policies that reference non-indexed columns or use functions, the filter becomes a post-scan filter that cannot benefit from indexes. At AlpineEnergy, the country_isolation policy filters on region (indexed) — the RLS overhead is < 1% because the optimizer pushes the policy condition into the index scan. A proposed policy that filtered on a computed expression (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = ...) was rejected during review because it would bypass the date index and force a sequential scan.
Diagnosing RLS-Related Query Issues

The most common RLS debugging scenario at AlpineEnergy: a new team member reports that their query returns zero rows on a table that other team members can query successfully. The cause is almost always a missing role mapping — the member's database role is not listed in any RLS policy's USING clause, so the default-deny (ELSE FALSE) returns zero rows. The fix: add the role to the appropriate policy. The platform team's onboarding checklist includes 'verify RLS visibility for every table the new member needs to access' — catching the issue before the member wastes time debugging their query.

blocks.title: RLS policies are invisible to the querying user
blocks.content: A query filtered by RLS returns a valid, non-empty result set — just not the full result set. The user sees no error, no warning, and no indication that rows were filtered. This is by design (security through transparency would defeat the purpose), but it creates a diagnostic challenge: the user believes the data is incomplete because their query is wrong, not because a policy restricted their view. At AlpineEnergy, every RLS-protected table has a companion view (v_meter_readings_access_check) that returns the current user's effective access scope — enabling self-service diagnosis without requiring platform team involvement.

RLS controls which rows a user sees. Column-level security controls which columns a user sees — or more precisely, which columns contain readable values versus masked or encrypted values. The Swiss billing team needs the full meter address (for invoicing). The analytics team needs the region and customer type (for aggregation) but not the address (no analytical need for PII). The ML team needs meter consumption patterns (for demand forecasting) but not any identifying information. Column-level security ensures that each team sees exactly the columns they need, with PII masked or hidden for teams that do not need it.
PostgreSQL Column-Level GRANT

-- Column-level privileges: grant SELECT on specific columns only
-- The analytics team can read meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type
-- but NOT address_encrypted or customer_name_encrypted

REVOKE ALL ON silver.meter_details FROM austrian_analytics;
GRANT SELECT (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type)
  ON silver.meter_details TO austrian_analytics;

-- If the analytics role tries to SELECT address_encrypted:
-- ERROR: permission denied for table meter_details
-- Note: the error does not specify WHICH column is denied (security through obscurity)

Column-level GRANT is the simplest column security mechanism: the role physically cannot read the restricted column. The limitation: the error message when accessing a denied column is generic ('permission denied for table'), which can confuse users who have access to some columns but not all. At AlpineEnergy, column-level GRANT is used for the strictest PII restriction (encrypted address and customer name columns). For columns that should be visible but masked (showing 'Kra***' instead of the full address), the dynamic masking view from Module 7 (lesson 8) is used instead — because GRANT does not support partial column visibility.
Dynamic Masking Architecture

Dynamic masking presents different column values to different roles without maintaining separate copies of the data. The masking logic lives in a view or a function that evaluates the current user's role and returns either the full value or a masked version. At AlpineEnergy, three masking levels exist: full (authorized roles see the complete value), partial (semi-authorized roles see a truncated or obfuscated value), and null (unauthorized roles see NULL). The masking view is the only access path — direct table access is revoked for all non-admin roles. This architecture ensures that masking cannot be bypassed by querying the base table directly.
Masking in Spark SQL

-- Spark SQL: dynamic masking via view with session property
-- The session's 'user_role' property determines the masking level
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW alpine_ch.gold.v_meter_details_masked AS
SELECT meter_id,
       daily_kw,
       date,
       region,
       customer_type,
       CASE
         WHEN current_user() IN ('billing_service', 'platform_admin')
           THEN address
         WHEN current_user() LIKE '%_analytics'
           THEN CONCAT(LEFT(address, 3), '***')
         ELSE NULL
       END AS address
FROM   alpine_ch.silver.meter_details;

Spark SQL does not support PostgreSQL-style column-level GRANT or RLS natively. Column security in Spark SQL is implemented through views that evaluate the current user (via current_user() or a session configuration property) and apply masking logic. The view is registered in the catalog as the consumer-facing table; the underlying Delta table is accessible only to the pipeline service account. At AlpineEnergy, every Spark SQL table with PII columns has a masking view. The platform team's governance policy requires that the view name follows the convention v_{table_name}_masked, making it immediately obvious that the view applies masking.
When NOT to Use Dynamic Masking

Dynamic masking adds a view layer between every consumer and the base table, which introduces maintenance overhead (views must be updated when the base table schema changes) and a minor performance cost (the CASE evaluation per row). For tables with no PII columns, the masking view adds complexity without benefit. At AlpineEnergy, only 12 of 320 tables contain PII columns and require masking views. The remaining 308 tables are accessed directly without a masking layer. The governance policy: masking views are required for tables flagged as 'contains PII' in the schema registry (Module 5); all other tables are exempt.

blocks.title: Test masking with every role before deployment
blocks.content: A masking view that returns full PII for an unintended role is a data breach — not a bug. At AlpineEnergy, the CI test suite includes a masking verification test for every masking view: the test connects as each registered role, queries the view, and asserts that the PII column returns the expected masking level (full, partial, or NULL). The test has caught 2 masking errors in 12 months: once when a new role was added without updating the CASE expression, and once when a view was recreated from a template that defaulted to full access.

The Austrian DSG and Swiss FDPA both require that organizations can demonstrate who accessed personal data, when, and for what purpose. GDPR extends this to the right of access: a data subject can request a report of all accesses to their data. These requirements mandate audit logging — a persistent, tamper-resistant record of every data access event. PostgreSQL's built-in logging (log_statement = 'all') records every SQL statement but produces enormous log volumes and does not capture which specific rows or columns were accessed. The pgaudit extension provides fine-grained audit logging that records the specific objects accessed, the operation type, and the querying role — at a manageable volume and in a structured, queryable format.
pgaudit Configuration

pgaudit supports two auditing modes. Session auditing (pgaudit.log per role) logs all operations by specific roles — useful for auditing high-risk roles like analytics teams that access PII. Object auditing (pgaudit.log per table) logs all access to specific tables — useful for auditing PII-containing tables regardless of which role accesses them. At AlpineEnergy, both modes are active: object auditing on the 12 PII-containing tables (logging every access regardless of role) and session auditing on the 6 analytics and ML roles (logging their access to any table). The dual approach ensures that PII access is captured even if a role accesses a PII table through a view or a function that pgaudit's object-level tracking might miss.
Audit Log Management

Audit logs at enterprise scale generate significant volume. AlpineEnergy's 10 teams executing approximately 50,000 queries per day produce approximately 200,000 audit log entries per day (each query may access multiple objects). At 500 bytes per entry, the daily audit volume is approximately 100 MB — 36 GB per year. The audit logs are shipped from PostgreSQL's log files to a dedicated audit database (a separate PostgreSQL instance with read-only access for the compliance team) via a log shipping pipeline. The compliance team queries the audit database for access reports, incident investigations, and regulatory filings. The audit database has a 7-year retention period (the maximum required by any of the three regulators) — longer than the 3-year retention for operational data.
Spark SQL Audit Integration

Spark SQL does not have a pgaudit equivalent. Audit logging for Spark SQL queries is implemented through the Spark event listener API: a custom listener captures QueryExecutionEvent records (containing the SQL text, the user, the tables accessed, and the timestamp) and writes them to the audit database. At AlpineEnergy, the Spark SQL audit listener is deployed as a cluster-level configuration (spark.sql.queryExecutionListeners) that activates for every session. The listener captures the logical plan's referenced tables — providing table-level audit granularity. Column-level audit granularity (which specific columns were accessed) requires parsing the SQL text, which the listener does for PII-flagged tables using a pattern matcher against the schema registry's PII column list.

blocks.title: Audit logs must be tamper-resistant
blocks.content: An audit log that can be modified by the same roles it audits has no compliance value. At AlpineEnergy, the audit database is a separate PostgreSQL instance with a dedicated admin role that no operational team can access. The log shipping pipeline writes via an append-only role that can INSERT but not UPDATE or DELETE. The compliance team has SELECT-only access. This separation of duties ensures that no single role can both access PII and modify the audit record of that access — satisfying the non-repudiation requirement of all three regulators.

Data residency is the regulatory requirement that certain data must not leave its country of origin. Swiss personal data must reside on Swiss infrastructure. Austrian personal data must reside on Austrian infrastructure. Italian personal data must reside on Italian infrastructure. This is not access control (who can read the data) — it is physical location control (where the data is stored and processed). The architectural challenge: AlpineEnergy's cross-country analytics (the Platinum enterprise dashboard, the cross-border demand forecast) require joining data from all three countries. If Swiss data cannot leave Switzerland, how does the dashboard join it with Austrian data?
The Residency Architecture

AlpineEnergy satisfies data residency through three complementary mechanisms. First, physical isolation: PII-containing tables (meter addresses, customer names, billing details) reside on country-specific infrastructure — the Swiss PostgreSQL instance on Azure Switzerland North, the Austrian instance on Azure Austria East, the Italian instance on Azure Italy North. Second, anonymized cross-border transfer: non-PII data (aggregated consumption, region-level statistics, meter readings without addresses) is replicated to the shared analytical platform without residency restrictions. Third, federated query: when cross-country analysis requires PII (rare — primarily regulatory investigations), the query federates to each country's instance, performs the PII processing locally, and returns only the anonymized or aggregated result.
Implementing Residency in the Pipeline

-- The CDC pipeline enforces residency: PII columns are stripped before cross-border transfer
-- Swiss Silver → Shared Delta Lake (PII stripped)
CREATE TABLE alpine_shared.silver.meter_readings_anonymized
USING DELTA AS
SELECT meter_id,       -- pseudonymized: hash(meter_id || salt) in production
       daily_kw,        -- non-PII: consumption data
       date,
       region,
       customer_type
       -- address, customer_name EXCLUDED — PII stays in Swiss instance
FROM   alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings;

-- The Platinum enterprise dashboard joins anonymized cross-country data
SELECT country, region, SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM (
  SELECT 'CH' AS country, region, daily_kw FROM alpine_shared.silver.meter_readings_ch_anon
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'AT', region, daily_kw FROM alpine_shared.silver.meter_readings_at_anon
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'IT', region, daily_kw FROM alpine_shared.silver.meter_readings_it_anon
) AS combined
GROUP BY country, region;

The anonymized cross-border transfer strips PII columns before replicating data to the shared platform. The enterprise dashboard joins the anonymized copies — which is legally permissible because the joined data contains no PII. The meter_id is pseudonymized (hashed with a country-specific salt) to prevent cross-country re-identification. At AlpineEnergy, the anonymization pipeline runs as a dedicated DAG step after the Silver refresh, producing country-specific anonymized tables that the shared Spark SQL cluster can access. The anonymization logic is reviewed quarterly by the compliance team to verify that no PII column was inadvertently included.
Federated Cross-Border Queries

For regulatory investigations that require PII across countries (rare — approximately 3-5 per year), the federated query pattern executes the PII-processing portion locally in each country's database and transfers only the aggregated or anonymized result. The query is submitted through a governance workflow: the compliance team approves the query, the platform team executes it under audit logging, and the result is reviewed before delivery. The federated execution ensures that raw PII never crosses the border — only the investigation's specific, approved output. At AlpineEnergy, the federated investigation pipeline uses PostgreSQL's dblink extension to connect to each country's instance, execute the approved query locally, and aggregate the results on the requesting country's instance.
Residency Verification

Residency compliance is verified by two mechanisms. The infrastructure audit verifies that PostgreSQL instances are deployed in the correct Azure regions (Switzerland North, Austria East, Italy North) — this is a cloud configuration check performed quarterly. The data flow audit verifies that no PII column appears in cross-border transfers — this is a pipeline analysis performed by the compliance team using the column-level lineage graph from Module 5 (lesson 8). The lineage graph traces every column from its source table through every transformation to its destination table. If a PII column (flagged in the schema registry) appears in a cross-border destination, the audit fails and the pipeline is blocked until the PII column is removed from the transfer.

blocks.title: Residency restricts infrastructure, not analytics
blocks.content: Data residency does not prevent cross-country analytics — it prevents cross-country PII transfer. AlpineEnergy's enterprise dashboard, demand forecast, and capacity planning all operate on cross-country data. The key: the cross-country data is anonymized. The anonymization boundary (stripping PII before cross-border transfer) is the architectural decision that makes cross-country analytics possible within residency constraints. Every cross-border data flow at AlpineEnergy passes through this boundary — there are no exceptions.

Security isolation controls who can access data. Resource isolation controls how much compute each team can consume. Without resource isolation, one team's expensive query can starve another team's time-sensitive pipeline. At AlpineEnergy, the billing pipeline has a platinum SLA (must complete by 06:00 UTC), but the analytics team's ad-hoc notebooks have no SLA. If an analytics notebook runs a full-table scan that consumes all available CPU and I/O during the pipeline window, the billing pipeline slows from 15 minutes to 45 minutes — breaching the SLA. Resource isolation prevents this by allocating compute capacity to teams with guaranteed minimums and configurable maximums.
PostgreSQL Resource Management

PostgreSQL does not natively support resource groups or compute quotas. Resource isolation is achieved through connection pooling configuration (Module 6, lesson 8): separate PgBouncer pools with different work_mem settings, statement_timeout limits, and max_connections per pool. The billing pipeline connects through a dedicated pool with work_mem = 512 MB, statement_timeout = 0 (no timeout), and a guaranteed 4 backend slots. The analytics team connects through a separate pool with work_mem = 64 MB, statement_timeout = 30s, and a shared 15 backend slots. The ML team connects through a third pool with work_mem = 256 MB, statement_timeout = 300s, and 5 backend slots. This pool-based isolation is coarser than true resource groups but effective — the billing pipeline always has its 4 backends available regardless of analytics team activity.
Spark SQL Resource Management

Spark SQL provides finer-grained resource management through dynamic resource allocation and scheduler pools. Each team is assigned a scheduler pool with a guaranteed minimum number of executor cores and a maximum that can be exceeded when other pools are idle. The billing pipeline's pool has min = 16 cores, max = 32 cores. The analytics pool has min = 8 cores, max = 24 cores. The ML pool has min = 8 cores, max = 16 cores. During the nightly pipeline window (03:00-06:00), the billing pool uses its guaranteed 16 cores while the analytics and ML pools are idle — so the billing pool can burst to 32 cores. During business hours, all three pools compete, and each gets at least its guaranteed minimum.
Priority Scheduling

-- Spark SQL: set the scheduler pool for the billing pipeline session
SET spark.scheduler.pool = billing_platinum;

-- Pool configuration (in fairscheduler.xml):
-- <pool name="billing_platinum">
--   <schedulingMode>FAIR</schedulingMode>
--   <weight>3</weight>        <!-- 3x priority vs analytics -->
--   <minShare>16</minShare>   <!-- guaranteed 16 cores -->
-- </pool>
-- <pool name="analytics_gold">
--   <schedulingMode>FAIR</schedulingMode>
--   <weight>1</weight>
--   <minShare>8</minShare>
-- </pool>

-- PostgreSQL: set statement_timeout per pool
-- billing pool: no timeout (SET statement_timeout = 0)
-- analytics pool: 30-second timeout
-- This prevents runaway analytics queries from consuming resources indefinitely

Priority scheduling ensures that when resource contention occurs (all pools requesting more than the total cluster capacity), higher-priority workloads get resources first. The billing pipeline's weight of 3 means it receives 3x the share of a weight-1 analytics pool during contention. During off-peak hours (no contention), all pools can use available resources up to their maximum. At AlpineEnergy, the weight configuration reflects the SLA tiers: platinum = weight 3, gold = weight 2, silver = weight 1. The configuration is reviewed quarterly alongside the SLA governance review (Module 10).
When NOT to Isolate Resources

Resource isolation reduces total cluster utilization: guaranteed minimums reserve capacity that may sit idle during off-peak hours. For platforms with fewer than 3 teams and no conflicting SLA tiers, a shared resource model with statement_timeout as the only protection (preventing runaway queries) is simpler and achieves higher utilization. At AlpineEnergy, resource isolation was introduced when the analytics team's ad-hoc queries caused two billing SLA breaches in one month. Before that, the shared model with a 60-second statement_timeout was sufficient — the analytics queries were short enough to not conflict with the pipeline. Resource isolation was adopted in response to a measured conflict, not as a preemptive architecture.

blocks.title: Monitor pool utilization, not just pool configuration
blocks.content: A pool with 16 guaranteed cores that consistently uses only 4 is wasting 12 cores that other teams could use. At AlpineEnergy, pool utilization is tracked weekly: guaranteed cores vs actual peak cores per pool. If a pool's peak usage is consistently below 50% of its guarantee for 3 consecutive months, the guarantee is reduced and the freed capacity is reallocated to higher-demand pools. This utilization-driven right-sizing prevents the common pattern of teams requesting generous guarantees 'just in case' and never using them.

sections.title: Encryption at Rest and In Transit

blocks.content: Module 7 (lesson 8) covered column-level encryption for PII columns. This lesson covers the broader encryption architecture: encrypting all data at rest (not just PII columns), encrypting all data in transit (between clients and databases, between databases and storage), and the key management lifecycle that ensures encryption keys are rotated, stored securely, and recoverable. The three regulators (Swiss FDPA, Austrian DSG, Italian GDPR) all require encryption at rest and in transit as baseline controls — not just for PII but for all data classified as confidential or sensitive.
Encryption at Rest

Encryption at rest protects data stored on disk from unauthorized access to the physical storage. At AlpineEnergy, encryption at rest operates at two levels. The storage level: Azure Managed Disks (for PostgreSQL) and Azure Data Lake Storage (for Delta Lake) both provide transparent encryption using Microsoft-managed keys — every byte written to disk is encrypted with AES-256 and decrypted transparently on read. No application or database configuration is required. The column level: PII columns are additionally encrypted with customer-managed keys (Module 7, lesson 8) — providing defense in depth. If the storage-level encryption is compromised (e.g., a storage service vulnerability), the PII data remains protected by the column-level encryption with a different key.
Encryption In Transit

-- PostgreSQL: enforce TLS for all connections
-- postgresql.conf:
-- ssl = on
-- ssl_cert_file = '/etc/ssl/certs/server.crt'
-- ssl_key_file = '/etc/ssl/private/server.key'
-- ssl_min_protocol_version = 'TLSv1.3'

-- pg_hba.conf: reject non-SSL connections
-- hostssl  all  all  0.0.0.0/0  scram-sha-256
-- hostnossl all  all  0.0.0.0/0  reject

-- Verify that a connection is encrypted
SELECT ssl, version AS ssl_version, cipher
FROM   pg_stat_ssl
WHERE  pid = pg_backend_pid();

Encryption in transit protects data flowing between clients and databases over the network. PostgreSQL's ssl = on enables TLS encryption; the pg_hba.conf hostnossl reject rule ensures that unencrypted connections are refused. At AlpineEnergy, all connections (including internal pipeline connections, PgBouncer connections, and cross-engine JDBC connections) require TLS 1.3. The ssl_min_protocol_version = 'TLSv1.3' rejects older TLS versions that have known vulnerabilities. For Spark SQL, the JDBC connection string includes ssl=true and sslmode=require, and the Spark cluster's internal communication (between driver and executors) is encrypted via spark.network.crypto.enabled = true.
Key Management Lifecycle

Encryption is only as strong as the key management. At AlpineEnergy, encryption keys are stored in Azure Key Vault — a hardware security module (HSM)-backed service that provides key generation, storage, rotation, and access logging. The key lifecycle has four stages: generation (keys are created in Key Vault, never exported), rotation (customer-managed keys for column encryption are rotated quarterly — new data is encrypted with the new key, old data is re-encrypted during the next OPTIMIZE cycle), revocation (compromised keys are immediately revoked and replaced), and recovery (key backups enable recovery if Key Vault is unavailable). The key access policy follows least privilege: the pipeline service account can encrypt and decrypt; the platform admin can rotate and revoke; no human role can export the raw key material.
When NOT to Use Customer-Managed Keys

Customer-managed keys add operational overhead: key rotation requires re-encrypting data, key loss prevents data access (permanently, if backups fail), and key management adds a dependency on the vault service's availability. For data that is not regulated (internal pipeline metadata, temporary staging tables, test fixtures), the cloud provider's default encryption with provider-managed keys is sufficient — simpler, cheaper, and equally effective against the threat model (physical disk theft or unauthorized storage access). At AlpineEnergy, customer-managed keys are used only for the 12 PII-containing tables. The remaining 308 tables use provider-managed encryption. The distinction is documented in the schema registry: each table's YAML file includes an encryption_policy field (customer_managed or provider_managed).

blocks.title: Key loss is permanent data loss
blocks.content: If the customer-managed encryption key is lost and no backup exists, every column encrypted with that key is permanently unreadable — the data exists on disk but cannot be decrypted. At AlpineEnergy, key backup is a quarterly tested procedure: the platform team exports a wrapped key backup to a geographically separate Key Vault, verifies that the backup can decrypt a test dataset, and documents the test result. The quarterly key recovery test has never failed — but it exists because the consequence of failure (permanent loss of all PII data) is catastrophic.

Security controls that are not tested are assumptions. An RLS policy that was correct when deployed may have been inadvertently modified during a schema migration. A masking view that correctly hides PII may expose it through a newly created view that queries the base table. A column-level GRANT that restricts access may be circumvented by a function that runs with the definer's privileges (SECURITY DEFINER). Security testing verifies that every control produces the intended result for every role — and that no backdoor exists through views, functions, or indirect access paths. At AlpineEnergy, security tests run in CI alongside the schema contract tests and data quality assertions from earlier modules.
RLS Policy Testing

-- Automated RLS test: verify that each role sees only its permitted data
-- This test runs in CI against the fixture database

-- Test 1: Swiss billing sees only Swiss data
SET ROLE swiss_billing;
SELECT COUNT(*) AS visible_rows,
       COUNT(DISTINCT region) AS visible_regions,
       bool_and(region LIKE 'BERN%' OR region LIKE 'ZURICH%') AS all_swiss
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';
-- Expected: all_swiss = TRUE

-- Test 2: Austrian analytics sees only Austrian non-industrial data
SET ROLE austrian_analytics;
SELECT COUNT(*) AS visible_rows,
       bool_and(region LIKE 'INNSBRUCK%' OR region LIKE 'SALZBURG%') AS all_austrian,
       bool_and(customer_type <> 'INDUSTRIAL') AS no_industrial
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';
-- Expected: all_austrian = TRUE, no_industrial = TRUE

-- Test 3: Unregistered role sees zero rows (default deny)
SET ROLE test_unregistered_role;
SELECT COUNT(*) AS visible_rows
FROM   silver.meter_readings;
-- Expected: 0

RESET ROLE;

Each test impersonates a role (SET ROLE), queries the protected table, and asserts that the result matches the expected access scope. The tests verify both the positive case (the role sees what it should see) and the negative case (the role does not see what it should not see). The default-deny test (test 3) verifies that an unregistered role receives zero rows — the most important security assertion because a missing ELSE FALSE in the policy would grant access to every role not explicitly listed. At AlpineEnergy, the RLS test suite contains 28 tests (14 roles × 2 assertions each) and runs in CI in under 5 seconds against fixture data.
Masking View Testing

Masking view tests verify that PII columns return the correct masking level for each role. The test connects as each role, queries the masking view, and asserts that the PII column value matches the expected pattern: full value for authorized roles (checked against a known fixture value), partial mask for semi-authorized roles (checked against a regex pattern like '^.{3}\*{3}$'), and NULL for unauthorized roles. At AlpineEnergy, the masking test suite additionally verifies that the base table is inaccessible to non-admin roles: the test attempts to SELECT directly from the base table (bypassing the masking view) and asserts that the query returns a permission error.
Privilege Escalation Testing

Privilege escalation occurs when a user gains access beyond their authorized scope through indirect means. At AlpineEnergy, the three most common escalation vectors are: SECURITY DEFINER functions (a function created by a privileged role runs with the creator's privileges, potentially exposing data to callers who lack direct access), view chain escalation (a view owned by a privileged role exposes data from a restricted table to a less-privileged consumer), and COPY TO FILE (an authorized user exports restricted data to a file accessible by unauthorized users). The security test suite includes escalation tests for all three vectors: it verifies that no SECURITY DEFINER function returns PII to non-privileged callers, that no view chain exposes restricted base table data, and that COPY TO FILE is disabled for non-admin roles.

blocks.title: Security testing is continuous, not one-time
blocks.content: A security configuration that was correct yesterday may be incorrect today because a migration modified a policy, a new view created an escalation path, or a new role was added without updating the RLS policies. At AlpineEnergy, security tests run on every CI build (catching configuration changes), weekly as a scheduled regression suite (catching drift from manual changes), and quarterly as a penetration-style assessment (the platform team attempts to access data they should not see through creative SQL). Each layer catches a different class of security regression.

A security event is any unauthorized or anomalous data access: a role accessing PII it should not see, an unusually large data export from a restricted table, a failed authentication attempt from an unknown IP, or a privilege escalation detected by the security test suite. The incident response process for security events is more urgent and more structured than the operational incident response from the mid-level track (Module 10, lesson 9) because regulatory notification deadlines apply. GDPR requires notification of the supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovering a data breach. Swiss FDPA requires notification 'as soon as possible.' The incident response must produce an assessment within hours, not days.
The Security Incident Workflow

AlpineEnergy's security incident workflow has five stages. Stage 1 (Detect): the audit log monitoring system flags an anomalous access pattern — a role accessing a PII table it has not accessed before, or a data export volume exceeding the baseline by 10x. Stage 2 (Contain): the platform team immediately revokes the suspicious role's access (ALTER ROLE ... NOLOGIN) and kills active sessions (pg_terminate_backend). Stage 3 (Assess): the compliance team queries the audit log to determine the scope — which data was accessed, how much, and whether it includes personal data that triggers the notification obligation. Stage 4 (Notify): if the assessment determines that personal data was compromised, the compliance team files the regulatory notification within the 72-hour GDPR deadline. Stage 5 (Remediate): the platform team identifies the root cause (misconfigured RLS policy, missing GRANT revocation, escalation vulnerability) and deploys the fix.
Audit-Based Forensics

-- Forensic query: what did the suspicious role access in the last 72 hours?
-- Run against the audit database (separate instance, tamper-resistant)
SELECT audit_timestamp,
       username,
       command,
       object_type,
       object_name,
       LEFT(statement, 200) AS query_preview
FROM   audit.pgaudit_log
WHERE  username = 'suspicious_role'
  AND  audit_timestamp >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '72 hours'
ORDER BY audit_timestamp DESC;

-- Scope assessment: did the role access any PII-flagged tables?
SELECT DISTINCT a.object_name, r.classification
FROM   audit.pgaudit_log AS a
  JOIN platform.schema_registry AS r
    ON a.object_name = r.table_name
WHERE  a.username = 'suspicious_role'
  AND  a.audit_timestamp >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '72 hours'
  AND  r.classification = 'pii';

The forensic queries extract the suspicious role's complete access history from the audit database. The first query shows every operation with timestamps. The second query cross-references the accessed tables against the schema registry's PII classification to determine whether personal data was exposed. If any PII-classified table appears in the result, the GDPR notification obligation is triggered. At AlpineEnergy, the forensic query template is pre-written and stored in the security incident runbook — the on-call engineer fills in the role name and the time window, runs the query, and sends the result to the compliance team. The entire forensic analysis takes under 10 minutes.
Post-Incident Security Review

Every security incident at AlpineEnergy produces a security-specific post-incident review within 5 business days. The review extends the standard blameless post-mortem (mid-level Module 10, lesson 9) with security-specific questions: what control failed (RLS policy, GRANT configuration, masking view, or audit detection)? Why did it fail (human error in configuration, schema migration side effect, or design gap)? What test would have caught it (RLS assertion, masking verification, escalation test, or audit anomaly detection)? The answer to the third question becomes a new automated test added to the security test suite — making the same class of incident progressively harder to repeat.

blocks.title: The 72-hour clock starts at discovery, not at confirmation
blocks.content: GDPR's 72-hour notification deadline begins when the organization becomes aware of the breach — not when it confirms the breach. A 48-hour investigation that concludes 'yes, this was a breach' leaves only 24 hours for the notification filing. AlpineEnergy's policy: file a preliminary notification within 24 hours of detection (stating the suspected scope) and a detailed notification within 72 hours (stating the confirmed scope and remediation). The preliminary filing preserves regulatory compliance while the investigation continues. Waiting for complete investigation before filing risks missing the deadline.

This module covered multi-tenant security across the complete SQL platform: the hybrid tenancy model with database, schema, and row-level isolation matched to regulatory requirements; RLS policy architecture with performance-aware design and diagnostic tooling; column-level security and dynamic masking across both PostgreSQL and Spark SQL; audit logging with pgaudit and Spark SQL event listeners feeding a tamper-resistant audit database; cross-border data residency with anonymized transfer and federated query patterns; workload management with pool-based resource isolation in PostgreSQL and scheduler pools in Spark SQL; encryption at rest and in transit with customer-managed key lifecycle; security testing with CI-integrated RLS, masking, and escalation tests; and incident response with 72-hour-aware forensic procedures.
The Security Architecture Summary

rows: PostgreSQL RLS policies; Spark SQL view-based filtering
pgaudit + Spark SQL event listener → audit database
All PII table access + all analytics/ML role activity
Monthly audit log review by compliance
Azure storage encryption (all data) + column encryption (PII)
TLS 1.3 for all connections; Spark internal encryption

blocks.content: The security architecture is layered: each control addresses a different threat at a different level. Physical isolation prevents cross-border data movement at the infrastructure level. Row-level security prevents unauthorized row access at the query level. Column-level security and dynamic masking prevent unauthorized PII exposure at the field level. Audit logging provides non-repudiation and forensic capability at the access level. Encryption at rest and in transit prevents unauthorized access to storage media and network traffic. Resource isolation prevents denial-of-service through compute exhaustion. No single control is sufficient — the layered approach ensures that a failure in one control does not compromise the entire security posture.

The security posture is not static — it evolves with the platform. When AlpineEnergy added the Italian subsidiary, the security architecture expanded: a new physical instance, new RLS policies for Italian roles, new masking views for Italian PII columns, a new audit logging scope, and a new residency verification pipeline. The expansion was implemented through the same migration and deployment process as any other schema change (Module 5, Module 7 of the mid-level track) — security configuration is code, reviewed in PRs, tested in CI, and deployed through staging to production.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 9 covers the data flows that connect the security-isolated components: CDC and streaming SQL architecture. The CDC pipeline that replicates Swiss data to the shared analytical platform must respect the anonymization boundary from lesson 5. The streaming materialized views that provide near-real-time analytics must enforce the same RLS policies as the batch views. The exactly-once delivery guarantees must work across the security boundary — a failed CDC event must not be lost, duplicated, or visible to unauthorized roles during retry. Security and data flow are inseparable at enterprise scale.

modules.title: Advanced Streaming and CDC Architecture
modules.description: Design and operate CDC and streaming SQL pipelines at enterprise scale — PostgreSQL logical replication, Debezium connector architecture, streaming materialized views, event sourcing patterns, and the exactly-once delivery guarantees that make streaming SQL production-reliable.

blocks.content: AlpineEnergy's batch architecture refreshes the Gold billing summary nightly at 05:30 UTC. The grid operations team sees yesterday's data at 06:00. The billing team processes yesterday's consumption at 07:00. For billing, this latency is acceptable — invoices are generated monthly. For grid operations, it is not: a substation overload that occurs at 14:00 is invisible to the monitoring dashboard until 06:00 the next morning — 16 hours of blindness during which the overload may cause equipment damage. The operations team needs the data within 15 minutes, not 16 hours. This gap between the nightly batch latency and the operational freshness requirement is the architectural motivation for streaming: processing data as it arrives rather than accumulating it for a nightly batch.
The Batch-Streaming Spectrum

rows: Lowest: single pipeline run, full recompute
Billing summary, regulatory filings, capacity forecast
Grid operations dashboard, regional consumption alerts
High: connector management, schema propagation, exactly-once
Meter status updates, real-time grid monitoring
Highest: event processing, state management, backpressure
Not used at AlpineEnergy (no sub-second requirement)

blocks.content: The spectrum reveals a fundamental trade-off: lower latency requires higher complexity and weaker consistency guarantees. Each step down the spectrum adds architectural components (connectors, state stores, watermark management), failure modes (connector failures, state corruption, exactly-once violations), and operational burden (monitoring streaming lag, managing consumer offsets, handling schema evolution in flight). The architectural decision is not 'batch or streaming' — it is 'what is the minimum latency that satisfies the business requirement, and what is the maximum complexity the team can operate?' At AlpineEnergy, billing stays on nightly batch (latency is irrelevant, consistency is critical). Grid operations uses micro-batch (15-minute freshness satisfies the requirement, micro-batch complexity is manageable). Only meter status uses near-real-time CDC (online/offline events must be visible within seconds for grid stability).
When NOT to Stream

Streaming adds persistent infrastructure (Kafka cluster, Debezium connectors, streaming Spark jobs) that must run 24/7, be monitored for lag, and be recoverable from failures. If the freshness requirement is 4 hours or more, a batch pipeline that runs every 3 hours provides the same freshness with dramatically lower complexity — no connectors, no state stores, no exactly-once concerns. At AlpineEnergy, the platform team rejected 3 streaming proposals in 12 months because the stated freshness requirement (1 hour) could be met by increasing the batch frequency from daily to hourly. The streaming architecture was approved only for the 2 use cases where the batch approach could not meet the requirement: grid operations (15-minute freshness) and meter status (seconds-level freshness).

blocks.title: Streaming is not better than batch — it is more expensive than batch
blocks.content: A streaming pipeline that produces the same result as a nightly batch is not an improvement — it is the same result at 5x the operational cost. Streaming is justified only when the latency reduction has measurable business value: preventing equipment damage (grid operations), reducing customer complaint response time (meter status), or satisfying a regulatory real-time reporting mandate. At AlpineEnergy, the streaming business case for grid operations was quantified: 15-minute freshness prevents an estimated EUR 120,000/year in overload-related equipment damage. The streaming infrastructure costs approximately EUR 18,000/year. The 6.7x return justified the investment.

PostgreSQL's Write-Ahead Log (Module 6, lesson 3) records every data change for durability and crash recovery. Logical replication decodes the WAL into a stream of logical change events — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — and transmits them to subscribers. Unlike physical replication (which streams raw WAL bytes for identical replica maintenance), logical replication produces structured change events that can be consumed by different systems: another PostgreSQL instance, a Kafka topic via Debezium (lesson 3), or a Spark Structured Streaming job (lesson 4). Logical replication is the foundation of AlpineEnergy's CDC architecture — the mechanism that extracts changes from the PostgreSQL source of truth and feeds them to the analytical platform.
Publications and Subscriptions

-- PostgreSQL logical replication setup
-- Step 1: Configure the source for logical replication
-- postgresql.conf:
-- wal_level = logical  (required — default is 'replica')
-- max_replication_slots = 10
-- max_wal_senders = 10

-- Step 2: Create a publication on the source (Swiss PostgreSQL)
CREATE PUBLICATION alpine_ch_cdc
  FOR TABLE silver.meter_readings, silver.meters, silver.meter_status
  WITH (publish = 'insert, update, delete');

-- Step 3: Create a replication slot (reserves WAL for the consumer)
SELECT pg_create_logical_replication_slot('debezium_ch', 'pgoutput');

-- Step 4: Verify the slot is active and WAL is retained
SELECT slot_name, active, restart_lsn,
       pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) / (1024*1024) AS retained_mb
FROM   pg_replication_slots
WHERE  slot_name = 'debezium_ch';

The publication declares which tables' changes should be replicated. The replication slot reserves WAL segments for the consumer — ensuring that WAL is not recycled before the consumer has read it. The retained_mb metric shows how much WAL the slot is holding. At AlpineEnergy, the Swiss publication covers 8 tables: the 3 Silver fact tables (meter_readings, meter_status, grid_events) and the 5 dimension tables (meters, regions, substations, regulatory_zones, tariffs). The publication uses publish = 'insert, update, delete' — capturing all DML operations. DDL changes (ALTER TABLE) are not captured by logical replication and must be handled separately (lesson 7).
The Replication Slot Problem

Replication slots are the most operationally dangerous feature of logical replication. A slot that is not consumed (because the Debezium connector is down, or the Kafka cluster is unreachable) continues to retain WAL — preventing PostgreSQL from recycling old WAL segments. If the consumer is offline for 24 hours during AlpineEnergy's nightly pipeline (which generates ~2 GB of WAL per hour), the slot retains 48 GB of WAL. If the disk fills, PostgreSQL halts — no writes, no queries, a complete outage. At AlpineEnergy, replication slot monitoring is a critical alert: if a slot's retained WAL exceeds 10 GB (approximately 5 hours of backlog), a PagerDuty alert fires. If it exceeds 30 GB, the on-call engineer drops the slot to prevent disk exhaustion — accepting that the consumer will need a full re-snapshot to recover.
Logical Decoding Output Plugins

The logical decoding process converts WAL records into a consumable format using an output plugin. PostgreSQL ships with pgoutput (the default, used by native logical replication and Debezium's pgoutput connector). The wal2json plugin produces JSON change events — useful for direct consumption by non-PostgreSQL systems. The decoderbufs plugin produces Protocol Buffers — compact and fast for high-throughput scenarios. At AlpineEnergy, the Debezium connector uses pgoutput (native, no additional plugin installation required). The platform team evaluated wal2json for a direct-to-Spark integration (bypassing Kafka) but rejected it because the JSON overhead was 3x larger than pgoutput's binary format at AlpineEnergy's 4.8 million events per day volume.

blocks.title: Monitor replication slots as critical infrastructure
blocks.content: A forgotten replication slot from a decommissioned consumer is a ticking time bomb: it retains WAL indefinitely, invisible to normal monitoring unless specifically checked. At AlpineEnergy, the quarterly infrastructure audit includes a replication slot inventory: every slot must have a registered consumer in the platform registry. Orphaned slots (no registered consumer) are dropped immediately. The platform team's automation creates and drops slots as part of the CDC pipeline deployment — never manually.

Debezium is an open-source CDC platform that reads change events from database replication logs and publishes them to Kafka topics. It provides a standardized abstraction over different database replication protocols — PostgreSQL's logical decoding, MySQL's binlog, and others — producing a uniform change event format regardless of the source database. At AlpineEnergy, Debezium connects to each country's PostgreSQL instance, reads the logical replication stream via the pgoutput plugin, and publishes change events to Kafka topics organized by table name. The Spark Structured Streaming job (lesson 4) subscribes to these topics and applies the changes to Delta Lake.
The Debezium Architecture

blocks.title: AlpineEnergy CDC architecture with Debezium
blocks.content:
PostgreSQL (CH)          Debezium Connector          Kafka           Spark Structured Streaming
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────────────────────┐
│ WAL (logical │────▶│ PostgreSQL Connector  │───▶│ Topic:   │───▶│ Read Kafka stream         │
│  decoding)   │     │                      │    │ ch.silver│    │ Apply to Delta Lake       │
│              │     │ • Reads pgoutput     │    │ .meter_  │    │ • INSERT → append         │
│ Publication: │     │ • Converts to Avro   │    │ readings │    │ • UPDATE → merge          │
│ 8 tables     │     │ • Publishes to Kafka │    │          │    │ • DELETE → delete          │
└──────────────┘     │ • Tracks offset      │    │ Topic:   │    │                          │
                     └──────────────────────┘    │ ch.silver│    │ Exactly-once via           │
PostgreSQL (AT)                                  │ .meters  │    │ Kafka offsets + Delta      │
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────┐    │          │    │ transactions              │
│ WAL          │────▶│ PostgreSQL Connector  │───▶│ ...      │    └──────────────────────────┘
└──────────────┘     └──────────────────────┘    └──────────┘

PostgreSQL (IT)
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────┐
│ WAL          │────▶│ PostgreSQL Connector  │───▶ (same pattern)
└──────────────┘     └──────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: Each country's PostgreSQL instance has a dedicated Debezium connector that reads the logical replication stream and publishes to Kafka. Each published table has its own Kafka topic. The Spark Structured Streaming job subscribes to the topics and applies changes to the corresponding Delta Lake tables. The architecture runs 3 connectors (one per country) publishing to approximately 24 Kafka topics (8 tables × 3 countries).

blocks.content: Snapshot Mode

When a Debezium connector starts for the first time (or after a replication slot is dropped and recreated), it performs a snapshot: reading the entire current state of each published table and publishing it as a batch of INSERT events. This initial snapshot provides the baseline state; subsequent WAL events provide the incremental changes. At AlpineEnergy, the initial snapshot of the Swiss meter_readings table (1.4 billion rows) takes approximately 4 hours. During the snapshot, the connector holds a short-lived ACCESS SHARE lock on each table (preventing DDL but not DML) — the pipeline continues to write new data while the snapshot reads historical data. After the snapshot completes, the connector switches to streaming mode and processes WAL events in real time.
Schema Change Handling

Debezium captures data changes but handles schema changes (ALTER TABLE) differently. When a column is added, Debezium's schema registry is updated and subsequent events include the new column. When a column is renamed, Debezium sees it as a column drop (old name disappears) and a column add (new name appears) — which can confuse downstream consumers that expect column continuity. When a column type changes, Debezium propagates the new type in the schema — but the Kafka topic may contain events with both the old and new types, requiring the Spark consumer to handle the type variation. At AlpineEnergy, all schema changes to CDC-published tables follow the SCR process (Module 5, lesson 4) with an additional CDC impact assessment: the connector configuration is updated before the schema change is applied to production.
Connector Monitoring

# Key Debezium metrics to monitor (exposed via JMX / Prometheus)

# Lag: time between the WAL event and its publication to Kafka
# debezium_metrics_MilliSecondsBehindSource
# Threshold: WARNING at 60s, CRITICAL at 300s

# Throughput: events processed per second
# debezium_metrics_NumberOfEventsFiltered
# Baseline: ~55 events/sec during day, ~200 events/sec during pipeline window

# Snapshot status: percentage complete during initial snapshot
# debezium_metrics_SnapshotCompleted (boolean)
# debezium_metrics_RemainingTableCount (during snapshot)

# Replication slot retention: WAL retained by the slot
# Monitored via PostgreSQL pg_replication_slots (lesson 2)

The MilliSecondsBehindSource metric is the primary health indicator: it measures the delay between a change occurring in PostgreSQL and its publication to Kafka. Under normal operation at AlpineEnergy, this metric is under 5 seconds. During the nightly pipeline window (when the write volume spikes), it may reach 15-30 seconds — acceptable for the 15-minute freshness SLA. A sustained lag above 60 seconds triggers investigation (connector may be throttled, Kafka may be slow). A lag above 300 seconds triggers a critical alert because the slot WAL retention is growing and may approach the disk exhaustion threshold.

blocks.title: Deploy one connector per source instance, not per table
blocks.content: Debezium's PostgreSQL connector reads the entire logical replication stream for the instance and filters by publication. Deploying one connector per table creates multiple replication slots on the same instance — each retaining WAL independently, multiplying the WAL retention risk. At AlpineEnergy, each country has exactly one connector reading from one publication (covering all 8 tables). The Kafka topic routing is handled by Debezium's topic configuration — not by multiple connectors.

sections.title: Streaming SQL with Spark Structured Streaming

blocks.content: Spark Structured Streaming processes Kafka topics as streaming DataFrames — applying the same SQL transformations that the batch pipeline uses, but on a continuous basis. The streaming query reads change events from Kafka, applies transformations (filtering, joining, aggregating), and writes the results to Delta Lake. The key architectural insight: the same SQL logic that the batch Gold pipeline uses can be reused in the streaming pipeline with minimal modification. The difference is not the transformation — it is the execution model: batch processes a complete dataset once, streaming processes an incremental dataset continuously.
The Streaming Pipeline Pattern

-- Spark SQL: streaming read from Kafka CDC topic
-- Read the Swiss meter readings CDC events
CREATE OR REPLACE STREAMING TABLE alpine_ch.streaming.meter_readings_stream
AS SELECT
  from_json(CAST(value AS STRING), 
    'meter_id STRING, daily_kw DOUBLE, date DATE, region STRING, customer_type STRING, op STRING'
  ) AS event
FROM STREAM(read_kafka(
  bootstrapServers => 'kafka:9092',
  subscribe => 'ch.silver.meter_readings'
));

-- Streaming aggregation: 15-minute regional consumption windows
CREATE OR REPLACE STREAMING TABLE alpine_ch.gold.regional_consumption_15min
AS SELECT
  event.region,
  window(event_time, '15 minutes') AS time_window,
  SUM(event.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
  COUNT(DISTINCT event.meter_id) AS active_meters
FROM alpine_ch.streaming.meter_readings_stream
WHERE event.op IN ('c', 'u')  -- CREATE and UPDATE events only (not DELETE)
GROUP BY event.region, window(event_time, '15 minutes');

The streaming table reads Kafka events, deserializes the JSON payload, and writes a continuous stream of meter readings. The downstream streaming aggregation computes 15-minute regional consumption summaries — the data that the grid operations dashboard needs. The window function groups events into 15-minute windows; the WHERE clause filters to INSERT and UPDATE events (DELETE events indicate meter decommissioning, which should not reduce the consumption total). The streaming pipeline runs continuously — not triggered by a scheduler — producing updated results every micro-batch interval (default 100ms, configured to 30 seconds at AlpineEnergy to reduce compute cost).
Watermarking and Late Data

Streaming events can arrive out of order: a meter reading for 14:00 may arrive at 14:03 because of network delays between the meter and the data collector. Without watermarking, the streaming engine must keep every window open indefinitely — waiting for potentially late events. Watermarking declares a threshold: events arriving more than N minutes after the window boundary are discarded. At AlpineEnergy, the watermark is set to 10 minutes: a 15-minute window closing at 14:15 accepts events until 14:25. Events arriving after 14:25 are dropped. The 10-minute watermark was determined by analyzing the historical distribution of event delays — 99.7% of events arrive within 8 minutes of their timestamp, so the 10-minute watermark captures 99.9% of events while bounding the state that the streaming engine must maintain.
Trigger Interval Configuration

The trigger interval determines how frequently the streaming engine processes accumulated events. A 100ms interval (the default) processes events nearly in real time but generates many small writes to Delta Lake — creating the small file problem that OPTIMIZE must remediate (Module 3, lesson 6). A 30-second interval accumulates events for 30 seconds before processing — producing fewer, larger writes at the cost of 30 seconds of additional latency. At AlpineEnergy, the grid operations streaming pipeline uses a 30-second trigger (15-minute freshness SLA means 30 seconds of processing latency is negligible). The meter status streaming pipeline uses a 5-second trigger (seconds-level freshness requirement). The trigger interval is the primary knob for trading latency against write efficiency.

blocks.title: Streaming produces small files — OPTIMIZE is mandatory
blocks.content: Each micro-batch writes one or more Parquet files to Delta Lake. A 30-second trigger running 24 hours produces 2,880 files per day — each potentially only a few MB. Without OPTIMIZE, the file count grows unboundedly: after 30 days, the partition contains 86,400 files. File listing, statistics evaluation, and scan planning all degrade with file count. At AlpineEnergy, the streaming pipeline is paired with a scheduled OPTIMIZE that runs every 4 hours during business hours and every 2 hours during the pipeline window — compacting the accumulated small files into target-sized files (128 MB).

Module 4 (lesson 5) introduced materialized views as batch pre-computation: a nightly-refreshed view that pre-joins tables so consumers avoid repeating the join. Streaming materialized views extend this concept to continuous refresh: the view is updated every micro-batch with the latest data, providing near-real-time access to pre-computed results. At AlpineEnergy, the grid operations dashboard needs a pre-joined view that combines meter readings with meter metadata and substation capacity — the same enrichment that the batch Gold pipeline produces, but with 15-minute freshness instead of 24-hour freshness.
The Streaming Enrichment Pattern

-- Streaming materialized view: continuously enrich CDC events with dimension data
-- The dimension tables (meters, substations) are read as static DataFrames
-- The fact stream (meter_readings) is read as a streaming DataFrame
-- The join produces a continuously-updated enriched stream

CREATE OR REPLACE STREAMING TABLE alpine_ch.gold.enriched_readings_stream
AS SELECT
  mr.event.meter_id,
  mr.event.daily_kw,
  mr.event.date,
  mr.event.region,
  m.customer_type,
  m.substation_id,
  s.substation_name,
  s.capacity_mw
FROM alpine_ch.streaming.meter_readings_stream AS mr
