  INNER JOIN alpine_ch.silver.meters AS m          -- static dimension
    ON mr.event.meter_id = m.meter_id
  INNER JOIN alpine_ch.silver.substations AS s     -- static dimension
    ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
WHERE mr.event.op IN ('c', 'u');

The pattern joins the streaming fact (meter_readings CDC events) with static dimensions (meters, substations). In Spark Structured Streaming, this is a stream-static join: the dimension DataFrames are loaded once (or periodically refreshed) and broadcast to all streaming micro-batches. The join produces an enriched stream that the grid operations dashboard reads. The dashboard's query is a simple SELECT against the enriched streaming table — no join at query time. At AlpineEnergy, the stream-static join adds approximately 2 seconds to each micro-batch (the broadcast dimension is 45 MB), well within the 30-second trigger interval.
Dimension Refresh in Stream-Static Joins

Static dimensions become stale: a meter's customer_type changes, a substation's capacity is upgraded. If the dimension DataFrame is loaded once at streaming job start, the enrichment uses the dimension values from startup — which may be hours or days old. At AlpineEnergy, the dimension DataFrames are refreshed every 4 hours by restarting the streaming query with a fresh dimension load. The restart takes approximately 30 seconds (the streaming job checkpoints its Kafka offsets and resumes from the last committed position). The 4-hour dimension refresh cycle is acceptable because dimension changes are infrequent (meters change customer_type at most monthly, substations change capacity at most annually). For the meter_status dimension (which changes frequently — meters go online/offline throughout the day), a separate CDC stream provides real-time dimension updates.
Consistency Between Batch and Streaming Views

AlpineEnergy maintains both a batch Gold enriched view (refreshed nightly, used by billing) and a streaming Gold enriched view (refreshed continuously, used by grid operations). The two views should produce the same result for the same time period — but they may not, because the streaming view processes events as they arrive (with watermarking that may drop late events) while the batch view processes the complete dataset (with no late-event loss). At AlpineEnergy, a daily reconciliation job compares the streaming view's 24-hour aggregation against the batch view's equivalent. Discrepancies exceeding 0.1% trigger investigation. In 12 months, 3 discrepancies were found — all caused by events that arrived after the watermark threshold and were dropped by the streaming view but captured by the batch view.

blocks.title: Batch is the source of truth; streaming is the fast approximation
blocks.content: The streaming materialized view provides speed. The batch materialized view provides completeness. When the two disagree, the batch view is correct. This hierarchy is explicit at AlpineEnergy: the grid operations dashboard shows streaming data with a 'latest update' timestamp, and the billing system uses only the batch data. No financial or regulatory decision is made from the streaming view alone. The streaming view's SLA is freshness (15 minutes); the batch view's SLA is accuracy (99.9% completeness). Different guarantees for different consumers.

The CDC pipeline moves data from PostgreSQL to Kafka to Delta Lake. At each boundary (PostgreSQL → Kafka, Kafka → Spark, Spark → Delta Lake), an event can be lost (at-most-once), duplicated (at-least-once), or delivered exactly once. Loss means the grid operations dashboard misses a meter reading — showing incorrect consumption totals. Duplication means the dashboard counts a meter reading twice — showing inflated totals. Exactly-once delivery ensures that every event from PostgreSQL appears exactly once in Delta Lake — no loss, no duplication. Achieving exactly-once across the entire pipeline requires coordinated guarantees at each boundary.
The Three Delivery Guarantees

rows.left: Producer publishes to Kafka with acks=all; retry on failure
rows.right: Producer publishes with idempotent writes (enable.idempotence=true); Kafka deduplicates

rows.left: Consumer commits Kafka offset after processing; re-processes on failure → duplicates
rows.right: Consumer commits offset atomically with the Delta Lake write → no duplicates

rows.left: Delta Lake may contain duplicate rows after consumer restart
rows.right: Delta Lake contains exactly one copy of each event

rows.left: Requires downstream deduplication (MERGE with conflict resolution)
rows.right: No downstream deduplication needed — correctness guaranteed by the pipeline

blocks.content: Idempotent Writes

The practical approach to exactly-once in most production systems is at-least-once delivery with idempotent writes. The pipeline guarantees that events are not lost (at-least-once) and that duplicate events are harmlessly absorbed (idempotent writes). Idempotent writes use a MERGE pattern: each event carries a unique key (e.g., meter_id + date), and the MERGE inserts the event if the key does not exist or updates it if the key already exists. A duplicate event produces the same final state as a single event. At AlpineEnergy, the streaming pipeline uses at-least-once delivery from Kafka (simpler configuration, no transactional producer overhead) with idempotent MERGE into Delta Lake.
The MERGE-Based Idempotent Sink

-- Idempotent sink: MERGE CDC events into Delta Lake
-- Handles INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE events
-- Duplicate events (same meter_id + date) are absorbed by the MERGE condition

MERGE INTO alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings AS target
USING (
  SELECT event.meter_id, event.daily_kw, event.date,
         event.region, event.customer_type, event.op
  FROM   alpine_ch.streaming.meter_readings_stream
  WHERE  event.op IS NOT NULL
) AS source
ON target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date
WHEN MATCHED AND source.op = 'd' THEN DELETE
WHEN MATCHED AND source.op IN ('c', 'u') THEN UPDATE SET *
WHEN NOT MATCHED AND source.op IN ('c', 'u') THEN INSERT *;

The MERGE handles all three CDC operation types: INSERT (op = 'c' for create) inserts the row if it does not exist, UPDATE (op = 'u') updates the row if it exists, DELETE (op = 'd') removes the row. If the same event arrives twice (a Kafka consumer restart replays the event), the MERGE produces the same final state: the second INSERT becomes an UPDATE (matched on the key), the second UPDATE writes the same values, and the second DELETE is a no-op (the row was already deleted). The MERGE key (meter_id + date) must match the event's natural key — if the key is wrong, duplicates accumulate instead of being absorbed.
When NOT to Use Exactly-Once

Exactly-once delivery (whether native or via idempotent writes) adds complexity and latency. The MERGE pattern is more expensive than a simple append: each micro-batch must probe the target table for matching keys before deciding to INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. For append-only data (meter readings that are never updated or deleted), at-most-once with duplicate detection is sufficient: the pipeline appends all events, and a daily deduplication job removes any duplicates that occurred during failures. At AlpineEnergy, the meter_readings streaming pipeline uses append + daily deduplication (readings are immutable). The meter_status streaming pipeline uses MERGE-based idempotent writes (status updates are frequent and must reflect the latest state).

blocks.title: Choose the delivery guarantee per table, not per pipeline
blocks.content: Append-only tables (meter_readings, grid_events) need only at-least-once with deduplication — simple and fast. Mutable tables (meter_status, tariffs) need idempotent MERGE — correct but slower. At AlpineEnergy, the CDC pipeline processes both table types from the same Kafka topics but applies different sink strategies: append for immutable tables, MERGE for mutable tables. The sink strategy is configured per table in the pipeline's YAML configuration and documented in the data product manifest.

A single Debezium connector replicating one table is straightforward. AlpineEnergy operates 3 connectors replicating 24 tables across 3 countries to a Kafka cluster with 24 topics consumed by 4 streaming jobs writing to 24 Delta Lake tables. At this scale, CDC becomes an infrastructure platform with its own operational concerns: multi-connector management, cross-country CDC routing for data residency compliance, schema evolution propagation, failure recovery across the full pipeline, and capacity planning for the Kafka cluster and the streaming compute. This lesson covers the operational patterns that make enterprise CDC reliable.
Multi-Connector Management

Each country's Debezium connector has its own configuration, its own replication slot, its own Kafka topics, and its own failure domain. The Swiss connector failing does not affect the Austrian or Italian connectors — each reads from a separate PostgreSQL instance. At AlpineEnergy, connector configuration is managed as code in a Git repository: each connector's JSON configuration file specifies the source database, the publication, the topic naming convention, and the serialization format. Connector deployment uses Kafka Connect's REST API: the deployment script validates the configuration, deploys the connector, verifies that it reaches the RUNNING state, and checks the initial lag metric. A failed deployment rolls back to the previous configuration.
Schema Evolution Through CDC

When a PostgreSQL table's schema changes (a column is added, renamed, or removed), the change propagates through the CDC pipeline: Debezium detects the schema change from the WAL, updates its internal schema registry, and begins publishing events with the new schema. The Kafka topic now contains events with two schemas — old events (before the change) and new events (after the change). The Spark consumer must handle both schemas: it reads the schema from each event's header and applies the appropriate deserialization. At AlpineEnergy, schema evolution in CDC follows a strict protocol: the schema change is first applied to the Delta Lake target (adding the new column with a default value), then applied to the PostgreSQL source (adding the column to the publication). This ordering ensures the target can accept events with the new schema before the source begins producing them.
Cross-Country CDC and Data Residency

# CDC topic naming and residency routing
# Each country's topics are prefixed with the country code

# Swiss topics (consumed by Swiss and shared streaming jobs):
ch.silver.meter_readings    → alpine_ch.silver.meter_readings (Delta Lake)
ch.silver.meters            → alpine_ch.silver.meters (Delta Lake)
ch.silver.meter_status      → alpine_ch.silver.meter_status (Delta Lake)

# Austrian topics (consumed by Austrian and shared streaming jobs):
at.silver.meter_readings    → alpine_at.silver.meter_readings (Delta Lake)

# Italian topics (consumed by Italian streaming jobs ONLY on Italian infrastructure):
it.silver.meter_readings    → alpine_it.silver.meter_readings (Delta Lake, Italy region)

# Cross-country anonymized topics (PII stripped before publication):
ch.shared.meter_readings_anon → alpine_shared.silver.ch_meter_readings_anon
at.shared.meter_readings_anon → alpine_shared.silver.at_meter_readings_anon
it.shared.meter_readings_anon → alpine_shared.silver.it_meter_readings_anon

The CDC routing enforces data residency (Module 8, lesson 5) at the streaming layer. Italian CDC events are consumed only by streaming jobs running on Italian infrastructure — the Italian Kafka topics are not subscribed to by Swiss or Austrian consumers. Cross-country analytics use the anonymized topics: a separate Debezium transformation step strips PII columns before publishing to the shared topic namespace. This anonymization happens in the Debezium connector's Single Message Transform (SMT) chain — before the event reaches Kafka — ensuring that PII never enters the shared topic.
Failure Recovery

The CDC pipeline has three failure points: the Debezium connector (crashes, loses connection to PostgreSQL), the Kafka cluster (broker failure, topic unavailable), and the Spark streaming consumer (job failure, checkpoint corruption). Each failure point has a recovery mechanism. Connector failure: Kafka Connect automatically restarts the connector from its last committed offset. Kafka failure: the connector pauses and retries until the broker recovers; WAL retention at the PostgreSQL slot bridges the gap. Consumer failure: the Spark job restarts from its checkpoint (stored in Delta Lake), resuming from the last committed Kafka offset. At AlpineEnergy, the mean time to recovery for each failure type is: connector restart (15 seconds), Kafka broker recovery (2-5 minutes), Spark job restart (45 seconds).

blocks.title: The slot-to-disk failure cascade
blocks.content: The most dangerous CDC failure is a silent consumer failure where the replication slot continues retaining WAL. Scenario: the Spark job crashes, the Kafka consumer stops reading, but the Debezium connector continues publishing to Kafka (which buffers the events). Meanwhile, the PostgreSQL replication slot retains WAL because the connector's committed offset lags behind. If the Spark job stays down for 12+ hours, the retained WAL exceeds the disk threshold. At AlpineEnergy, the monitoring system correlates three metrics: Spark streaming job status, Kafka consumer lag, and PostgreSQL slot retention. All three must be green; any one being red triggers investigation within 30 minutes.

Event sourcing stores business events as an append-only log rather than maintaining a current-state table that is overwritten on each change. Instead of a meter_status table that is UPDATEd when a meter goes online or offline, an event-sourced model stores an events table: meter_id CH-BERN-00142 went ONLINE at 14:00, went OFFLINE at 14:35, went ONLINE at 14:40. The current state is derived by aggregating the event log: the latest event for each meter determines its current status. The event log is the source of truth; the derived state is a materialized view. This pattern provides a complete audit trail (every state change is recorded), enables point-in-time reconstruction (what was the state at any historical timestamp?), and simplifies CDC (events are appended, never updated — no MERGE needed).
The Event Log Pattern

-- Event log table: append-only record of every state change
CREATE TABLE silver.meter_events (
    event_id     BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
    meter_id     VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,
    event_type   VARCHAR(30)  NOT NULL,  -- 'STATUS_CHANGE', 'READING', 'CLASSIFICATION_CHANGE'
    event_data   JSONB        NOT NULL,  -- {"old_status": "ONLINE", "new_status": "OFFLINE"}
    event_time   TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    produced_by  VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL   -- pipeline or system that produced the event
);

-- Current state: derived from the event log via window function
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW gold.mv_meter_current_status AS
SELECT DISTINCT ON (meter_id)
       meter_id,
       (event_data->>'new_status')::VARCHAR(20) AS current_status,
       event_time AS status_since
FROM   silver.meter_events
WHERE  event_type = 'STATUS_CHANGE'
ORDER BY meter_id, event_time DESC;

-- Point-in-time reconstruction: what was the status at a specific time?
SELECT DISTINCT ON (meter_id)
       meter_id,
       (event_data->>'new_status')::VARCHAR(20) AS status_at_point,
       event_time
FROM   silver.meter_events
WHERE  event_type = 'STATUS_CHANGE'
  AND  event_time <= '2025-01-15 14:30:00+00'
ORDER BY meter_id, event_time DESC;

The event log stores every state change with its timestamp, the old and new values (in JSONB), and the producing system. The materialized view derives the current status by selecting the most recent event per meter (DISTINCT ON ordered by event_time DESC). The point-in-time query uses the same pattern with a timestamp filter — producing the status as of any historical moment. At AlpineEnergy, the meter_events table is the authoritative source for the regulatory audit trail: every meter status change, every customer reclassification, and every tariff adjustment is recorded as an event. The regulatory filing includes a point-in-time reconstruction of every meter's status at the quarter-end timestamp.
Event Sourcing and CDC Synergy

Event-sourced tables simplify CDC because events are append-only: the CDC pipeline captures INSERT events only — no UPDATEs, no DELETEs. The Kafka topic is a direct reflection of the event log. The Spark consumer appends events to the Delta Lake event log without MERGE. The current-state materialized view is recomputed on the analytical side (Spark SQL) from the replicated event log. This append-only architecture eliminates the most complex CDC patterns: no MERGE-based idempotent sinks, no delete event handling, no out-of-order update conflict resolution. At AlpineEnergy, event-sourced tables account for 3 of the 8 CDC-published tables — and they are the 3 with the simplest, most reliable CDC pipelines.
When NOT to Use Event Sourcing

Event sourcing trades write simplicity (append-only) for read complexity (deriving current state requires aggregating the event log). For tables with high query volume on current state (the customer portal querying meter status 10,000 times per day), deriving state from the event log on every query is expensive — the materialized view must be refreshed frequently. For tables with high event volume (meter_readings with 4.8 million events per day), the event log grows rapidly and the point-in-time reconstruction query becomes expensive. At AlpineEnergy, event sourcing is used for low-volume, audit-critical tables (meter_events: ~50,000 events/day, regulatory audit required). The high-volume meter_readings table uses the traditional state-based model because the event log would grow to 1.7 billion rows per year and the point-in-time query would be impractically slow.

blocks.title: Event sourcing is an audit architecture, not a performance optimization
blocks.content: Event sourcing is slower for current-state queries (requires aggregation) and uses more storage (the full event history is retained). Its value is in the audit trail (every state change is recorded with its timestamp and producer) and the point-in-time capability (any historical state can be reconstructed). Choose event sourcing when the regulatory or operational requirements demand a complete, immutable record of every change — not when the goal is faster reads or simpler queries.

Batch pipelines produce a complete result that can be validated after completion: row counts match, aggregates reconcile, quality assertions pass. Streaming pipelines produce an evolving result that is never 'complete' — validation must occur on partial, continuously changing data. The quality framework from the mid-level track (Module 9) was designed for batch: it runs after the pipeline completes and validates the entire output. Streaming quality requires a different approach: continuous monitoring of streaming-specific metrics (lag, throughput, late event rate), periodic reconciliation against the batch source of truth, and disaster recovery procedures for streaming-specific failure modes (checkpoint corruption, consumer group reset, full re-snapshot).
Streaming-Specific Quality Metrics

rows: Time from PostgreSQL write to Delta Lake availability
Number of unprocessed events in the topic
Percentage of events arriving after watermark expiry
Percentage difference between streaming and batch aggregates for same period

blocks.content: The micro-batch duration metric is the most critical operational indicator. If a micro-batch takes longer than the trigger interval (30 seconds at AlpineEnergy), the streaming job falls behind: each batch processes more events than the previous, making the next batch even longer — a cascading failure that eventually exhausts memory. At AlpineEnergy, a micro-batch duration exceeding 80% of the trigger interval triggers an investigation: the cause is typically a spike in source write volume (more events to process per batch) or a degradation in the Delta Lake write performance (small file accumulation requiring OPTIMIZE). If the duration exceeds 100% for 3 consecutive batches, the on-call engineer increases the trigger interval temporarily while investigating the root cause.
Disaster Recovery Procedures

Three streaming-specific disasters require documented recovery procedures. Checkpoint corruption: the Spark streaming checkpoint (which stores the last committed Kafka offset and the in-flight state) becomes unreadable. Recovery: delete the corrupted checkpoint, restart the streaming job from the earliest available Kafka offset (within the topic retention window), and run a deduplication pass on the Delta Lake target to remove events that were processed before the corruption but replayed after the restart. Consumer group reset: the Kafka consumer's offset is lost (topic retention exceeded, or administrative reset). Recovery: perform a Debezium re-snapshot from the source PostgreSQL, which produces a fresh baseline followed by incremental events. Full re-snapshot: the Delta Lake target is corrupted or deleted. Recovery: recreate the table, trigger a Debezium re-snapshot, and wait for the snapshot + incremental catch-up to complete.
The Streaming SLA

AlpineEnergy's streaming SLA defines four guarantees: freshness (data available in Delta Lake within 15 minutes of source write for grid operations, within 60 seconds for meter status), completeness (≥ 99.9% of source events captured — the 0.1% gap accounts for events dropped by watermarking), correctness (batch-streaming divergence < 0.1% for any 24-hour period), and recoverability (the streaming pipeline recovers from any single-component failure within 30 minutes without manual intervention). The SLA is monitored by the metrics in the table above, and violations trigger the same incident response process as batch SLA violations (Module 10, lesson 5). In 12 months, the streaming pipeline has had 4 SLA violations: 2 freshness violations (Debezium connector restart after Kafka maintenance), 1 completeness violation (watermark too aggressive during a high-latency network event), and 1 recoverability violation (checkpoint corruption required manual intervention).

blocks.title: Test disaster recovery before you need it
blocks.content: Every disaster recovery procedure at AlpineEnergy is tested quarterly in a staging environment: the team intentionally corrupts a checkpoint, resets a consumer offset, and deletes a Delta Lake table, then executes the documented recovery procedure and verifies the end-to-end pipeline returns to a healthy state within the 30-minute SLA. The quarterly test has identified 2 procedure gaps (the deduplication step after checkpoint recovery was missing from the runbook, and the re-snapshot trigger command was out of date). Testing found both gaps before a production incident required the procedure.

This module covered the streaming and CDC architecture across AlpineEnergy's multi-engine, multi-country platform: the batch-streaming spectrum with quantified decision criteria, PostgreSQL logical replication with publication and replication slot management, Debezium connector architecture with snapshot and schema change handling, Spark Structured Streaming with watermarking and trigger interval configuration, streaming materialized views with stream-static joins and dimension refresh, exactly-once delivery via idempotent MERGE sinks with per-table sink strategy selection, enterprise CDC with multi-connector management and cross-country residency routing, event sourcing as an audit-oriented append-only architecture, and streaming quality monitoring with disaster recovery procedures.
The Streaming Architecture Summary

rows: Debezium PostgreSQL connectors on Kafka Connect
Delta Lake (append for immutable, MERGE for mutable)
Custom metrics + daily reconciliation against batch

blocks.content: The Complete Data Flow

The streaming architecture integrates with every module in the senior track. The platform architecture (Module 1) defines the dual-engine topology that CDC bridges. The optimizer internals (Module 2) determine how streaming queries are planned and executed. The partition strategy (Module 3) determines how streaming writes are organized and compacted. The join architecture (Module 4) determines how streaming facts are enriched with static dimensions. The schema governance (Module 5) ensures that schema changes propagate safely through the CDC pipeline. The engine internals (Module 6) explain the WAL mechanics that logical replication reads. The storage internals (Module 7) determine how streaming writes create Parquet files and how OPTIMIZE compacts them. The security architecture (Module 8) ensures that CDC respects data residency and access control across boundaries.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 10 closes the senior track with enterprise governance and capacity planning — the organizational frameworks that sustain the entire platform over years. The streaming pipeline from this module is one of the most operationally complex subsystems: it runs 24/7, it spans all three countries, and it touches every layer of the architecture. Module 10's capacity planning (lesson 3) must forecast the streaming compute and Kafka storage growth alongside the batch compute and Delta Lake storage growth. Module 10's incident management (lesson 5) must handle streaming-specific incidents (connector lag, checkpoint corruption) alongside batch incidents (pipeline failure, data quality breach). The governance framework in Module 10 governs everything — batch and streaming, PostgreSQL and Spark SQL, Swiss and Austrian and Italian.

modules.title: Enterprise Governance and Capacity Planning
modules.description: Establish the organizational and operational frameworks that sustain a multi-engine, multi-team SQL platform over years — data lineage, capacity planning, SLA governance, incident management, and the team structures that make data engineering reliable at enterprise scale.

blocks.content: Module 5 (lesson 8) introduced column-level lineage for schema change impact analysis. This lesson extends lineage to the full platform scope: 300+ tables across two engines, 200+ pipelines, and data flows that cross engine boundaries via CDC (Module 9). At this scale, lineage answers questions that no single team can answer alone: which downstream tables are affected if the Swiss Bronze ingestion pipeline fails? Which reports depend on the meter_readings table — directly or through chains of Gold and Platinum views? Which pipelines contribute to the regulatory filing, and in what order must they complete? Platform-scale lineage is the map that makes the 200-pipeline dependency graph navigable — enabling impact analysis, root cause tracing, and critical path identification.
The Lineage Collection Architecture

-- Platform lineage table: stores table-level data flow relationships
-- Populated by parsing pipeline SQL, CDC configuration, and view definitions
CREATE TABLE platform.data_lineage (
    source_table    VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,  -- fully qualified: catalog.schema.table
    source_engine   VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,  -- 'postgresql' or 'sparksql'
    target_table    VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    target_engine   VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,
    pipeline_name   VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,  -- pipeline that creates this data flow
    flow_type       VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,  -- 'batch', 'streaming', 'cdc', 'view'
    last_verified   TIMESTAMPTZ  DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (source_table, target_table, pipeline_name)
);

-- Recursive lineage trace: find all upstream dependencies of a table
WITH RECURSIVE upstream AS (
    SELECT source_table, target_table, pipeline_name, flow_type, 1 AS depth
    FROM   platform.data_lineage
    WHERE  target_table = 'alpine_shared.platinum.enterprise_dashboard'
  UNION ALL
    SELECT l.source_table, l.target_table, l.pipeline_name, l.flow_type, u.depth + 1
    FROM   platform.data_lineage l
      JOIN upstream u ON l.target_table = u.source_table
    WHERE  u.depth < 10  -- prevent infinite recursion
)
SELECT source_table, target_table, pipeline_name, flow_type, depth
FROM   upstream
ORDER BY depth DESC;

The recursive CTE traces the complete upstream dependency chain from the Platinum enterprise dashboard back through Gold, Silver, and Bronze tables to the source systems. At AlpineEnergy, this query reveals 42 upstream dependencies for the enterprise dashboard: 3 Platinum tables, 9 Gold tables, 12 Silver tables, 8 Bronze tables, 6 CDC flows, and 4 external source connections. The depth column shows the distance — depth 1 is the direct dependency, depth 7 is the most distant upstream source. The critical path (the longest dependency chain) determines the earliest time the dashboard can be refreshed: if the longest chain is 7 steps with each step averaging 15 minutes, the minimum refresh time is 105 minutes after the source data arrives.
Cross-Engine Lineage

AlpineEnergy's dual-engine architecture creates lineage that crosses engine boundaries: a PostgreSQL Silver table feeds a CDC pipeline that produces a Delta Lake Silver table, which feeds a Spark SQL Gold transformation, which feeds a materialized view written back to PostgreSQL for the customer portal. The lineage table's source_engine and target_engine columns capture these boundary crossings. The boundary crossings are the highest-risk points in the lineage: they involve network transfer, format conversion, and type mapping (Module 5, lesson 7). When investigating a data quality issue, the cross-engine boundaries are the first places to check — because they are where precision loss, type mismatch, and latency issues most commonly occur.
Lineage Maintenance

Lineage is only useful if it is current. At AlpineEnergy, the lineage table is populated by three mechanisms. First, a nightly pipeline parser reads every SQL migration and pipeline script, extracts the source and target tables using SQL parsing, and updates the lineage table. Second, the CDC pipeline configuration is parsed to capture cross-engine flows (each Debezium connector configuration declares its source and target). Third, PostgreSQL view definitions are queried from information_schema.view_column_usage (Module 5, lesson 8) to capture view-based lineage. The three mechanisms together cover batch flows, streaming flows, and view-based derivation. A quarterly audit compares the lineage table against the pipeline execution logs to detect lineage gaps — pipelines that executed but are not represented in the lineage graph.

blocks.title: Lineage is the platform's nervous system
blocks.content: Without lineage, every operational question requires manual investigation: 'call the Swiss team and ask if their pipeline ran, then call the Austrian team and ask if their Gold refresh completed.' With lineage, the same question is a SQL query: traverse the dependency graph from the affected table, identify every upstream pipeline and its status, and determine the root cause in seconds. At AlpineEnergy, the lineage graph has reduced mean time to root cause identification from 45 minutes (manual cross-team investigation) to 8 minutes (recursive query + pipeline status check).

Lineage traces upstream (where does this data come from?). Impact analysis traces downstream (what breaks if this changes?). The two are complementary: upstream lineage diagnoses problems (the dashboard shows wrong numbers — trace upstream to find the broken pipeline), and downstream impact analysis prevents problems (before changing this table's schema, identify every downstream consumer). Module 5 (lesson 4) used impact analysis for schema change negotiation. This lesson extends it to all change types: schema changes, pipeline schedule changes, data volume changes, engine configuration changes, and team ownership changes — any modification that propagates through the dependency graph.
The Impact Analysis Query

-- Downstream impact analysis: what is affected if silver.meter_readings changes?
WITH RECURSIVE downstream AS (
    SELECT target_table, pipeline_name, flow_type, 1 AS depth
    FROM   platform.data_lineage
    WHERE  source_table = 'alpineenergy_ch.silver.meter_readings'
  UNION ALL
    SELECT l.target_table, l.pipeline_name, l.flow_type, d.depth + 1
    FROM   platform.data_lineage l
      JOIN downstream d ON l.source_table = d.target_table
    WHERE  d.depth < 10
),
impact AS (
  SELECT d.target_table, d.pipeline_name, d.flow_type, d.depth,
         o.owning_team, o.sla_tier, o.tech_contact
  FROM   downstream d
    LEFT JOIN platform.table_ownership o
      ON d.target_table = o.table_catalog || '.' || o.table_schema || '.' || o.table_name
)
SELECT depth, target_table, owning_team, sla_tier, pipeline_name, flow_type
FROM   impact
ORDER BY sla_tier DESC, depth ASC;

The query reveals every table, pipeline, and team affected by a change to the Swiss Silver meter_readings table — sorted by SLA tier (platinum first, the most critical consumers) and depth (closest dependencies first, the most immediately affected). At AlpineEnergy, this query returns 28 downstream dependencies: 6 Gold tables, 3 Platinum tables, 2 CDC targets, 4 materialized views, and 13 dependent pipelines owned by 7 different teams. The platinum-tier dependencies (billing, regulatory) require the full SCR negotiation process (Module 5, lesson 4). The gold-tier dependencies require notification. The silver-tier dependencies require documentation but not formal coordination.
Change Propagation Types

rows: Every pipeline that references the changed column
Renaming region → sub_region affects 9 pipelines across 6 teams
Every downstream pipeline with a freshness dependency
Moving Swiss Silver from 05:30 to 06:00 delays Austrian Gold past its 06:30 SLA
Every pipeline that scans or joins the growing table
Italian expansion tripled meter_readings volume; 4 Gold pipelines exceeded SLA
Every query on the affected instance
Every consumer with a contract referencing the old team
Swiss billing team transferred meter_status to the platform team; 5 contracts updated

blocks.content: Each change type propagates through a different mechanism and affects a different scope. Schema changes propagate through the contract system. Schedule changes propagate through the dependency registry's freshness checks. Volume changes propagate through performance degradation that is detected by monitoring. Engine config changes propagate through plan changes that are detected by plan stability tests. Ownership changes propagate through the contract notification channels. The common thread: every change type has a detection mechanism (how do we know the change will cause impact?) and a notification mechanism (how do we tell the affected teams?). At AlpineEnergy, all five mechanisms are automated — no change propagation depends on manual investigation.

blocks.title: Schedule changes are the most commonly overlooked impact
blocks.content: Schema changes are obvious — they break queries with error messages. Schedule changes are silent: a pipeline that starts 30 minutes later produces correct results, just later. The downstream pipelines that depend on its freshness wait longer, consume their SLA margin, and eventually breach their own SLA — without any error, any plan change, or any data quality issue. At AlpineEnergy, the dependency registry's freshness check (Module 1, lesson 7) is the detection mechanism: it blocks downstream pipelines from processing stale data and alerts the on-call engineer to the upstream delay.

Capacity planning forecasts the compute, storage, and network resources the platform will need 6-12 months from now. Without capacity planning, growth arrives as a crisis: the PostgreSQL disk fills at 02:00 on a Monday, the Spark cluster's executors are fully allocated during the pipeline window, or the Kafka cluster's topic retention drops below the 7-day target because partition count exceeded the broker's capacity. At AlpineEnergy, capacity planning runs quarterly and feeds directly into the cloud budget request — the forecast determines the infrastructure spend for the next quarter. Inaccurate forecasts waste budget (over-provisioned resources sit idle) or cause incidents (under-provisioned resources fail under load).
The Capacity Model

-- Storage growth forecast: project when each engine will exhaust capacity
WITH monthly_growth AS (
    SELECT engine,
           DATE_TRUNC('month', measured_at) AS month,
           MAX(storage_used_gb) AS storage_gb
    FROM   monitoring.capacity_metrics
    WHERE  measured_at >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '12 months'
    GROUP BY engine, DATE_TRUNC('month', measured_at)
),
growth_rate AS (
    SELECT engine,
           REGR_SLOPE(storage_gb, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM month)) AS gb_per_second,
           REGR_SLOPE(storage_gb, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM month)) * 86400 * 30 AS gb_per_month,
           MAX(storage_gb) AS current_gb
    FROM   monthly_growth
    GROUP BY engine
)
SELECT engine,
       ROUND(current_gb) AS current_gb,
       ROUND(gb_per_month, 1) AS growth_gb_month,
       ROUND(current_gb + gb_per_month * 6) AS projected_6mo_gb,
       ROUND(current_gb + gb_per_month * 12) AS projected_12mo_gb,
       CASE
         WHEN engine = 'postgresql' THEN 5000  -- 5 TB provisioned
         WHEN engine = 'sparksql'   THEN 15000 -- 15 TB ADLS budget
       END AS capacity_limit_gb,
       ROUND((CASE WHEN engine = 'postgresql' THEN 5000 ELSE 15000 END - current_gb)
             / NULLIF(gb_per_month, 0)) AS months_to_exhaustion
FROM   growth_rate;

The forecast uses linear regression on 12 months of monthly storage measurements to project the growth rate. PostgreSQL grows at 42.5 GB/month (primarily from meter_readings and audit logs); the 5 TB provisioned capacity will last approximately 35 months at current growth. Spark SQL grows at 185 GB/month (Delta Lake fact tables, CDC replicas, and the ML feature store); the 15 TB ADLS budget will last approximately 37 months. Both projections include a safety margin: AlpineEnergy's policy triggers a capacity expansion review when months_to_exhaustion drops below 12. The expansion review evaluates three options: increase provisioned capacity (straightforward but costs more), implement data archival (move old data to cold storage), or optimize storage efficiency (better compression, more aggressive retention).
Compute Capacity Planning

Storage capacity is predictable — data grows monotonically. Compute capacity is variable — query volume and complexity change with team activity, seasonal patterns, and platform growth. At AlpineEnergy, compute capacity planning tracks three metrics: PostgreSQL peak concurrent backend count (measured hourly, projected by linear trend), Spark SQL peak executor utilization during the pipeline window (measured per-batch, projected by workload growth), and Kafka broker CPU utilization (measured per-minute, projected by topic throughput growth). The forecast identifies which compute resource will become the bottleneck first: at current growth rates, the Spark SQL cluster will need 4 additional executor cores within 9 months (the ML team's feature engineering workload is growing 12% per quarter as they add new model features).
When NOT to Plan Capacity

Capacity planning has diminishing returns for elastic cloud infrastructure that auto-scales. If the Spark SQL cluster uses auto-scaling executors (adding and removing nodes based on workload), the compute capacity planning is less critical — the cloud provider handles the scaling. At AlpineEnergy, the Spark SQL cluster auto-scales between 4 and 16 nodes, so compute capacity planning focuses on the cost ceiling (maximum auto-scale spend per month) rather than the resource ceiling (maximum available cores). PostgreSQL does not auto-scale (vertical scaling requires downtime), so PostgreSQL capacity planning remains critical — a disk exhaustion or memory exhaustion on a non-elastic instance is an outage.

blocks.title: Plan for growth events, not just organic growth
blocks.content: Linear growth projection captures organic data accumulation. It does not capture growth events: a new country expansion (the Italian addition tripled meter volume overnight), a new regulatory requirement (GDPR's right-to-be-forgotten added a deletion pipeline that generates WAL), or a new team onboarding (the ML team's feature store added 500 GB in its first month). At AlpineEnergy, the capacity plan includes a 'growth event buffer' — 20% of capacity reserved for unplanned growth events. The buffer has been consumed once (the Italian expansion) and replenished at the next quarterly review.

Module 1 (lesson 4) introduced SLA tiers (platinum, gold, silver) and the ownership registry. This lesson covers the governance process that makes SLA tiers operational: how SLAs are defined, how they are measured, how violations are detected and escalated, and how the governance committee arbitrates competing SLA requirements. At AlpineEnergy, every table with a registered data product (Module 1, lesson 6) has a declared SLA specifying freshness, completeness, and quality thresholds. The SLA governance system monitors every declaration, detects violations in real time, and routes violations to the responsible team through the escalation protocol.
SLA Definition and Measurement

rows: Data available within 60 min of source update
Zero critical data quality assertions failed

blocks.content: Each SLA dimension is measurable. Freshness is the time difference between the source update timestamp and the target table's latest loaded_at value — measured by the monitoring queries from Module 1 (lesson 9). Completeness is the ratio of actual row count to expected row count (derived from the source system's event count) — measured by the quality framework from the mid-level track. Quality is the count of failed data quality assertions — measured by the dq_metrics table. The measurements are stored in monitoring.sla_measurements and evaluated every 15 minutes against the declared thresholds. A threshold breach triggers the escalation protocol appropriate to the SLA tier.
The Governance Committee

The governance committee is a quarterly meeting of the platform team lead (Lena), one representative from each country's data engineering team, and one representative from the analytics team. The committee has four responsibilities: arbitrating SLA conflicts (the Austrian analytics team wants 15-minute freshness but the platform budget supports only hourly refresh), reviewing SLA tier assignments (is this table still platinum-worthy, or has its consumer base shrunk to gold-level?), approving breaking changes that affect platinum-tier tables (the Schema Change Request escalation path from Module 5), and reviewing the capacity plan (lesson 3). At AlpineEnergy, the committee has met 6 times in 18 months, resolving 4 SLA conflicts, reclassifying 8 tables (5 promoted from silver to gold as consumer count grew, 3 demoted from gold to silver as consumers decommissioned), and approving 2 breaking changes.
SLA Conflict Resolution

SLA conflicts arise when a consumer's freshness requirement exceeds what the producer's pipeline can deliver within the allocated resources. The Austrian analytics team's 15-minute freshness request for the regional consumption dashboard required the Swiss Silver pipeline to run every 15 minutes — but the pipeline takes 12 minutes per run, leaving only 3 minutes of margin and no time for VACUUM or ANALYZE between runs. The governance committee evaluated three options: upgrade the pipeline to streaming (EUR 18,000/year additional cost), optimize the pipeline to run in 8 minutes (4 weeks of engineering effort), or relax the SLA to 30 minutes (no additional cost). The committee approved the 30-minute SLA with a commitment to re-evaluate after the next Spark cluster upgrade (which would reduce the pipeline runtime).

blocks.title: SLAs are contracts between teams, not aspirations
blocks.content: An SLA without measurement is a wish. An SLA without enforcement is a suggestion. An SLA without a governance process is a number in a document. At AlpineEnergy, every SLA is measured (monitoring queries), enforced (alerting and escalation), and governed (quarterly review, conflict resolution, tier reclassification). The SLA governance system is the organizational equivalent of the database's constraint system: it declares the invariants the platform must satisfy and provides the mechanisms to detect and correct violations.

The mid-level track's operational module (Module 10, lesson 9) covered single-team incident response: detect the failure, diagnose the root cause, apply the fix, run the post-mortem. At enterprise scale, incidents span multiple teams, multiple engines, and multiple countries — requiring coordination that single-team response cannot provide. A CDC pipeline failure in the Swiss PostgreSQL instance affects the Austrian Gold refresh, which affects the Platinum enterprise dashboard, which affects the executive team's quarterly report. The incident response must identify the root cause (Swiss CDC), notify all affected consumers (Austrian, Platinum, executive), coordinate the recovery sequence (restore CDC, refresh Gold, refresh Platinum), and track the resolution against the SLA clock.
The Incident Classification

rows: Platinum SLA breach or data loss affecting production consumers
PagerDuty → on-call engineer → platform lead within 30 min
Billing pipeline failed; billing data stale for 4 hours
Gold SLA breach or degradation affecting multiple teams
Slack alert → owning team → platform team if not resolved in 2 hours
Austrian Gold refresh delayed; 3 dashboards showing stale data
Silver SLA breach or quality warning affecting internal tables
Bronze ingestion quality assertion failed; 0.3% rows rejected
PostgreSQL autovacuum took 3x longer than baseline on meter_status

blocks.content: The severity classification maps directly to SLA tiers: a platinum table's SLA breach is P1, a gold table's is P2, and a silver table's is P3. The classification determines the response urgency, the escalation path, and the post-incident review requirements. P1 and P2 incidents require a blameless post-mortem within 5 business days. P3 incidents require a root cause note in the incident tracker. P4 incidents require only acknowledgment. At AlpineEnergy, P1 incidents have occurred 3 times in 18 months: a CDC pipeline failure (lesson context), a PostgreSQL disk exhaustion (capacity planning lesson context), and a security event (Module 8 context). Each produced a post-mortem that added monitoring, testing, or capacity improvements that make the same incident class harder to repeat.
The Blameless Post-Mortem

The post-mortem has five sections, matching the structure from Module 8 (lesson 9) but generalized beyond security events. Timeline: what happened, when, and who was involved. Root cause: the specific technical failure and the conditions that enabled it (not 'human error' — the question is why the system allowed the error to have impact). Impact: which tables, pipelines, teams, and SLAs were affected, and for how long. Remediation: the immediate fix applied and the permanent improvement to prevent recurrence. Action items: specific, assigned, and deadline-tracked improvements — monitoring additions, test additions, configuration changes, or process updates. At AlpineEnergy, every P1 post-mortem produces a minimum of 3 action items, each assigned to a specific engineer with a deadline.

blocks.title: Multi-team incidents need a single incident commander
blocks.content: When a P1 incident affects 5 teams, each team investigates independently — producing 5 parallel diagnosis efforts that may conflict (the Austrian team restarts their pipeline, which overloads the shared Spark cluster that the Swiss team is using to diagnose). At AlpineEnergy, every P1 incident has a single incident commander (the platform team's on-call engineer) who coordinates the investigation, prevents conflicting recovery actions, and manages communication to all affected teams. The incident commander does not diagnose the root cause — they coordinate the people who do.

The mid-level track's data quality framework (Module 9) implemented table-level assertions for one team's pipelines: row count checks, null fraction monitoring, value range validation, and freshness tracking. At AlpineEnergy's scale (300+ tables, 10 teams, 2 engines), the quality framework must be centralized (all teams use the same quality infrastructure), federated (each team defines and owns their quality rules), and cross-engine (quality assertions run on both PostgreSQL and Spark SQL tables). The challenge is not writing quality checks — it is governing them: ensuring every SLA-bound table has adequate checks, monitoring the checks themselves for false positives and stale thresholds, and providing a unified quality dashboard that the governance committee can review.
The Centralized Quality Registry

-- Quality rule registry: every assertion across both engines in one table
CREATE TABLE platform.quality_rules (
    rule_id         SERIAL       PRIMARY KEY,
    table_name      VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,  -- fully qualified name
    engine          VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,
    rule_name       VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    rule_type       VARCHAR(30)  NOT NULL,  -- 'completeness', 'freshness', 'validity', 'uniqueness'
    assertion_sql   TEXT         NOT NULL,  -- SQL that returns 0 rows on success, N rows on failure
    severity        VARCHAR(10)  NOT NULL CHECK (severity IN ('critical', 'warning', 'info')),
    owning_team     VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    sla_tier        VARCHAR(10)  NOT NULL,
    last_run        TIMESTAMPTZ,
    last_result     VARCHAR(10),  -- 'pass', 'fail', 'error'
    enabled         BOOLEAN      DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Quality dashboard: current status of all rules by team and tier
SELECT owning_team, sla_tier,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE last_result = 'pass')  AS passing,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE last_result = 'fail')  AS failing,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE last_result = 'error') AS errors,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE last_run < CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '2 hours') AS stale
FROM   platform.quality_rules
WHERE  enabled = TRUE
GROUP BY owning_team, sla_tier
ORDER BY sla_tier, failing DESC;

The quality registry stores every assertion across both engines in a single table. Each team registers their quality rules with their owning_team, and the centralized runner executes all rules on a schedule (every 15 minutes for platinum tables, hourly for gold, daily for silver). The dashboard query shows the current quality status per team per tier: passing rules, failing rules, errored rules (the assertion SQL itself failed — typically a schema change that broke the assertion), and stale rules (not run within the expected window — indicating a runner failure). At AlpineEnergy, the registry contains 420 quality rules across 180 tables. The platform team monitors the dashboard; each team monitors their own rules.
Anomaly Detection Across 300+ Tables

Static threshold rules (row count > 0, null fraction < 5%) catch deterministic failures. Anomaly detection catches statistical failures: a table that normally receives 4.8 million rows per day receives only 3.1 million (35% drop — no threshold was breached, but the deviation from the historical pattern is significant). At AlpineEnergy, anomaly detection computes the trailing 30-day average and standard deviation for each table's daily row count, daily_kw aggregates, and null fractions. A metric that deviates by more than 3 standard deviations triggers a warning; more than 5 standard deviations triggers a critical alert. The anomaly detection has caught 6 quality issues in 12 months that static thresholds missed — all caused by upstream source system changes that reduced data volume without triggering any individual assertion failure.
Quality Rule Governance

Quality rules themselves require governance: rules that are too sensitive produce false positives (crying wolf), rules that are too lenient miss real failures (false security), and rules that reference columns that have been renamed or removed produce errors (stale rules). At AlpineEnergy, the quarterly quality review evaluates three metrics per rule: the false positive rate (rules that triggered but the data was actually correct — indicating the threshold is too tight), the coverage gap (tables with SLA tiers but zero quality rules — indicating missing assertions), and the staleness rate (rules that have not run successfully in 7+ days — indicating broken assertions). The review produces action items: tighten thresholds on rules that never trigger, loosen thresholds on rules that trigger weekly with false positives, and add rules for coverage gaps.

blocks.title: Start with 3 rules per table, not 30
blocks.content: A table with 30 quality rules and 10% false positive rate triggers 3 false alerts per day — enough noise to make the team ignore all alerts. A table with 3 high-quality rules (freshness, completeness, and one domain-specific validity check) and 0% false positive rate triggers only genuine failures. At AlpineEnergy, new tables start with the 3 default rules. Additional rules are added only after a quality incident reveals a gap. This incident-driven rule growth produces a quality suite that reflects actual failure modes — not hypothetical ones.

Platforms grow by adding tables, pipelines, views, and materialized views. Without active deprecation, they also accumulate orphaned resources: tables that no pipeline writes to, views that no consumer queries, materialized views that refresh nightly but serve no dashboard, and pipelines that run successfully but whose output nobody uses. At AlpineEnergy, a resource audit 12 months after the Italian expansion discovered 23 orphaned tables (created during the migration and never cleaned up), 8 unused materialized views (their consumer dashboards had been decommissioned), and 3 pipelines that ran nightly producing data that no downstream consumer read. These orphans consumed approximately 340 GB of storage, 45 minutes of nightly compute, and 12 quality rule evaluations — waste that accumulated invisibly because no deprecation process existed.
The Deprecation Lifecycle

AlpineEnergy's deprecation policy defines a four-stage lifecycle. Stage 1 (Identify): the quarterly resource audit queries the dependency graph, the pipeline execution logs, and the pg_stat_user_tables access statistics to identify resources with zero consumers or zero recent access. Stage 2 (Announce): identified resources are marked as deprecated in the schema registry with a 90-day sunset date. The owning team and all registered consumers (if any exist) are notified. Stage 3 (Validate): during the 90-day window, the platform team monitors access to the deprecated resource. If any consumer accesses it, the deprecation is paused and the consumer is asked to register a contract or migrate to an alternative. Stage 4 (Sunset): after 90 days with zero access, the resource is dropped. For tables, the data is archived to cold storage (retained for 1 year for recovery). For pipelines, the DAG is disabled. For views, the view definition is archived in the schema registry's Git history.
Identifying Orphaned Resources

-- Find tables with no recent access (potential deprecation candidates)
-- Combines dependency registry (no registered consumers) with access stats
SELECT own.table_catalog || '.' || own.table_schema || '.' || own.table_name AS table_fqn,
       own.owning_team,
       own.sla_tier,
       s.last_seq_scan,
       s.last_idx_scan,
       GREATEST(s.last_seq_scan, s.last_idx_scan) AS last_access,
       CURRENT_DATE - GREATEST(s.last_seq_scan, s.last_idx_scan)::DATE AS days_since_access,
       (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM platform.contracts c
        WHERE c.producer_table = own.table_catalog || '.' || own.table_schema || '.' || own.table_name
       ) AS contract_count
FROM   platform.table_ownership own
  LEFT JOIN pg_stat_user_tables s
    ON own.table_name = s.relname AND own.table_schema = s.schemaname
WHERE  GREATEST(s.last_seq_scan, s.last_idx_scan) < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days'
   OR  s.last_seq_scan IS NULL
ORDER BY days_since_access DESC NULLS FIRST;

The query identifies tables that have not been accessed (via sequential scan or index scan) in 90 days and have zero registered consumer contracts. These are the strongest deprecation candidates. Tables with zero access but registered contracts require investigation — the contract may be stale (the consumer pipeline was decommissioned but the contract was not removed). Tables with recent access but zero contracts are the opposite problem: active consumers that have not registered. At AlpineEnergy, the quarterly audit produces a report with three categories: deprecation candidates (no access, no contracts), stale contract candidates (no access, contracts exist), and unregistered consumer candidates (recent access, no contracts). Each category has a different action path.
When NOT to Deprecate

Some tables have low access frequency by design: the quarterly regulatory filing table is accessed 4 times per year; the annual capacity planning archive is accessed once. A 90-day zero-access window for these tables is normal, not a deprecation signal. At AlpineEnergy, tables with a declared refresh_frequency of 'quarterly' or 'annual' in the data product manifest are excluded from the 90-day access threshold. Their deprecation threshold is 2x the declared refresh frequency: 180 days for quarterly tables, 730 days for annual tables. This frequency-aware threshold prevents the quarterly audit from repeatedly flagging low-frequency tables as deprecation candidates.

blocks.title: Deprecation is maintenance, not failure
blocks.content: Decommissioning a table that nobody uses is not a failure of the team that created it — it is evidence of a healthy platform lifecycle. Requirements change, consumers migrate to better alternatives, and exploration tables serve their purpose and become obsolete. The deprecation process treats resource removal as routine maintenance — not as a blame exercise. At AlpineEnergy, the quarterly deprecation report is presented alongside the quarterly growth report: 'we added 12 tables and removed 8, net growth of 4 tables.' The removal count is a health metric — a platform that only grows and never shrinks is accumulating technical debt.

sections.title: Team Structures for SQL Data Platforms

blocks.content: Every architectural decision in the senior track has an organizational counterpart. The dual-engine architecture (Module 1) requires engineers who understand both PostgreSQL and Spark SQL. The schema governance system (Module 5) requires a process owner who coordinates cross-team schema changes. The streaming platform (Module 9) requires on-call engineers with CDC and Kafka expertise. The team structure — how engineering talent is organized, how responsibilities are distributed, and how cross-team coordination occurs — determines whether the technical architecture succeeds in practice or collapses under organizational misalignment. This lesson covers the three team structures that AlpineEnergy evaluated and the hybrid model they adopted.
The Three Organizational Models

rows.left: One team owns all infrastructure, all pipelines, all governance
rows.right: Each domain team owns their pipelines, their tables, their quality

rows.left: Strong consistency: one coding style, one deployment process, one monitoring
rows.right: Domain autonomy: teams choose their tools, patterns, and schedules

rows.left: Bottleneck: central team becomes the chokepoint for all data requests
rows.right: Coordination cost: cross-domain changes require negotiation between autonomous teams

rows.left: Works at: 1-5 teams, < 50 pipelines, strong platform expertise
rows.right: Works at: 5-20 teams, 50-500 pipelines, strong domain expertise

blocks.content: AlpineEnergy's Hybrid Model

AlpineEnergy uses neither pure centralized nor pure federated — it uses a hybrid model with three organizational layers. The platform team (4 engineers, led by Lena) owns the shared infrastructure: PostgreSQL instances, Spark SQL cluster, Kafka, Debezium connectors, the schema registry, the quality framework, and the monitoring system. Country DE teams (Swiss, Austrian, Italian — 2 engineers each) own their country's Bronze-to-Silver pipelines and the country-specific Gold transformations. Cross-cutting teams (analytics, ML) own the Platinum tables and the consumer-facing outputs. The platform team provides the infrastructure and the governance; the domain teams provide the data and the transformations.

The hybrid model distributes ownership while centralizing infrastructure. The platform team does not build pipelines for other teams — it builds the infrastructure that makes pipelines buildable (Module 1's platform engineering shift). Each domain team is autonomous in their pipeline design, their table naming (within the namespace convention), and their deployment schedule. The governance mechanisms (schema registry, contract system, SLA framework) provide the coordination that prevents autonomy from becoming chaos. At AlpineEnergy, the hybrid model emerged through evolution: the original centralized platform team became a bottleneck at 5 teams and 80 pipelines, so the domain teams were created and the platform team transitioned to infrastructure ownership.
The On-Call Rotation

The on-call rotation reflects the organizational model. At AlpineEnergy, two on-call rotations exist: the platform on-call (responsible for infrastructure incidents — database, cluster, CDC, monitoring) and the data on-call (responsible for pipeline incidents — failed pipelines, stale data, quality breaches). The platform on-call is staffed by the 4-person platform team on a weekly rotation. The data on-call rotates across all DE teams: each country team covers one week per month, and the Swiss team covers the remaining week. Platinum-tier incidents page the platform on-call first (infrastructure is the most common root cause for platinum failures). Gold-tier incidents page the data on-call first (pipeline logic is the most common root cause for gold failures).

blocks.title: Team structure follows architecture — not the reverse
blocks.content: Conway's Law states that organizations produce systems that mirror their communication structure. The reverse is equally true: the architecture constrains the effective team structures. AlpineEnergy's dual-engine architecture requires a platform team that spans both engines. The country-level isolation requires country-level DE teams. The cross-cutting Platinum layer requires cross-cutting analytics teams. Reorganizing the teams without changing the architecture (or vice versa) creates organizational friction — teams responsible for components they do not control, or components without a responsible team.

Disaster recovery protects against regional infrastructure failure: an Azure region outage, a data center fire, or a catastrophic storage failure. Business continuity ensures that the most critical data products (billing, regulatory filings, grid operations) remain available during and after the disaster. The two are related but distinct: disaster recovery is the technical mechanism (replication, backups, failover), and business continuity is the operational plan (which products are restored first, how quickly, and to what level of service). At AlpineEnergy, the DR strategy is tiered: platinum tables are replicated to a secondary region with automatic failover; gold tables are backed up daily with 4-hour recovery time; silver tables are backed up daily with 24-hour recovery time.
PostgreSQL DR Architecture

AlpineEnergy's PostgreSQL DR uses streaming replication (Module 6, lesson 3) to maintain a hot standby in a secondary Azure region. The standby receives WAL continuously and is typically < 1 second behind the primary. Failover (promoting the standby to primary) takes approximately 30 seconds using pg_promote(). After failover, the application connection strings must be updated — at AlpineEnergy, PgBouncer's configuration is updated via a DNS failover that points the connection endpoint to the new primary. The RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is < 1 second (the WAL replication lag). The RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is < 5 minutes (failover + DNS propagation + PgBouncer reconnection). These targets apply only to the Swiss and Austrian instances; the Italian instance does not have a hot standby (cost decision — the Italian workload is lower priority, and the backup-based recovery with 4-hour RTO is acceptable).
Delta Lake DR Architecture

Delta Lake tables on ADLS benefit from Azure's built-in geo-redundant storage (GRS): every blob is replicated to a paired region automatically. The DR challenge for Delta Lake is not data replication (handled by GRS) but compute recovery: the Spark SQL cluster in the primary region is unavailable, so a secondary cluster must be provisioned in the paired region. At AlpineEnergy, the secondary Spark SQL cluster is pre-provisioned in a warm standby configuration: the cluster infrastructure exists but executors are scaled to zero (no compute cost). On failover, the executors scale up (3-5 minutes) and the streaming jobs restart from their checkpoints (which are stored in GRS-replicated storage). The Delta Lake tables are immediately accessible because GRS provides read access to the replicated data within seconds of a region failover.
Recovery Priority

rows: Hot standby with streaming replication; automatic failover
Kafka topic retention (7 days); Spark checkpoint restart
GRS replication; warm standby Spark cluster scale-up
Daily pg_dump backup; restore to new instance
Reprocessable from source; no separate backup needed

blocks.content: The recovery priority reflects the business impact of downtime: billing (P1) generates revenue, CDC (P2) feeds the grid operations dashboard that prevents equipment damage, Gold/Platinum (P3) serves executive reporting and regulatory filings, the Italian instance (P4) serves a smaller user base, and Bronze/Silver (P5) can be reprocessed from source data without backup. The priority ordering determines which components the on-call engineer restores first during a multi-component failure. At AlpineEnergy, the DR plan is tested semi-annually: the platform team simulates a regional failover in staging, executes the recovery procedures, and measures the actual RPO and RTO against the targets. Two of the four semi-annual tests have identified recovery procedure gaps that were fixed before a production incident required them.

blocks.title: An untested DR plan is not a plan — it is documentation
blocks.content: The most dangerous DR assumption is that the recovery procedure works. At AlpineEnergy, the first semi-annual DR test revealed that the PostgreSQL failover took 12 minutes instead of 5 — because the DNS propagation was slower than expected and PgBouncer's reconnection logic had a bug that required a manual restart. The second test revealed that the Spark streaming checkpoint was stored in the primary region's non-GRS storage — making it unavailable during failover. Both issues were invisible until the test exposed them. The semi-annual DR test is the most valuable operational exercise AlpineEnergy runs.

This is the final lesson of the SQL for Data Engineers Senior-Level Track. The nine preceding modules built the components of a multi-engine, multi-team data platform: the organizational architecture (Module 1), the optimizer expertise (Module 2), the partition governance (Module 3), the join architecture (Module 4), the schema governance (Module 5), the engine internals (Module 6), the storage architecture (Module 7), the security posture (Module 8), and the streaming platform (Module 9). This module added the governance and operational frameworks: lineage, impact analysis, capacity planning, SLA governance, incident management, data quality, deprecation, team structures, and disaster recovery. This final lesson synthesizes everything into AlpineEnergy's enterprise governance framework — the system of systems that sustains the platform.
The Governance Framework Summary

rows: Plan stability tests, optimizer diagnostics, cost parameter tuning
Partition design reviews, maintenance automation, partition health audit
Topology classification, join architecture audit, materialized view policy
Schema registry, contracts, CI hooks, drift detection
MVCC monitoring, WAL tracking, wraparound prevention, lock detection
Format governance, compression policy, OPTIMIZE scheduling, encryption
RLS policies, masking views, audit logging, residency enforcement
Continuous CI testing + quarterly security assessment
CDC monitoring, streaming SLA, exactly-once verification, DR procedures
Lineage, SLA governance, incident management, capacity planning

blocks.content: The Platform Maturity Assessment

The governance framework was not built all at once — it accumulated over 18 months as the platform grew. The order of adoption followed the pain: ownership registry first (when the third team could not find tables), schema governance next (when the first cross-team breaking change caused an incident), SLA governance next (when competing team requirements exceeded the cluster capacity), and lineage last (when the first multi-team incident required 45 minutes of manual dependency tracing). Each governance mechanism was adopted in response to a measured problem — not preemptively. A platform that is smaller or simpler does not need all ten governance domains. The maturity model from Module 5 (lesson 10) applies to the entire framework: adopt each domain when the scale or incident rate justifies it.
The Senior Track Complete

The SQL for Data Engineers Senior-Level Track taught you to design and govern a multi-engine, multi-team data platform — the kind of system that other engineers build on. The shift from mid-level to senior is not more complex SQL or faster queries — it is the transition from building pipelines to designing the platform that makes pipelines buildable, governable, and sustainable. The platform architecture (Module 1) defines the structure. The optimizer and partition expertise (Modules 2-3) ensures the structure performs. The join and schema governance (Modules 4-5) ensures the structure extends safely. The engine and storage internals (Modules 6-7) enables diagnosis beyond the surface. The security, streaming, and governance frameworks (Modules 8-10) ensures the structure survives years of growth, regulatory change, and team evolution.

blocks.title: The platform outlives the architect
blocks.content: The senior engineer who designs the platform today will not be the engineer who operates it in 3 years. Teams change, technologies evolve, and business requirements shift. The governance framework — the ADRs, the ownership registry, the contract system, the lineage graph, the SLA governance, the incident playbooks, the capacity plan — is the institutional memory that enables the next generation of engineers to understand, operate, and evolve the platform without the original architect's presence. The best platform architecture is one that is comprehensible to someone who was not in the room when the decisions were made.
